There were no statistically significant differences in any of the biochemical parameters between the groups. DISCUSSION The patients studied were admitted to the ICU with acute cerebrovascular disease. We excluded patients with intestinal pathologies and infectious diseases, such as sepsis, as these comorbidities and the therapies used to treat them could affect intestinal function. The main outcome of the study was that of preferred nutrition feed for gastrointestinal function. We showed that fibre-free nutrition generated higher gastrointestinal complications, especially diarrhoea, than fibre-enriched nutrition. To demonstrate the consequences of the gastrointestinal complications like diarrhoea, volume ratio was used; it provided a measure of feed efficiency. The main result of this study was that fibre-enriched nutrition resulted in less gastrointestinal complications and higher VR values, which was predictive of more effective patient nutrition. Montejo et al7 found that the most frequent complication in enteral nutrition was high gastric residual volume. This is different from the results of our study and may be due to the use of a different nutrition solution or different GRV measurement methods. At present, a clear consensus regarding the measurement method and the limits of gastric residual volume has not been reached. In our clinical practice, enteral nutrition is started at a rate of 20 mL/h and is increased gradually. If, the gradual increment is not interrupted on account of intolerance and the patient receives the prescribed feed volume, daily GRV measurement is sufficient to anticipate upper gastrointes- tinal tract tolerance. When this was not the case, during the first days of enteral nutrition, we measured GRV frequently (three or four times a day). Additionally, the routine metoclopramide administration in our study may have been another factor that caused the low gastric residual volumes that we observed. Chang et al8 reported that drugs (e.g. antibiotics), infections, illness severity and enteral feedings are the causes of diarrhoea in the ICU. Whelan et al9 reported that the frequency of diarrhoea in enteral-fed patients ranged from 2 to 95% and that this large variation was due to different definitions of diarrhoea and different measurement methods. In the present study, the most frequent complication was diarrhoea, and it occurred in 50% of patients. Enteral nutrition is a contributing factor to ICU diarrhoea because it alters gut physiology. Whelan et al10 hypothesized that enteral feeding changes both transit time, secretory mechanisms and the microbiota in the gastrointestinal tract. Luft et al11 showed the importance of enteral feeding solution and feeding equipment sterility by decreasing the frequency of diarrhoea using hygiene protocols. The properties of nutrition solutions that have been examined for their diarrhoeal effects are temperature, osmolality, fat content and caloric density, but strict guidelines for these properties of nutrition solutions have not been made.12 One of the most controversial components of feeding solutions is fibre. Dietary fibre has been shown to have various effects by multiple studies. Krusawa et al13 and Cummings JH14 showed that dietary fibre increases stool weight allowing for easier defecation. Salmeron et al15 reported glucose regulating effects of dietary fibre. Fibres that promote beneficial bacterial growth, such as that of lactobacillus and bifidobacteria, are called prebiotics. Prebiotics improve gut barrier function and host immunity and reduce the overgrowth of pathogenic bacteria, such as clostridia.16 Inulin and FOS are the main prebiotics in our study solution. As examples of the immunological support provided by prebiotics, it has been shown that FOS consumption increases T-lymphocytes in adults, increases antibody response to vaccines in infants and reduces antibiotic consumption.17-19 Elia et al20 reported a systemic review and meta-analysis that proved that fibreenriched feeding formulas reduce diarrhoea incidence. Chittawatanarat et al21 showed that mixed fibre formulas, like our formula, in particular, can reduce diarrhoea in septic ICU patients receiving broad spectrum antibiotics. Soluble fibres (e.g. pectin, FOS, inulin, guar gum) have 744 H Yagmurdur and F Leblebici antidiarrhoeal mechanisms. Namely, after soluble fibre fermentation by colonic anaerobic bacteria, short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) are produced. SCFAs are beneficial for colonocytes and stimulate water uptake.21,22 Majid et al23 reported that fibres reduced diarrhoea incidence and acted as a type of prebiotic. Rushdi et al24 performed a study with only one type of fibre, guar gum, and still found that fibre-enriched nutrition reduced diarrhoeal episodes. The required amount of fibre needed to decrease the incidence of diarrhoea has not yet been determined. In a meta-analysis, Elia et al20 reported that the beneficial mean fibre intake amount is approximately 30 g/day in most studies. In the present study, the fibreenriched group mean fibre intake was approximately 28 g per day, which is in agreement with the results of the meta-analysis. Controversially, there have been studies that did not show beneficial effects of fibres. Hart et al6 and Dobb et al25 reported that fibre-enriched nutrition did not reduce diarrhoea frequency. These conflicting results may be due to differences in the types of fibres used in those studies. Jack et al26 reported that the consequences of diarrhoea are electrolyte imbalance, dehydration, perianal skin breakdown and wound contamination. In the present study, low risk ICU patients were examined in respect to gastrointestinal complications; thus, we did not observe these consequences of diarrhoea in our short study period. In our opinion, one of the most important consequences of diarrhoea is the compulsory cessation of enteral nutrition. Majid et al thought that diarrhoea may contribute to malnutrition in ICU patients.27 In the present study, the two groups were similar in regards to the biochemical measurements at the beginning of the study, and no differences were found at the end of the study. Rushdi et al24 reported higher calcium and magnesium levels in a fibre-enriched group compared with a control group, but did not find a difference in albumin level. These variable results between studies may be due to the different fibres used or to the small sample sizes of those studies. For diarrhoea management in enteral-fed patients, various protocols have been used. Decreasing the nutrition infusion volume and nutrition interruption are the basics of diarrhoea management, and changing the feeding formula is another technique for diarrhoea management.7 In these circumstances, energy intake and metabolic requirements (glucose, electrolytes and protein) must be replaced with intravenous solutions. Unnecessary intravenous access for parenteral replacement is a cause of ICU infections. Protein-energy malnutrition causes many complications in ICU patients, and mortality increases with these complications, especially infectious complications and immunosuppression.2-5 In conclusion, gastrointestinal complications have various harmful consequences in ICU patients. Diarrhoea is one of the most frequent complications in tube-fed patients and is a complicating factor for enteral nutrition management. On the basis of the present study, we suggest that ICU staff initiate enteral nutrition with fibreenriched formulas rather than fibre-free formulas to avoid frequent feeding interruptions which cause protein-energy malnutrition in ICU patients. title: Influence of synbiotic containing Lactobacillus acidophilus La5 , Bifidobacterium lactis Bb 12 , Streptococcus thermophilus, Lactobacillus bulgaricus and oligofructose on gut barrier function and sepsis in critically ill patients: a randomised controlled trial
abstract: Background & aims: Infective complications are a common cause of mortality and morbidity in critically ill patients. Many factors affect sepsis, one of which is gut barrier function. The aim of this study was to determine whether the oral administration of a synbiotic preparation could alter gut barrier function in critically ill patients and thus reduce sepsis. Methods: A total of 90 patients admitted to an intensive care unit (ICU) were randomised to receive either synbiotic or placebo preparations (45 into each group). The synbiotic preparation consisted of Lactobacillus acidophilus La5, Bifidobacterium lactis Bb 12, Streptococcus thermophilus and Lactobacillus bulgaricus (probiotics) with oligofructose (prebiotic). Gut barrier function was assessed by measurement of intestinal permeability (lactulose/rhamnose test) and culture of nasogastric aspirate on days 1 and 8. All septic complications and mortality were recorded. Results: There were no differences between the groups in terms of age, sex, APACHE II or POSSUM scores. After 1 week of therapy, patients in the synbiotic group had a significantly lower incidence of potentially pathogenic bacteria (43% versus 75%, P = 0.05) and multiple organisms (39% versus 75%, P = 0.01) in their nasogastric aspirates than controls. There were no significant differences between the groups in terms of intestinal permeability, septic complications or mortality. Conclusions: The administration of synbiotic in critically ill patients favourably altered the microbial composition of the upper gastrointestinal tract but had no effect on intestinal permeability and was not associated with measurable clinical benefit.

Gut barrier function was assessed by measurement of intestinal permeability (lactulose/rhamnose test) and culture of nasogastric aspirate on days 1 and 8. All septic complications and mortality were recorded. Results: There were no differences between the groups in terms of age, sex, APACHE II or POSSUM scores. After 1 week of therapy, patients in the synbiotic group had a significantly lower incidence of potentially pathogenic bacteria (43% versus 75%, P ¼ 0.05) and multiple organisms (39% versus 75%, P ¼ 0.01) in their nasogastric aspirates than controls. There were no significant differences between the groups in terms of intestinal permeability, septic complications or mortality. Introduction In health, the indigenous flora of the human gastrointestinal tract is a stable and tightly regulated ecosystem, comprising of over four hundred bacterial species.1 Some of the resident bacteria are potential pathogens and may be a source of sepsis when the gut barrier is functionally or physically breached. This may occur in those patients who are immunocompromised, in states of increased intestinal permeability or when the spectrum of normal gastrointestinal microflora is altered.2–4 Epidemiological studies have demonstrated that the majority of nosocomial infections in critically ill patients are caused by gram negative organisms.5,6 The most obvious source of these pathogens is the gastrointestinal tract. The stomach and proximal small bowel become rapidly colonised by bacteria in ICU patients.4,7 There is good evidence that colonisation of the upper gastrointestinal tract promotes nosocomial infection, presumably either through the aspiration of gastric contents or secondary to bacterial translocation.3,8,9 Given these findings, it is reasonable to hypothesise that favourable alteration of upper gastrointestinal flora might reduce septic morbidity in the critically ill. One method of modulating gastrointestinal flora in humans is through the administration of pre- and probiotics. A probiotic can be defined as a preparation containing an appropriate number of viable, defined micro-organisms, which alter host microflora and exert beneficial health effects. Probiotic organisms are thought to act through a variety of mechanisms including the competitive exclusion of potential pathogens from enterocyte adhesion sites, degradation of toxins, production of antimicrobial factors and immunomodulation. Prebiotics are non-digestible food supplements which selectively promote the growth of colonic bacteria in favour of a ‘‘healthier’’ composition. When a pre- and probiotic are used simultaneously, the combination is termed a synbiotic.10 The mixture of probiotic strains used in this study has not been used before in any published clinical trial. The aim of this double blind, prospective and randomised trial was to investigate the effects of the enteral administration of a synbiotic preparation contain- ing oligofructose and Lactobacillus acidophilus La5, Bifidobacterium lactis Bb-12, Streptococcus thermophilus and L. bulgaricus on upper gastrointestinal colonisation, intestinal permeability and systemic inflammation. Material and methods Patients Patients admitted to an intensive care and high dependency unit were eligible for the study. Exclusion criteria were; age o18 years, pregnancy or lactation, and contraindication to enteral administration of medication. Patients who had been admitted more than 24 h before samples for the study could be collected were excluded as the microbiology of gastric aspirates changes rapidly following ICU admission. Informed consent was obtained from patients on entry into the study whenever possible. If not, the study was discussed with the next of kin. All survivors were informed of their participation in this study and approval obtained for inclusion of their data. The study was approved by the Scarborough Local Research Ethics Committee. Study medication The synbiotic preparation consisted of a combination of the probiotic TrevisTM (Chr Hansen Biosystem, Denmark), in dose of one capsule three times a day and the prebiotic oligofructose (7.5 gm of RaftiloseTM powder, Orafti Active Food Ingredients, Belgium) administered twice a day. TrevisTM capsules contain 4  109 colony forming units of L. acidophilus La5 (La5), B. lactis Bb-12 (Bb-12), S. thermophilus and L. bulgaricus. The placebopreparation consisted of identical doses of powdered sucrose and placebo-capsules (Chr Hansen Biosystem, Denmark). Study design This study was a single centre double blind, placebocontrolled trial. Randomisation to treatment or ARTICLE IN PRESS Synbiotics in the Critically iII control groups was achieved by means of a random number sequence and sealed envelopes. The pharmacist opened each envelope and dispensed the appropriate medication. Active and placebopreparations were dispensed in identical packaging. The medication was administered orally or through a nasogastric tube by ICU nursing staff. The investigators, nurses, attending physicians and patients were therefore unaware which preparation each patient received. All patients in the study received concomitant therapy with antibiotics, inotropes and enteral or parenteral nutrition (without glutamine) as considered appropriate by the attending clinician. Proton pump inhibitors or histamine (H2) antagonists were not used routinely. The study medication was continued until discharge from hospital. Outcome measures Gastric colonisation The primary outcome measure was the incidence and nature of gastric colonisation. This was defined as the presence of bacteria or fungi in a nasogastric (NG) aspirate. A 5 ml aspirate was obtained in a sterile syringe after discarding the first 10 ml of fluid (aspirated by separate syringe) and transported to the laboratory in a sealed sterile container for culture. This technique has been shown to yield reliable and reproducible results, comparable with those obtained by direct sampling of gastric contents at laparotomy.11 An NG aspirate was collected on the day of admission to ICU (day 1), before commencing synbiotics. Further samples were collected on days 8 and 15 if the patient still had an NG tube in situ. The aspirates were inoculated onto blood agar and cysteinelactose-electrolyte deficient (CLED) medium for aerobic culture and blood and neomycin blood containing media for anaerobic culture. All cultures were maintained at 371C for 48 h. Isolates were identified using standard microbiological techniques.12 All Gram-negative bacteria, Pseudomonas species and Staphylococcus aureus were considered as potentially pathogenic organisms. Intestinal permeability Small intestinal permeability was measured on days 1 and 8 using the lactulose/rhamnose dual sugar probe technique. Patients were administered 5 g of lactulose and 1 g rhamnose after an overnight fast. Urine was collected for 5 h and the concentration of urinary sugars assayed using HPLC.13 A lactulose/ rhamnose ratio (L/R ratio) of greater than 0.05 in a 469 5-h urine collection was indicative of increased intestinal permeability. Systemic inflammatory response To quantify the systemic inflammatory response, serum levels of C-reactive protein (CRP) were measured on days 1, 8 and 15 using an auto analyser (Synchron CX system, Beckman Coulter Inc., USA). The upper limit of normal in our laboratory was 10 mg/l. Septic morbidity and mortality Septic complications were defined as the presence of recognised pathogens in tissue which is normally sterile, confirmed by the results of culture and supported by clinical, haematological or radiological evidence. Any infection present at the time of entry to the study was excluded from analysis. All septic events that occurred between the time of entry into the study and death or discharge from hospital were recorded. Severity of illness was assessed for every patient at the time of entry into the study using the Acute Physiological and Chronic Health Evaluation (APACHE II) score.14 The Physiological and Operative Severity Score for enUmeration of Morbidity (POSSUM) was calculated for those patients who had undergone surgery.15 Statistical analysis A sample-size calculation based on published figures demonstrated that approximately 22 patients would be required in each group to demonstrate a reduction in gastric colonisation by pathogenic organisms from 70% to 30% at the 5% significance level with a power of 80%.4,8 Patients were enrolled into the study until day 8 nasogastric samples had been obtained from this number of patients. Analysis of results was performed on the basis of ‘‘intention to treat.’’ Data was stored on a Microsoft Excel spreadsheet and statistical analysis was performed using SPSS for Windows Version 10.0 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, USA). Qualitative data were compared using the Pearson w2 test or Fisher’s exact test for small samples. Quantitative data were expressed as medians with interquartile range (IQR) or means with standard error of the mean (SEM). Differences between means were evaluated using analysis of variance or paired t-tests as appropriate. The Mann–Whitney test was used to compare non-parametric data. A P-value of 0.05 or less was taken to signify statistical significance. ARTICLE IN PRESS 470 Table 1 P.K. Jain et al. Baseline patient characteristics. Results Patients A total of 90 patients were recruited into the study (45 in each group). There were no statistically significant differences between the groups in terms of age, sex, POSSUM or APACHE II scores. The use of antibiotics was similar in both groups (Table 1). Synbiotic intake The median duration of intake of study medication was 10 days in both groups. Eighteen patients received medication for less than 1 week. The most common reasons for reduced intake were early death (n ¼ 12), high nasogastric aspirates (n ¼ 2), transfer to another hospital (n ¼ 2). reasons. Candida was the most frequently cultured organism in both groups. The most common potentially pathogenic organisms isolated from nasogastric aspirates were Escherichia coli (12%) and Enterococcus faecalis (9.6%). The mean values of NG aspirate pH are shown in Table 3. There was no significant difference in NG pH between the two groups on day 1 (P ¼ 0.334, Mann–Whitney) and day 8 (P ¼ 0.165, Mann–Whitney). There were no significant differences in the total number of positive isolates in synbiotic and control groups at any time point. By day eight, however, only 9 (39%) patients in the synbiotic group were colonised with multiple organisms compared to 18 (75%) patients in the control group (P ¼ 0.01, Fisher’s exact test), and 10 (43%) patients in the synbiotic group grew potentially pathogenic organisms compared to 18 (75%) patients in the control group (P ¼ 0.05, Fisher’s exact test) (Table 3). An adequate NG sample was obtained in 75% (34/45) patients in the control group and 84% (38/45) patients in the synbiotic group on day 1. Septic complications Fifty-nine patients developed one or more septic complication during the study; 33/45 (73%) in the synbiotic group and 26/45 (58%) in the control group (Table 4). This did not represent a statistically significant difference (w2 2.41, P ¼ 0.12). An identical organism was isolated from the septic focus and day 8 nasogastric aspirate in 7/ 23 (30.4%) patients in the synbiotic group and 8/24 (33.3%) patients in the control group. Septic complications occurred in 25/27 (92.5%) patients who had growth of potentially pathogenic organisms from their day 8 NG aspirate, compared with 11/20 (55%) patients without growth of potentially pathogenic organisms (P ¼ 0:007; Fisher’s exact test). Mortality and hospital stay The mortality rate was similar in both groups; 49% (22/45) in the synbiotic group and 45% (20/45) in the control group (w2 0.17, P ¼ 0.672). A total of 14 (15%) patients died within a week of entry into the study. The median duration of ICU stay in those patients who survived was 4 (IQR 3-13) days in the synbiotic group and 4 (3–6) days in the control group. The median total hospital stay was 15 (IQR 9–26) days and 14 (9–29) days in the synbiotic and control groups respectively (P ¼ 0.913, Mann–Whitney). Discussion The results of this randomised, controlled trial suggest that the upper gastrointestinal flora of ARTICLE IN PRESS Synbiotics in the Critically iII critically ill patients can be modulated through the enteral administration of a synbiotic preparation containing oligofructose, L. acidophilus La 5, B. lactis Bb-12, S. thermophilus and L. bulgaricus. Gastric colonisation with multiple organisms and potentially pathogenic bacteria was significantly reduced after 1 week of therapy, as compared to controls. Synbiotic administration had no effect on intestinal permeability, the systemic inflammatory response, septic morbidity, mortality or length of hospital stay. Although the pathogenesis of sepsis and multiple organ failure in critical illness remains obscure, it is generally agreed that the gut plays a pivotal role. The precise mechanism is unclear, but there is evidence to suggest that changes in GI microflora associated with the use of antibiotics, immunosuppression or changes in intestinal permeability may disrupt the normal ecological balance and alter gut barrier function. This may predispose to translocation of bacteria or endotoxin, which may in turn result in systemic inflammation and septic morbidity.2–4 Strategies intended to protect the gut during critical illness have been predominately aimed at the maintenance of adequate splanchnic perfusion and prevention of mucosal ischaemia/reperfusion injury.16 Another approach has been the use of nutritional therapies directed towards promoting enterocyte function, an example of which is glutamine.17 One other potential target is the gastrointestinal microflora. Commensal bacteria contribute substantially to the gut mucosal barrier through diverse means, including colonisation resistance, immunomodulation and mucus production.18,19 In critically ill patients, the balance of the gut microecology is altered in such a way that the number of potentially pathogenic bacteria increases and the healthy interaction between the host and microbe is disturbed. There is evidence to suggest that preand probiotics help to stabilise the gut microbial environment, enhance systemic and local immune responses, and possibly promote gut mucosal barrier function.20,21 Use of pre and probiotics has been of therapeutic value in clinical conditions associated with gut barrier dysfunction and inflammatory response.22–24 Animal and in vitro studies have shown that the use of probiotics can reduce the rate of bacterial translocation.25,26 The use of pre- and probiotics in clinical conditions which commonly result in critical illness, for example acute pancreatitis,27 abdominal surgery28 and organ transplantation 29 has been associated with therapeutic benefit. This is the first study to investigate the effect of combination of pre- and probiotics (synbiotic) on 473 sepsis and indices of gut barrier function in critically ill patients. The ‘‘gold-standard’’ method for identifying compromise of the gut barrier is the identification of enteric organisms in mesenteric lymph nodes. The harvest of such nodes at laparotomy is a well-established technique, but was not practical in this study as very few patients underwent surgery after therapy with synbiotics. We have previously demonstrated that gastric colonisation is a surrogate marker of bacterial translocation and is associated with an increased incidence of post-operative septic morbidity.30 This parameter was therefore adopted as the primary end point for this study. Our results confirm that colonisation of the proximal gastrointestinal tract is common in ICU patients, almost 90% of patients having positive gastric cultures at the time of recruitment. Synbiotics significantly altered the microbial composition of nasogastric aspirates, reducing the carriage of pathogenic bacteria and multiple organisms. We observed a significantly higher rate of septic complications in patients who had growth of potentially pathogenic bacteria in NG aspirates. Marshall9 reported a similar increase in nosocomial infection in critically ill patients with pathological gastric colonisation. The exact mechanism of action of synbiotics in altering gastric microflora is not clear. Both local and remote actions of probiotic organisms have been suggested. Some studies have shown a reduction in potentially pathogenic organisms even at sites distant to the gut (vagina and nasal cavity) following oral probiotic therapy.31,32 One proposed mechanism is that probiotics initiate an immune response in the gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT), resulting in circulation of primed T and B cells and immune activation at distant mucosal surfaces.33,34 Intestinal permeability was abnormal in over 86% of patients at the time of entrance into the study. This is in keeping with previously published reports of permeability in critically ill subjects.35 Synbiotic administration had no effect on intestinal permeability, as measured by a dual-sugar probe technique. Although probiotic bacteria have previously been shown to modify permeability in animal models, the evidence in humans is sparse.36,37 The effect of synbiotics on other measures of intestinal permeability, such as Cr-EDTA, was not assessed in this study. To date, there is little data in the literature to suggest that states of increased intestinal permeability promote bacterial translocation in humans.38 There are a number of possible reasons for the failure of synbiotics to demonstrate clinical benefit ARTICLE IN PRESS 474 in this study. It is well recognised that the use of septic morbidity, mortality or duration of hospital stay as primary end points necessitates huge numbers of patients to demonstrate significant differences with single therapeutic interventions. A sample size of many hundreds of patients would be required to detect a 5% reduction in the incidence of nosocomial infection. We accept that there may be further potential criticisms of this study. The most important of these relates to the nature, dose and duration of probiotic therapy. The ability of probiotic organisms to adhere to, colonise, and modulate the human gastrointestinal tract varies from species to species. The selection of TrevisTM capsules was based on a review of the literature but it is possible that other synbiotic preparations may be superior. The dosage used was as recommended by the manufacturers and the duration was determined pragmatically by duration of hospital stay. Further studies investigating more prolonged periods of administration are required. Another potential drawback of studies of this type relates to patient compliance and demonstration of efficacy of the preparation used. Compliance problems were minimised by ensuring that all study medications were administered by nursing staff as prescribed drugs. Efficacy of the trial preparation was confirmed by culture of TrevisTM capsules at regular intervals in order to confirm the presence of sufficient numbers of live probiotic organisms. Ideally, compliance would be confirmed by detection of probiotic organisms in the faeces. In practice this is extremely difficult, not simply because of logistical problems with culturing faeces, but also because DNA probes are necessary in order to confirm the presence of probiotic organisms.39 In conclusion, the results of this prospective and randomised trial suggest that the enteral administration of a synbiotic preparation to critically ill patients can significantly alter the microbial composition of the upper gastrointestinal tract. The clinical significance of this finding remains uncertain, and further studies using different preparations for more prolonged periods are required. The high incidence of enteric organisms isolated synchronously from nasogastric aspirates and septic foci in this study provides further circumstantial evidence in support of the gut-origin of sepsis hypothesis. title: Intestinal Obstruction Secondary to Enteral Feedings in Burn Trauma Patients
abstract: Background: Enteral feeding is preferred for maintaining gut integrity and providing nutrition in trauma patients. Recent reports suggest that use of early enteral feeds is successful and that complications are rare. A recent burn patient, who suffered apparent bowel obstruction and perforation secondary to enteral feedings, led us to review our experience with mechanical complications of tube feedings. Methods: We searched our registry of patients treated for acute burn trauma injury and identified patients treated for acute bowel obstruction in the past 3 years. Results: Four patients were identified, ages 22 to 44, with burns of 6 to 92% total body surface area. Each required intubation and ventilatory support during initial treatment, complicated by adult respiratory distress syndrome and sepsis. We began enteral feeds 1 to 3 days after admission. At approximately 14 days after admission, each patient deteriorated clinically, which led to emergent abdominal exploration; the tube feedings caused bowel obstruction and associated complications. Each patient improved with laparotomy. Conclusion: Bowel obstruction, ischemic necrosis, or both, secondary to early and aggressive nutrition with a fiber supplemented enteral feeding is an uncommon, life-threatening complication. Understanding and early recognition of this potential complication are essential to prevention or successful treatment.

Publication Type: [Articles] ISSN: 0022-5282 Accession: 00005373-199911000-00007 Keywords: Enteral nutrition, Complications, Obstruction, Tube feeding, Trauma [Articles] Intestinal Obstruction Secondary to Enteral Feedings in Burn Trauma Patients Scaife, Courtney L. MD; Saffle, Jeffrey R. MD; Morris, Stephen E. MD Author Information From the Department of Surgery and the Intermountain Burn Center, University of Utah Hospitals and Clinics, Salt Lake City, Utah. Address for reprints: Jeffrey R. Saffle, MD, Department of Surgery, 3B-306, University of Utah Hospitals and Clinics, 50 N. Medical Drive, Salt Lake City, UT 84132; email: jsaffle@msscc.med.utah.edu. Presented at the 29th Annual Meeting of the Western Trauma Association, February 28-March 6, 1999, Crested Butte, Colorado. Abstract Background: Enteral feeding is preferred for maintaining gut integrity and providing nutrition in trauma patients. Recent reports suggest that use of early enteral feeds is successful and that complications are rare. A recent burn patient, who suffered apparent bowel obstruction and perforation secondary to enteral feedings, led us to review our experience with mechanical complications of tube feedings. Methods: We searched our registry of patients treated for acute burn trauma injury and identified patients treated for acute bowel obstruction in the past 3 years. Results: Four patients were identified, ages 22 to 44, with burns of 6 to 92% total body surface area. Each required intubation and ventilatory support during initial treatment, complicated by adult respiratory distress syndrome and sepsis. We began enteral feeds 1 to 3 days after admission. At approximately 14 days after admission, each patient deteriorated clinically, which led to emergent abdominal exploration; the tube feedings caused bowel obstruction and associated complications. Each patient improved with laparotomy. Conclusion: Bowel obstruction, ischemic necrosis, or both, secondary to early and aggressive nutrition with a fiber supplemented enteral feeding is an uncommon, life-threatening complication. Understanding and early recognition of this potential complication are essential to prevention or successful treatment. Enteral nutrition has been shown to be superior to parenteral nutrition for maintaining gut integrity and providing nutrition in trauma patients for several reasons. 1-3 Enteral feeding immediately after injury (24-48 hours after resuscitation) may improve both early nitrogen balance and gut barrier and immune function, decrease infectious complications, and have cost benefits compared with parenteral nutrition. 3-9 A number of studies investigating early enteral feedings have documented few associated complications. Of the reported complications, diarrhea and tracheal aspiration are the two most common. 4,5,8,10-12 In light of these findings, use of early enteral feeds is widely promoted in the trauma literature, leading to a widespread perception that such feedings can be given early and aggressively, with little concern for complications. Our experience with early enteral feeding in trauma patients suggests that this technique can be associated with major complications. Experience with a recent patient, who suffered bowel ischemia, obstruction, and perforation secondary to enteral feedings in our burn center, prompted us to review our experience with early enteral feeding. We present four cases in which the enteral feedings were associated with bowel obstruction and perforation as well as a literature review of complications of enteral feedings. The purpose of this review was to identify factors that led to ischemia, obstruction, or both, after institution of enteral nutrition in burn patients. We hypothesized that clinical shock, sepsis, and the use of fiber-containing feedings would make additive contributions to the development of this catastrophic complication. MATERIALS AND METHODS We searched our computerized registry of all patients admitted to our burn center for the treatment of acute burns and soft-tissue injuries, during the period of 1995 to 1998, inclusive, to identify patients who developed complications of intestinal obstruction, perforation, or other acute abdominal conditions. There were 1,504 patients admitted during this period, of whom 237 patients (15.8%) required enteral nutritional support. Four patients were identified that required surgery for acute bowel obstruction or perforation. These four patients make up 0.2% of the total group admitted during this time period and 1.7% of patients given enteral support. Case 1 A healthy 39-year-old man suffered a 92% total body surface area (TBSA) burn with inhalation injury as a result of a propane flash. The patient was immediately transferred to the burn trauma intensive care unit and appropriate resuscitation was initiated. After 24 hours, a nasojejunal feeding tube was placed under fluoroscopic guidance (see Table 1 for a summary of the cases). Table 1. Summary of cases On posttrauma day (PTD) 2, we began jejunal feeds by using Replete with fiber starting at 20 mL/h and increasing gradually to the patient's calculated goal rate, based in indirect calorimetry. On PTD 3, the patient had high nasogastric tube residuals, and we held jejunal tube feeds for 6 hours and then restarted them at a 20 mL/h basal rate. This basal rate was maintained for 24 hours, after which we again slowly increased the feedings to the goal rate. On PTD 4, the patient developed hemodynamic instability, including hypotension requiring vasopressors, attributed to pneumonia, and appropriate antibiotic therapy was initiated. The septic episode resolved by PTD 5. On PTD 5, we were concerned that the patient had had no bowel movements. On examination, the abdomen was soft. An abdominal roentgenogram revealed the feeding tube in the appropriate position with no dilated loops of bowel. The patient tolerated tube feeds at the goal rate without bowel movements until PTD 12. On this day, the patient developed acute acidosis, oliguria, hypotension requiring fluid and vasopressor support, and a rigid abdomen. An emergent computed tomographic (CT) scan of the abdomen revealed evidence of a small bowel obstruction and a small amount of fluid in the abdomen. We took the patient emergently to the operating room for abdominal exploration, where we found an ischemic terminal ileum secondary to massive dilation and obstruction from inspissated tube feedings. We resected the terminal ileum, and created an ileostomy and a long Hartman's pouch. Pathology reported the specimen to contain 24 cm of extremely dilated small bowel filled with a large amount of feces. The specimen had mucosal necrosis with no evidence of full-thickness ischemic injury. The patient recovered from this event but eventually died on PTD 74 after a respiratory arrest as a consequence of mucous plugging his airway. Case 2 A healthy 44-year-old man suffered a 50% TBSA burn with inhalation injury as the result of a jet fuel explosion. He required emergent endotracheal intubation, and was immediately admitted to the burn trauma intensive care unit and resuscitation was initiated. On PTD 3, we placed a nasojejunal feeding tube under endoscopic guidance. We began enteral feedings by using Replete with fiber on PTD 4, and increased the feedings over 24 hours to the goal rate determined by indirect calorimetry. On PTD 5, we diagnosed pneumonia and sepsis based on a chest roentgenogram and physical examination findings. He required vasopressors for 48 hours but seemed to tolerate enteral feedings, despite his poor pulmonary status. By PTD 6, the patient had not had a bowel movement, and we began daily docusate sodium and bisacodyl to aid bowel stimulation. On PTD 10, we ordered a Fleets enema, from which he had minimal results. At this time, we noted a soft abdomen and active bowel sounds. On PTD 13, the patient rapidly developed hemodynamic instability requiring norepinephrine to maintain an adequate pressure. We withheld tube feeds and noted that bowel sounds were decreased and the abdomen was soft. Continued clinical deterioration prompted an abdominal CT scan on PTD 14. This scan revealed multiple dilated loops of small bowel and pneumatosis of the cecum (Fig. 1). The patient demonstrated progressive oliguria, hypotension, and decreased oxygen saturation. We took the patient for emergent abdominal exploration, which revealed an obstructed and dilated colon and small bowel, with inspissated thick paste-like tube feedings throughout. The patient was treated with a diverting ileostomy and manual decompression of the impacted feeds through this stoma. The pathology report noted grossly dilated small bowel with no microscopic abnormality. The patient tolerated the surgical procedure and ultimately survived his injury. The ileostomy was taken down at a later date. FIG 1. CT scan of the abdomen (case 2) revealing multiple dilated loops of small bowel and pneumatosis of the cecum. Case 3 A healthy 22-year-old woman suffered toxic epidermal necrolysis syndrome presumably as a result of amoxicillin exposure. The illness developed 5 days before admission. When this condition progressed to toxic epidermal necrolysis syndrome, she was transferred from a referring hospital to the burn trauma intensive care unit. At arrival at our facility, we continued aggressive resuscitation and took the patient to the operating room for debridement and Biobrane coverage. We documented epidermal loss over greater than 95% TBSA. On the first day after admission (PAD 1), the patient underwent placement of a nasojejunal feeding tube under fluoroscopic guidance. On the following day, we began enteral feedings with Replete with fiber at a basal (20 mL/h) rate and increased to the goal rate over a 24-hour period. Despite aggressive therapy, adult respiratory distress syndrome and sepsis ensued, and on PAD 4, the patient required vasopressors for hemodynamic support. On PADs 7 and 9, the patient had bowel movements and, therefore, tube feedings were continued. On PAD 10, we noted the patient had a distended abdomen along with increased nasogastric tube output, and we reduced the feedings to half the goal rate. On PAD 11, improving clinical status allowed discontinuation of vasopressors. Despite this improved hemodynamic status, the patient developed increasing abdominal distension, and feedings were decreased to 20 mL/h for the next 4 days. Acutely, on PAD 15, the patient developed profound hypotension, tachycardia, oliguria, and abdominal distension. An abdominal CT scan was obtained revealing free intraperitoneal fluid and dilated loops of small bowel. We performed emergent laparotomy, which revealed obstructed, necrotic, and perforated small bowel with free peritoneal contamination of enteral tube feedings. We believe that the source of the obstruction was inspissated tube feedings that filled the entire small bowel. We resected the necrotic terminal ileum and placed a diverting ileostomy. Pathology findings revealed grossly dilated small bowel containing thick fecal contents. There was mucosal necrosis throughout the specimen, with full-thickness necrosis only at the margins of perforation. The patient recovered from this event and eventually survived the illness. Case 4 A healthy 22-year-old man suffered a 6.5% TBSA with inhalation injury secondary to a diesel fuel explosion. At the time of admission, we noted bilateral pneumothoraces and chest tubes were placed. The patient completed resuscitation by PTD 4 and was able to proceed with fluoroscopic nasojejunal feeding tube placement. We began Replete with fiber at a basal rate of 20 mL/h and gradually increased to the patient's goal rate as determined by indirect calorimetry. The patient tolerated tube feeds with no associated abdominal distention, low nasogastric tube residuals, and normal active bowel sounds. In the first week of admission the patient developed polymicrobial pneumonia and adult respiratory distress syndrome, although he displayed no hemodynamic instability. On PTD 10, the patient tolerated surgical placement of a tracheostomy for projected long-term ventilatory support. On PTD 13, the patient began having diarrhea, with up to 6 to 10 bowel movements each day. We decreased the rate of enteral feedings to 20 mL/h. Loose stools of small quantity continued for 48 hours, at which time the tube feeds were discontinued. At this point, PTD 15, the patient developed a distended abdomen with no bowel movements and no bowel sounds. The following day, the patient became hemodynamically unstable, requiring vasopressor support with gross abdominal distention and a firm abdomen. We obtained an abdominal CT scan, which revealed small bowel dilation, extensive feces throughout the colon, and free intraperitoneal air (Fig. 2). We took the patient to the operating room where exploration revealed a perforated sigmoid colon with significant peritoneal contamination. The sigmoid perforation was associated with grossly dilated colon secondary to impaction of enteral contents. On review, we believed the patient's diarrhea had been periobstructive leakage rather than evidence of adequate bowel function. The patient tolerated a sigmoid colectomy with colostomy and Hartman's pouch. Pathology findings revealed a dilated, profoundly thin-walled sigmoid colon with an area of perforation. The tissue surrounding the perforation was necrotic. There was no other necrotic bowel noted. The patient required two further explorations to drain intra-abdominal abscesses and finally required repair of a ventral hernia. The patient recovered from this abdominal complication and ultimately survived. FIG 2. CT scan of the abdomen (case 4) revealing dilated loops of small bowel and colon, and free intraperitoneal air. DISCUSSION In the past decade, early enteral feedings have been widely advocated in the trauma literature, as several advantages to early enteral feedings are cited. Enteral feedings in animal models can reduce gut barrier breakdown and improve the immune function of the bowel. 13,14 Lowry performed a randomized, controlled, human trial which revealed that enteral feedings, when compared with parenteral nutrition, supported decreased elaboration of acute phase reactants including C-protein, TNF, and IL-6, when challenged with an endotoxin exposure. 6 Moore and Jones completed several related studies of the effects of enteral nutrition in trauma patients. In the first, they compared enteral feedings with no feedings. 4 In the second, they compared early enteral nutrition with early parenteral nutrition. 7,14 All of these studies revealed significant decreases in septic complications in the enterally fed groups. Mochizuki and Trocki found in an animal study that enteral feedings may decrease the secretion of catabolic hormones and reduce nutritional stress and protein depletion. 8 Finally, there are several reports that document the cost advantages of enteral feedings compared with parenteral nutritional support. 9,15 Kalfarentzos et al. report that enteral nutrition can be three times less expensive than parenteral nutrition. 9 For the reasons noted, current trauma literature promotes early enteral feeding in trauma patients. 11,16 The successful reports of early and aggressive use of enteral nutrition, sometimes beginning in the recovery room, have led to the widespread acceptance of this method of nutritional support, and the concept that such a regimen is easy and error-free. Unfortunately, no medical intervention is without associated adverse effects. Complications associated with tube feedings, although rare, are most commonly diarrhea and aspiration. A review of complications of enteral feedings by Cataldi Betcher et al., prospectively evaluated all enterally supported patients in a 12-month period. 10 They categorized complications as mechanical, including tube obstruction and placement; gastrointestinal complications, including diarrhea, bloating, and vomiting; and metabolic complications, including electrolyte disturbances. They found that mechanical complications occurred in 3.5% of the 253 patients evaluated, gastrointestinal complications occurred in 6.2%, and metabolic complications occurred in only 2%. In most reported studies, diarrhea was the most common complication, occurring in 2 to 68% of cases. 17 Other complications such as gastrointestinal obstruction have been infrequently reported. In our series, intestinal obstruction occurred in 4 of 237 enterally fed burn patients, an incidence of 1.7% in a 3-year period. An extensive Medline search of the literature revealed few references to gastrointestinal obstruction related to enteral feeds. Two reported cases describe tube feed bezoars obstructing the small bowel. One bezoar was of unknown cause. 18 the second was due to mechanical obstruction with Bilroth II anatomy. 19 In addition, there have been few case reports regarding esophageal obstruction from refluxed gastric tube feeds. 20,21 No references to complications of inspissated tube feedings were found. In each of the cases documented here, "early" enteral feedings were started after appropriate burn trauma resuscitation was completed. Each patient seemed to tolerate tube feeds until a sudden decompensation, at approximately 2 weeks after trauma, at which time they developed acute catastrophic complications. It is worthy of note that these were not uncomplicated patients: each patient suffered an episode of sepsis before the development of the tube feeding complication, in three cases requiring vasopressor support for short periods. Moore and Clevenger advocate initiation of enteral feedings after acute trauma resuscitation is completed. This correlates with the conversion from an initial anabolic state to a profound catabolic state within the first 24 hours after trauma. 4,5,22 The early initiation of feedings (<48 hours) seems to be most beneficial. 23,24 Patients have evidence of intestinal mucosal atrophy within 24 hours of admission to an intensive care unit. 25 Additionally, Cerra reported that enteral feedings in critically ill patients may be continued through episodes of intermittent ileus without awaiting flatus or bowel movements to document return of bowel function. 16 In contrast to these studies advocating early feeding in critically ill patients, Kirby and Teran argued that hypotensive and vasopressor dependent patients may have tube feed intolerance, secondary to shunting of intestinal blood flow. 11 We suspect that the persistent lowflow, septic state in each of our patients led to bowel dysmotility, which may have contributed to the eventual feeding complications. Another common denominator in these cases is that all four patients received tube feedings supplemented with bulk fiber. Several authors have advocated the use of fiber-supplemented formulas to treat loose or frequent bowel movements. 10,11 A recent review by Khalil et al. indicates that fiber supplemented formulas only increased stool consistency, but did not reduce the frequency of bowel movements and did not adequately treat feeding-induced diarrhea. 26 Additionally, bulk fiber feeding is not free of complications. Several cases reports have previously documented obstructing food bezoars caused by significant loads of bulk fiber. 27-29 It is our feeling that decreased bowel function, secondary to posttraumatic ileus and secondary sepsis as well as the bulk fiber load, contributed to the inspissated tube feeds that caused obstruction and emergent complications. Several case reports and literature reviews in the past decade have documented a syndrome of nonobstructive bowel necrosis secondary to enteral feedings. 30-32 Each of these reports cite diffuse or distinct areas of fullthickness necrosis of the small bowel without evidence of obstruction or strangulating pathology. These reports also conclude that vasoconstriction attributable to hypovolemia and vasopressor therapy leads to a low-flow state in a high-demand condition of chronic feedings. As noted, we concur that a low-flow state contributed to our patients' morbidity, yet, we believe that frank bowel obstruction from the enteral formula and bowel dysmotility led to the patients' complications. We do not feel they fit a strict definition of nonobstructive small bowel necrosis, although we recognize that this may represent an early presentation of a spectrum of this disease. Two of our four patients did not have evidence of full-thickness necrosis, and the remaining two had necrosis only at the margins of perforated bowel. Operative and pathology reports noted obvious sites of obstruction in each case, with no anatomic abnormalities of the bowel identified. These specimen reports do indicate that in each case the bowel lumen was grossly distended with enteral formula/fecal contents. It is evident from our case review and previous case reports that the cause of these ischemic/obstructive complications has not been identified and may be multifactorial. Gaddy et al. and Smith-Choban and Max reported on ischemic complications from feeding jejunostomies and hypothesized that although hypotension and burn trauma contributed to ischemia of the bowel, the content of the feedings, specifically the hyperosmolar content, may also have significantly contributed to these complications. 33,34 It seems complications of obstruction and ischemia may represent a spectrum of a disease state in overaggressive feeding in the face of bowel compromised from multiple illness-related factors. To summarize, we have identified a population who suffered bowel obstruction and necrosis from fiber supplemented enteral feedings. We believe the inciting event involves chronic delivery of bulky feedings in the face of compromised bowel motility. There are several factors that may have led to bowel dysmotility in this population, including anesthetic and analgesic medications, ventilator dependence, burn shock and hypovolemia, and pharmacologic vasoconstriction. Each of these factors puts patients at high risk for tube feeding intolerance. In addition, each of these factors inhibits the ability to evaluate for subjective evidence of gastrointestinal distress, frequently impeding recognition of potential complications. Our experience with four cases of profound complications associated with enteral feedings has led us to change our regimen for use of posttraumatic enteral feedings. We make the following recommendations: First, in the high-risk population, fiber supplemented formulas should be used in specific cases, only to increase stool consistency. Second, the use and rate of tube feeding should proceed more cautiously. Without early evidence of bowel function, feeds should be held at trophic-basal levels until bowel function is well documented. Patients suffering prolonged septic or hypotensive episodes should have tube feedings reduced to trophic levels or held until these circumstances resolve, and objective evidence of bowel function can be documented. Finally, patients with abdominal symptoms of unexplained cause should be evaluated with prompt imaging, and if obstruction is suspected, decompression and diversion of the bowel should be considered to prevent further complications. Bowel obstruction secondary to early fiber supplemented enteral feedings in septic trauma patients is a rare, lifethreatening complication. In a review of four cases, each patient was successfully treated with exploration and decompression. Understanding and early recognition of this potential catastrophic complication are essential to prevention and treatment. REFERENCES 1. Moore FA, Moore EE. The benefits of enteric feeding. title: Mixed fibers diet in surgical ICU septic patients
abstract: Diarrhea commonly occurs in surgical critically ill patients, especially septic patients and fiber formulas have been reported to improve diarrhea. Most reports have used soluble or insoluble fiber exclusively, while the effects of a mixed fiber diet remain unclear. This study compares diarrhea scores between mixed-fiber and non-fiber diets in surgical septic patients receiving broad spectrum antibiotics. We conducted a prospective randomized control double blind study in a general surgical ICU. Patients who received broad spectrum antibiotics and no contraindication to enteral feeding were randomly allocated to a fiber or non-fiber diet for up to 14 days. Nutritional delivery and diarrhea scores were recorded daily. Intention to treat analysis was performed. Thirty-four patients were enrolled in the study, 17 in the fiber group and 17 in non-fiber group. These two patients groups were similar in demographics, disease severity, nutritional status, cause of sepsis and total feeding per day. The proportion of patients with diarrhea score >=12 was higher in the non-fiber group than in the fiber group, but the difference was not statistically significant [8/17 (47.06%) vs. 4/17(23.53%); p=0.15]. However, the fiber group had a lower mean diarrhea score (fiber vs. non-fiber = 3.6 +/- 2.3 vs. 6.3 +/- 3.6; p=0.005), as well as a lower global diarrhea score from the generalized estimation equation model for repeated measurement [Coefficient -3.03 (95%CI= -5.03 to -0.92); p=0.005]. In summary, a mixed fiber diet formula can reduce the diarrhea score in surgical critically ill septic patients who received broad spectrum antibiotics.

Key Words: mixed fiber, soluble fiber, insoluble fiber, diarrhea, surgical critically ill INTRODUCTION Diarrhea commonly occurs in critically ill septic patients who receive broad spectrum antibiotics. About 70-80% of surgical patients admitted to the surgical-ICU in our institute need broad spectrum antibiotics due to sepsis.1 Fiberenriched enteral formulas can stimulate the growth of beneficial normal flora bacteria which inhibit harmful bacteria, and there is ample evidence of their beneficial effects. Soluble fiber pectin can prevent diarrhea related to enteral-feeding.2 Some cereal fibers could be fermented by colonic bacteria and produce short chain fatty acids (SCFAs).3 SCFAs are rapidly cleared from the colon and increase sodium absorption which promote transmucosal water transportation and diminish diarrhea.4-5 Homann et al. performed a study on soluble fiber diet (10 g/L of hydrolyzed guar gum) in total or supplemental enteral nutrition and found that it reduced the incidence of diarrhea but the authors excluded patients who received broad spectrum antibiotics.5 In septic patients, Spapen et al. investigated the effects of soluble fiber (22 g/L of partially hydrolyzed guar gum) and also found favorable outcomes.6 However, their study population was limited to those with severe sepsis, and septic shock, most of whom were patients from medical unit who received a concentrated soluble fiber guar diet. Although diet containing insoluble fibers increase intestinal motion and bulk up stool quantity, most purely insoluble fiber (soy polysaccharide) feeds have demonstrated negligible effects on diarrhea in the critically ill.7-9 Even though previ- ous studies have investigated fiber’s effects in mixed critically ill populations, they have used single fiber diets. Thus, the results of mixed fiber formulas remain unclear, especially in surgical patients who are predisposed to intestinal intolerance. Therefore, the aims of this study are to demonstrate the effects of an enteral formula containing mixed soluble and insoluble fiber in a single population of surgical critically ill septic patients who needed broad spectrum antibiotics. MATERIALS AND METHODS We conducted a prospective randomized control double blind study in a six-beds general intensive care unit (ICU) of a university hospital from February 2007 through November 2008. Patients who were admitted to surgical ICU were classified into those with sepsis and those without according to consensus guideline.10 Septic patients who received broad spectrum antibiotics and total enteral feeding were eligible for enrollment. Exclusion criteria were Corresponding Author: Dr Kaweesak Chittawatanarat, Department of Surgery, Faculty of Medicine, Maharaj Nakorn Chiang Mai Hospital, Chiang Mai University, Amphur Muang, Chiang Mai, Thailand 50200. Tel: +66-53945533; Fax: +66-53946139 Email: kchittaw@gmail.com Manuscript received 7 April 2010. Initial review completed 7 June 2010. Revision accepted 1 July 2010. K Chittawatanarat, P Pokawinpudisnun and Y Polbhakdee 1) hemodynamic instability; 2) bowel obstruction, hemorrhage, ileus; 3) acute pancreatitis; 4) post-endoscopy <24 hours; 5) bowel resection and anastomosis <24 hours; 6) ischemic bowel; 7) inflammatory bowel, ulcerative colitis, Crohn’s disease; 8) enteric fistula and; 9) gut malabsorptive syndrome. Eligible patients who did not sign informed consent were also excluded. We recorded the patients’ demographic data, admission disease, type of procedure, type of antibiotics, APACHE II score, SAPS II score, weight, height and albumin level. Nutritional requirements were calculated using the Harris Benedict equation. The main outcome assessment was a diarrhea score according to Hart and Dobb as shown in Table 1.11 After patients were randomized, they received assigned diets for at least 5 days. Diarrhea scores were followed for up to 14 days or until changing from enteral nutrition to normal oral diet. A daily score was obtained by summing the scores for each stool over a 24-hour period. Diarrhea was defined as a daily accumulation score more than or equal 12.6 Scores were collected by the same investigator who was blinded to the study. In this study, the length of ICU and hospital stay (ICU and hospital LOS) started at the intervention assignment date until ICU and hospital discharge respectively. We randomly assigned two diets to patients. The intervention group received a mixed fiber formula [Nutren Fibre®, Nestlé Suisse S.A. (Switzerland)] and the control group received a standard formula without combined fiber [Nutren Optimum®, Nestlé Suisse S.A. (Switzerland)]. The nutritional composition of fat: protein: carbohydrates of the mixed fiber and standard formula were 38: 40.1: 126.5 and 38: 40: 126.3 kcal/L respectively. Both diets had the same osmolarity 360 mOsm/L after brewing according to standard recommendation. The fiber formula contained 15.1 g/L of dietary fiber which was produced by yellow pea fiber mixed with fructo-oligosaccharide, which is not present in the standard formula. The formula had an approximately one to one ratio of soluble and insoluble fiber. Soluble fiber was 35% FOS and 15% pectin. Insoluble fiber was approximately 30% cellulose 5% lignin and 15% hemicelluloses. Both diet formulas were prepared and packaged by dietary unit in our hospital by randomized assignment then delivered to the surgical ICU and handled by a nurse who was unaware of the study allocation. All eligible patients strictly followed the feeding guidelines of the surgical critical care unit protocol. Medications that directly cause diarrhea such as metoclopramide, quinidine, xylitol, magnesium, erythromycin, aminophylline, sorbitol and phosphorus were prohibited. This study was approved by the Committee for Ethics in Human Research of Faculty of Medicine, Chiang Mai University. Informed consent was obtained from all cases. All data were analyzed by STATA 11 software. These are presented as mean with standard deviation in normal distribution continuous data. All categorical variables were analyzed by chi-square tests, except when small size required the use of Fisher’s exact test. Comparison of continuous variables among groups was performed using Student’s t test for variables with normal distribution and the Mann-Whitney U test for variables with non-normal distribution. All patients who were allocated to a treatment group were included in analytic processes regardless 459 Table 1. Diarrhea score proposed by Hart and Dobb Consistency Formed Semi-solid Liquid <200 1 3 5 Estimated volume (mL) 200-250 >250 2 3 6 9 10 15 of whether or not they completed the intervention protocol (Intention to treat analysis). The main outcomes of repeated measurement diarrhea scores were analyzed by repeated measurement model. Incidence density of diarrhea ≥12 was analyzed by Poisson’s distribution, while diarrhea score was analyzed by Gaussian distribution. Hazard ratio of time to start of first diarrhea was performed by Cox regression. Differences were considered to be statistically significant when p<0.05. RESULTS During of the 19-month study period, 189 septic patients who received broad spectrum antibiotics were eligible. Only 34 consenting septic patients with no contraindication for enteral feeding and consent were randomized and allocated to this study (Figure 1). The 17 patients in the intervention group were fed with mixed fiber supplement formula (fiber group). The other 17 patients, the control group, were fed with standard formula (non-fiber group). The baseline characteristics and severity of disease at admission for both groups were comparable as shown in Table 2. The most common sites for infection were pulmonary system and urinary system. The frequently prescribed antibiotics were beta-lactam and carbapenem for gram negative sepsis, glycopeptides for gram positive infection and clindamycin for anaerobic infection. After randomized allocation, all patients received enteral diet for at least five days. Only 10 patients in the Figure 1. Flow chart of study. The fiber group achieved mean caloric delivery of 1500 kcal earlier (at day 6th) than those in non-fiber group (at day 8th). However; there was no statistically significant difference in mean caloric intake when comparing daily delivery between groups (Figure 2). At the end of cohort, 14 days after the start of enteral feeding, we found that the accu- mulation and mean diarrhea score in the fiber group was significantly lower than in that found in the non-fiber group (50.8±32.0 vs. 87.7±50.7; p<0.01 and 3.6±2.3 vs. 6.3±3.6; p <0.01 respectively). Although the incidence of patients with at least one day of diarrhea, defined by diarrheal score ≥12 in this study, had a lower trend in the fiber receiving group (4/17 (23.5%) vs. 8/17 (47.1%); p=0.14), the overall incidence of diarrhea density per 100 K Chittawatanarat, P Pokawinpudisnun and Y Polbhakdee 461 the study period. Three patients died, one in the fiber group and two in the control group. The remaining 31 patients survived to hospital discharge. Cause of death of the participant in the fiber group was ARDS. In the nonfiber group, patients died from severe sepsis and acute myocardial infarction. No deaths were associated with our intervention. patient-fed days in the mixed model was significantly lower in the fiber group (6.7 vs. 14.8; IRR=0.45; 95% Confidence interval; CI, 0.2-0.9; p=0.01). Repeated measurement of incidence rate ratio of diarrhea (IRR) had also significantly lowered in the fiber group when enteral nutrition started after receiving broad spectrum antibiotics (IRR 0.44; 95% CI, 0.2-0.9; p=0.04). Daily mean and standard deviation of diarrhea scores after 14 days of random assignment are shown in Figure 3. Diarrhea scores were statistically different from day 3 through day 11 except for day 8. Those differences were confirmed by the repeated measurement model, in which the coefficient of the fiber group was -3.03 (95%CI= -5.0 to -0.9; p<0.01). Although data cannot show the median time of the start of diarrhea’s due to the lower incidence of diarrhea in the follow-up period but it is probable that the fiber group had a lower trend of first diarrhea beginning at day 14th (Table 3) with a hazard ratio of 0.45 (95% CI, 0.13-1.49; p=0.19). Surviving patients in the fiber supplement and control groups had mean durations of ICU stay of 16.8 days (6-37 days) and 25.5 days (11-50 days) respectively (p=0.06). Mean hospital LOS of the fiber and non-fiber groups were 30.9 days (6-120 days) and 36.1 days (15-61 days) (p=0.07). No adverse outcomes due to either type of diet (such as allergy, aspiration pneumonia, sinusitis, tube displacement and tube obstruction) were observed during DISCUSSION Sepsis-induced diarrhea may have many causes such as Clostidium difficile infection, gut ischemia, hypoalbuminemia and cellular derangement associated with shock. In addition, prolonged total parenteral nutrition impairs gut tolerance to enteral feeding by enhancing mucosal atrophy and reducing enzyme activity, which precipitates diarrhea in critically ill patients.12 The interventions which can prevent or intercept those mechanisms might minimize the occurrence of diarrhea. Of those interventions, satisfactory results have been reported for diets containing fiber. Dietary fibers have been divided into two groups, insoluble and soluble fibers. Insoluble fiber in a blended enteral diet, such as lignin cellulose and hemicelluloses, cannot hold water and has little effect on viscosity. The natural source of these fibers is soy bean or soy polysaccharide. Soluble fiber and fiber-like substance (e.g. pectin, guar gum, inulin and FOS) have gelling properties and increase viscosity. Many studies have demonstrated that a combination of these fibers can reduce diarrhea.2,5-6,16 Most of those could be fermented by intestinal bacteria and result in short chain fatty acids (SCFAs).2-3,14 These final products have an important role in gut function. Firstly, SCFAs are rapidly cleared from the colon and are associated with the activity of the sodium – hydrogen exchanger system which facilitate water absorption.17 Secondly, SCFAs have ancillary benefits in reversing toxin induced secretion in cholera and other diarrheas, as well as decrease the Clostidium difficile toxin uptake to cells and reducing the cytopathic effect to colonic cells.1819 Finally, SCFAs have the anti-inflammatory effects via molecular mechanism such as NFκB inhibition and reduction of proinflammatory cytokines.20-21 However, no hypotheses regarding these mechanisms could be confirmed in this study because it did not investigate the differences in intestinal flora and fecal SCFAs concentration between the two groups. Using these background hypotheses, the authors investigated the effects of a mixture of insoluble and soluble fiber on clinical outcomes (Table 3). After randomization, the patient background in each group was comparable (Table 1) Accumulation diarrhea scores, daily mean scores and incidence density of score per 100 patient–fed days revealed statistical differences between the groups. However, there were only lower trends of at least one day diarrhea incidence, hazard ratio of diarrhea beginning, ICU length of stay and hospital length of stay in the fiber group. It is possible this could occur due to the small sample size and that results would be different in larger trials. The authors analyzed both mixed model and repeated model of individual patient of diarrhea scores and diarrhea occurrences, and both analyses demonstrated statistically significant differences (Table 3 and figure 3). This study demonstrates the clinical outcomes of diarrhea but not the underlying mechanism. The important one is Clostidium difficile toxins. They are routinely investigated in clinical practice in developed countries, but laboratory settings are not commonly available in most of government public hospital in Thailand because they are not as cost effectiveness as therapeutic diagnosis with metronidazole. Therefore, the authors cannot conclude that Clostidium difficile inhibition or toxin neutralization was improved by soluble fiber. Although previous studies have preferred soluble fiber to insoluble fiber for diminishing diarrhea in critically ill patients, the authors’ results have demonstrated better outcome using mixed formula.6-9,16 There are two possible explanations: 1) only the soluble fibers play an important role and; 2) soluble and insoluble fibers have a synergistic interaction which enhances each other’s effects in mixed formula while there are fewer effects when separately administered. The authors prefers the second hypothesis for the following reasons. Despite different types of soluble fiber between studies, previous studies achieved favorable results by adding 10-22 g/L of soluble fiber to their trial formulas whereas this study obtained similar results by adding only 7.5 g/L.5-6 Accurate causal association needs further investigation, either by factorial two by two designs of soluble and insoluble fiber or by threearmed randomization of soluble, insoluble and mixed fiber fibers. Even though mean caloric intake was not different between the two groups, the fiber group reached its caloric target earlier than the non-fiber group. This phenomenon might be explained by fecal content in each group’s patients. In common practice, when diarrhea occurs, most physicians prefer to discontinue enteral feeding and observe until the problem is resolved before then resuming feeding. As previously explained, there was less enteral interruption and more enteral administration in the fiber group. Finally, there were many limitations to this study. The first is the small number of patients, so that some results were only demonstrated tendencies. Larger populations are needed to confirm these tendencies. Second, the study could only demonstrate global clinical outcomes but could not explain the underlying mechanisms. Detailed investigation in future trials could explain pathophysiological responses of intervention such as fecal intestinal flora difference, fecal SCFAs and Clostidium difficile toxin. Third, heterogeneous fiber composition in each study might have different results despite the similar physical characters.6,16 Therefore; this study can postulate and reveal the global effects of mixed fibers but it cannot accurately summarize which favorable results occur from which types of fiber in the formula. In addition, various methods were used to measure diarrhea in the different studies with no standard consensus, so that it might be difficult to compare results among different studies. Lastly, the primary endpoint in this study focused on occurrence of diarrhea. Further studies should concentrate on other important clinical endpoints such as severity of disease improvement, mortality and weight change or alteration of nutritional parameters such as albumin, prealbumin (transthyretin) or thyroglobulin between groups. In conclusion, formula containing mixed fiber is safe and can reduced diarrhea scores in surgical critically ill septic patients who need broad spectrum antibiotics. title: Pectin-containing liquid enteral nutrition for critical care: a historical control and propensity score matched study
abstract: BACKGROUND AND OBJECTIVES: Pectin-containing liquid enteral nutrition (PCLEN) contains pectin, which becomes solid in the stomach and therefore mitigates vomiting and diarrhea. Its efficacy for use in critical care medicine was evaluated. METHODS AND STUDY DESIGN: We used liquid enteral nutrition (LEN) (traditional LEN (TLEN)) as the primary LEN at the emergency and critical care center. We adopted PCLEN as the primary LEN from 2014. During 2012-2016, 954 patients admitted to intensive care units and emergency wards were given PCLEN or TLEN. We conducted propensity score matching for 693 eligible patients for age, sex, and organ dysfunctions for six organs. RESULTS: We included 199 PCLEN patients and 199 TLEN patients. Severity was higher in the PCLEN group. The enteral nutrition failure rate was significantly lower for PCLEN than for TLEN. The diarrhea incidence rates were 28.1% vs 38.2% (p=0.033), and the incidence rates of nosocomial pneumonia were 4.5% and 9.6% (p=0.048). For PCLEN, the enteral nutrition failure rates were not different for patients with gastric acid inhibitors and without them. CONCLUSIONS: PCLEN can be used effectively for critically ill patients irrespective of the use of gastric acid inhibitors. It can decrease the incidence of enteral nutrition failure and diarrhea.

Conclusions: PCLEN can be used effectively for critically ill patients irrespective of the use of gastric acid inhibitors. It can decrease the incidence of enteral nutrition failure and diarrhea. Key Words: enteral nutrition, critical care, pectin, diarrhea, intensive care INTRODUCTION Liquid enteral nutrition (LEN) has been reported as important for critical care medicine. We recommend starting its administration as soon as possible in clinical practice.1 Early enteral nutrition is physiologically advantageous for immunity and the gut tract system.2 Nevertheless, various phenomena resist enteral feeding in critically ill patients: enteral nutrition failure often occurs.3 Diarrhea and vomiting are the main reasons for enteral nutrition failure.4 The recent widespread use of antibiotics reportedly exacerbates diarrhea events.5 Severely ill patients often cannot be relieved with enteral nutrition. Diarrhea and vomiting are common complications affecting chronic patients and those undergoing critical care. Therefore, approaches have been undertaken to make LEN solid and thereby decrease gastroesophageal reflux, vomiting, and diarrhea. Some LEN with a semi-solid consistency are sold. Although some studies have examined the efficacy of semi-solid LEN against gastroesophageal reflux,6-8 no large clinical study has been reported. Especially, whether diarrhea can be decreased or not has not been studied. Pectin is dietary fiber that changes to a solid by forming a bridged structure when exposed to calcium ion.9-11 Pectin-containing liquid enteral nutrition (PCLEN), which can be given in a liquid condition, is expected to become solid in gastric acid pH. In Japan, PCLEN has been marketed as HINE E-GEL® from 2014. When given as a diet, the solidification can occur without risk of tube occlusion and can be expected to inhibit diarrhea and vomiting. The use of PCLEN is increasing in chronic enteral feeding patients in Japan. Nevertheless, no report has described a study examining the efficacy of PCLEN. Especially, no reported study has examined the use of PCLEN in critical care medicine. The efficacy of pectin alone reportedly decreases diarrhea in a small randomized study in critically ill patients. However, no significant difference was found between the pectin group and the fiber-free group.12 Because solidification of PCLEN occurs because of the gastric acid pH, it is necessary to examine for PCLEN whether gastric acid inhibitors weaken solidification or not. HINE E-GEL® is expected to be solid under acidic conditions with pH less than 5.
