There was no correlation between the average amount of glutamine supplied up to day 4 (13 ⫾ 6 g per day) and the L/M index on day 4 (r ⫽ ⫺0.02; 95% CI, 0.37– 0.34; exact p ⫽ .91), nor between the amount of glutamine given up to day 7 (12 ⫾ 6 g per day) and the L/M index on day 7 (r ⫽ 0.41; 95% CI, 0.29 – 0.83; exact p ⫽ .24). No correlation was observed between the amount of glutamine given and the average MOF score (r ⫽ ⫺0. 11; 95% CI, 0.22– 0. 42; exact p ⫽ .52). DISCUSSION Severe trauma is associated with a high incidence of septic complications and the multiple organ dysfunction syndrome, which markedly influence patient outcome. Nutrition plays an important role in the function of the immune system in these critically ill patients. In recent years, enteral nutrition has become the preferred method of feeding critically ill patients, and several substrates with presumably immunomodula- tory effects have been added to enteral diets. Nevertheless, analyses of separate groups of critically ill patients have shown that the beneficial effects are not the same for each patient population and substance added.29,30 In this study, no differences were observed in mortality, ICU stay, and days of mechanical ventilation between the groups treated with the various immunomodulatory supplements. Yet patients supplemented with Synbiotic 2000 developed fewer infections and had consequently lower PCT levels than those receiving fiber or peptide diets, even if they had larger gastric retention volumes. The gastric retention volume, as measured by repeat gastric aspirations, was asymptomatic and did not seem to increase the risk of regurgitation or chest infections, as other authors have recently reported in the literature.31 Instead, the presence of synbiotics in the stomach and intestinal tract might have contributed to the elimination of potentially pathogenic flora from the stomach and hereby contributed to the reduction in the incidence of pneu- TABLE IV Differences between multiple injured patients fed with glutamine supplements (A) and those fed a peptide diet (C) Age, y ISS APACHE II Days receiving mechanical ventilation Days of ICU treatment Initiation of EN after trauma (h) Volume of EN first 24 h (mL) Collective volume of EN first 4 days (mL) Average gastric retention volume first week (mL) Average CRP Average PCT Average MOF L/M index day 2 L/M index day 4 L/M index day 7 A (32 patients), median (Q1–Q3) C (26 patients), median (Q1–Q3) p values for U test 31 (23–50) 26 (22–33) 11 (4.5–18) 10 (6–16) 14 (8.3–23) 15.5 (13–20.3) 250 (157–562) 2720 (2356–3700) 410 (382–1062) 120 (95–142) 0.51 (0.41–1.58) 2.5 (2.0–3.6) 0.050 (0.013–0.116) 0.102 (0.029–0.201) 0.159 (0.088–0.311) 41 (26–54) 35 (25–42) 13.5 (10–18) 8 (4–15) 11.5 (6–20) 12.8 (10–18) 387 (87–740) 2675 (1515–3908) 620 (337–1190) 134 (63–182) 1.25 (1.00–2.32) 3.4 (2.8–4) 0.076 (0.032–0.103) 0.085 (0.043–0.160) 0.103 (0.066–0.183) .55 .005 .11 .20 .28 .12 .21 .93 .90 .45 .005 .16 .22 .64 .76 Significant differences in bold. It should also be observed that enteral nutrition in this study was achieved by gastric feeding, so the larger amounts of fibers administered into the stomach in group D might add to the increased volumes of gastric contents, as it is well known from the literature that dietary fibers increase gastroesophageal reflux but not gastric empting.32 In this study, synbiotic supplementation with 4 fibers (group D) influenced gastric emptying, in contrast to the smaller dose of only 1 fermentable fiber (group B). The increase in IP in critically ill patients, which has been observed in different groups of patients with shock, burns, and multiple injuries, is known to decrease with the introduction of enteral nutrition.18,33 In our group of 113 multiple injured patients, IP showed uniform growth until day 4 in 3 of the 4 patient groups, despite enteral nutrition, with only the patient group supplemented with Synbiotic 2000 exhibiting a significant drop on day 7. It should be emphasized that the glutamine-supplemented group showed a significant rise in IP, even though glutamine has been described as a substance that maintains gut structure in experimental settings in animals fed either enterally or parenterally.34,35 Glutamine should therefore be expected to decrease IP. In fact, most human studies suggest that the parenteral supply of glutamine is associated with a significant decrease in IP, whereas studies using the enteral route show variable results.36 –38 Novak and colleagues12 suggested that intolerance problems are the main obstacle to higher glutamine supplementation during enteral nutrition and thus recommended its parenteral use. Hulsewé and coauthors,39 however, were unable to document any improvements in gut morphology and barrier function after the parenteral application of a glutamineenriched solution. Our glutamine-supplemented patients received a relatively low dose of 13 ⫾ 6 g glutamine per day, which could possibly explain the ineffectiveness of the treatment. However, no correlation was observed between the amount of glutamine given and the severity of IP, or between the amounts of glutamine given and the average MOF scores. The latter has been reported to be unsuitable for critically ill patients.7 A recent study on 185 trauma patients was unable to prove any advantage of enteral supplementation of glutamine alone or in combination with arginine.40 A significant drop in IP was seen in the group supplemented with the synbiotic formula. We therefore conclude that early enteral administration of Synbiotic 2000 to patients after injury induces a significant drop in IP by the end of the first week. In our previous study of multiple injured patients who received early enteral feedings with Jevity (Abbott-Ross), which contains 14.4 g of fibers per L, we observed a decrease of IP on day 4. Unfortunately, no comparison can be made between Synbiotic 2000 and Jevity, as no measurements were made on day 7 in the previous study.18 The effects observed with the synbiotic treatment used in this study are most likely specific, as they have not been documented before with any synbiotic or fiber treatment.17,41,42 However, a minimal influence of a commercial probiotic composition plus a small dose of fiber in critically ill patients has recently been reported.43,44 It was found to favorably alter the microbial composition of the upper gastrointestinal tract but not to have any effect on IP, nor to be associated with any measurable clinical benefit.43 In that study, EN was commenced considerably later than in our present one, and a mixture of yogurt bacteria and lactobacilli was used, which probably have a low survival in gastric juice and therefore less immunologic effect. Our treatment was started as soon as possible after admission in apparently stabilized patients, and never ⬎24 hours after trauma, and was continued as long as the patients remained in the ICU. The LAB preparations in that study were supplied in a concentration of 109 in contrast to 1010 in the present study. Furthermore, a freeze-dried powder dissolved before use was used in our study as opposed to capsules—which might be disputable—in the other study.42 Another study did not find any beneficial effects of 5 ⫻ 107 strains of Lactobacillus plantarum 299v when supplied postoperatively to surgical patients; the most TABLE VIII Differences between multiple injured patients fed with peptide diets (C) and those fed with soluble fiber supplements (B) Age, y ISS APACHE II Days receiving mechanical ventilation Days of ICU treatment Initiation of EN after trauma (h) Volume of EN first 24 h (mL) Collective volume of EN first 4 days (mL) Average gastric retention volume first week (mL) Average CRP Average PCT Average MOF L/M index day 2 L/M index day 4 L/M index day 7 C (26 patients), median (Q1–Q3) B (29 patients), median (Q1–Q3) p values for U test 41 (26–54) 35 (25–42) 13.5 (10–18) 8 (4–15) 11.5 (6–20) 12.8 (10–18) 387 (87–740) 2675 (1515–3908) 620 (337–1190) 134 (63–182) 1.25 (1.00–2.32) 3.4 (2.8–4) 0.076 (0.032–0.103) 0.085 (0.043–0.160) 0.103 (0.066–0.183) 36 (22–51) 30 (24–35) 14 (11–18) 12 (8–15) 16 (10–21) 12.5 (9.6–15) 430 (100–600) 3250 (2400–3700) 740 (530–1510) 135 (108–164) 0.50 (0.10–1.80) 3.5 (2.6–4.0) 0.041 (0.022–0.154) 0.075 (0.031–0.170) 0.083 (0.052–0.124) .77 .08 .72 .24 .27 .60 .71 .46 .13 .78 .024 .86 .52 .99 .90 Significant differences in bold. Dynamics of the lactulose-mannitol index in multiple injured patients fed with supplements of glutamine (A), soluble fibers (B), peptide diet (C), and the group fed with supplements of soluble fibers and lactobacilli (D). *Significant rise in group A from 0.050 (0.013– 0.116) on day 2 to 0.159 (0.088 – 0.311) on day 7 (p ⬍ .02) and from 0.050 (0.013– 0.116) on day 2 to 0.102 (0.029 – 0.201) on day 4 (p ⬍ .057). **Significant drop in group D, from 0.1476 (0.0565– 0.2400 on day 4 to 0.0606 (0.0404 – 0.0995) on day 7 (p ⬍ .05). obvious reason for failure is probably that the dose used was too small.44 As for the peptide diet, our main reason for choosing the group fed with this diet, which showed no difference in comparison to others, was that it did not contain glutamine or fibers, and hence its main purpose was to serve as a control. This diet has also been proposed to be more efficacious and better tolerated than whole-protein formulas in critically ill patients. However we were unable to find any advantage of this feeding, which is in line with other observations.45 It is difficult to deliver a sufficient amount of immunomodulatory substance to critically ill patients, as they often have intolerance problems, as discussed in the review of enteral immunonutrition.12 Supplemented glutamine and fermentable fibers were diluted in a commercial diet. However, the Synbiotic was administered before feeding started in the morning; hence, some of its influence could be attributed to this administration protocol, which favors the full addition of immunonutrient. Our original intention was to deliver all the supplements in the same manner, but Synbiotic, which contains pectin, caused tube clogging when added to the formula, so the manufacturer’s instructions were followed, with Synbiotic being administered before feeding. Therefore, in order to avoid the influence of intolerance problems on the delivery of immunonutrients, it might be advisable to administer all immunomodulatory supplements separate from feedings. title: The effects of psyllium hydrophilic mucilloid on diarrhea in enterally fed patients
abstract: OBJECTIVE: To investigate the efficacy of psyllium hydrophilic mucilloid (PHM) for prevention of diarrhea and to compare methods of PHM delivery. DESIGN: Experimental. SETTING: University-affiliated Department of Veterans Affairs Medical Center. SUBJECTS: Sixty patients from medical-surgical and intensive care units who received newly initiated enteral feeding via feeding tube. OUTCOME MEASURES: Diarrhea, stool frequency and consistency, and feeding tube obstruction. INTERVENTION: Receipt of PHM (7 gm, twice-daily) added to continuous feeding or given as a bolus with intermittent feeding, or receipt of No PHM for 7 days after initiation of enteral feeding. RESULTS: Fifteen subjects (25%) developed diarrhea (defined as 3 or more liquid stools per day, or 2 or more liquid stools on successive days). There were no significant differences in incidence of diarrhea or percentage of days of diarrhea between subjects who did and did not receive PHM. However, subjects who received PHM in their continuous feedings had a significantly higher number of gelatinous stools, and the combined PHM groups had a significantly lower number of liquid stools and a higher number of normal stools than did subjects who did not receive PHM. For the combined PHM groups, there was a 12% incidence of small-bore feeding tube occlusion--requiring replacement. CONCLUSIONS: Further study with a larger sample is necessary to evaluate trends found in this pilot study and to determine PHM efficacy for prevention of diarrhea. PHM administration may result in small-bore feeding tube obstruction, and thus requires adequate dilution and close monitoring.

E-mail: Dorothy-Belknap@uokhsc.edu 0147-9563/97/$5.00 + 0 211180823 HEART & LUNG VOL. 26, NO. 3 The cause of diarrhea that occurs after onset of enteral feeding remains unclear. 6,9"~° Lacking a clear-cut cause, preventive measures for diarrhea are initiated infrequently in this population, and management approaches are variable and inconsistent. One method for prevention and management of diarrhea in patients receiving enteral feeding may be the addition of dietary fiber.l 1-17 Dietary fiber modifies stool consistency d u e to its influence on the f e r m e n t a t i o n p r o c e s s by colonic bacteria and its water-holding abilties. 13-15 P u b l i s h e d s t u d i e s investigating the addition of fiber to enteral feeding and its effects on stool consistency have included the use of soy, pectin, and psyllium (or ispaghula). Although formulas with soy fiber are u s e d widely in clinical practice, studies of diarrhea in patients receiving 229 these formulas have shown mixed outcomes, lae3 Zimmaro et al.ll found that adding pectin to formula alleviated diarrhea that developed in normal subjects who first received fiber-free formula. Psyllium hydrophilic mucilloid (PHM)--or its less refined counterpart ispaghula--is best known for its bulking characteristics in the treatment of constipation. 24"27 However, reports from uncontrolled studies indicate psyllium is also an effective treatment for diarrhea of varying causes. 2a'32 Three empiric studies were found in which subjects received psyllium or isphagula as the fiber source during enteral feeding. For patients in a burn unit 33 and an intensive care unit (1CU), 34 consistency of stools was improved with the addition of psyllium. Hart and Dobb 2 found no significant difference in diarrhea scores (volume and consistency) between groups of ICU patients who did and did not receive ispaghula husk (Fybogel). However, patients in the Hart and Dobb 2 study received a lower dosage of fiber compared to those in the former studies. When hydrated, psyllium quickly forms a viscous gel Administration through a small-bore feeding tube is difficult and occasionally results in feeding tube occlusion. 35 Therefore nurses and physicians are reluctant to consider psyllium as a possible treatment for diarrhea in enterally fed patients. Although potentially difficult to administer, PHM is inexpensive and has few adverse effects. 31 If administration difficulties are overcome, PHM might be an effective prophylactic treatment for patients receiving enteral feeding. In a laboratory study, 36 we found that PHM could be given successfully when mixed in a feeding bag with full-strength formula at room temperature, and administered with an infusion pump for 1 to 10 hours. We found no other empiric studies or anecdotal reports that described feasibility of administering PHM through feeding tubes, or that compared methods of PHM delivery. PURPOSE OF THE STUDY This pilot study was undertaken to investigate the efficacy of PHM for prevention of diarrhea, and to determine the feasibility of administering psyllium through feeding tubes. METHOD Subjects. During a 2-year period, all inpatients at a Department of Veterans Affairs Medical Center (DVAMC) in the Midwest United States, who had a physician's order for a newly initiated enteral feeding to be administered through a nasogastric feeding tube or percutaneous endo* 230 scopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube, were screened for acceptance into the study. Male or female patients, 18 years or older, on both ICU and general medical-surgical units, were enrolled. Patients were excluded if they had preexisting diarrhea or conditions contributing to diarrhea (known bowel disease, gastric disease, active gastrointestinal bleeding), were receiving medication known to result in diarrhea (laxatives, lactulose), or were receiving formula through a jejunostomy tube. Procedure. Subjects were assigned to one of three groups. Subjects in treatment group A received 7 gm PHM twice-daily added to 240 to 300 ml of the formula and infused at the prescribed formula infusion rate. Subjects in treatment group B received 7 gm PHM twice-daily in 60 to 120 ml of formula, juice, or water as a bolus injection as quickly as possible by syringe through the feeding tube, followed by 50 ml of tap water to flush the tube. Subjects in group C (control group) received No PHM. Subjects received formula either on a continuous basis or on an intermittent feeding schedule (e.g., 240 to 480 ml formula over 30 minutes every 4 to 8 hours) as recommended by the consulting dietitian and prescribed by their resident physician. Random permuted blocks 37 of 20 were used for group assignment of subjects receiving continuous feeding, with assignment to group A twice as likely as assignment to group B or C. Subjects receiving intermittent feeding were assigned to groups B or C with use of random permuted blocks of 10. This combination was designed to achieve equal groups of 10 subjects for each 30 subjects enrolled. PHM (Reguloid; Konsyl-D) was administered according to the study protocol by the medication nurse through one of three types of feeding tubes ordered by the resident physician: an 18 Fr Argyle Salem Sump (Sherwood Medical), an 8 Fr Silk Enteral Feeding Tube (Corpak), or a PEG tube. Kangaroo infusion pumps and 1000 ml Pump Sets (Sherwood Medical) were used to administer all continuous feeding and some intermittent feeding. Based on the recommendation of the consulting dietitian, the resident physician prescribed one of four commercial formulas available at the DVAMC during the study period: Ensure, Ensure Plus, Osmolite (Ross Laboratories), or Isocal (Mead-Johnson). Clinical evaluation and data collection. Subjects were studied for the first 7 days they received enteral feeding. 5,3a Clinical evaluations performed at the onset of the study included assess- MAY/JUNE 1997 HEART & LUNG ment of nutritional status (ideal body weight, serum albumin, percentage of body fat), demographic data (age, sex), and medical diagnosis. Data regarding stools, PHM dosage, and formula were collected daily from a bedside data sheet, the medical and nursing record, and nurse or patient interview. Nursing staff were oriented to the study protocols through staff meetings and inservices before the study and periodically throughout the 2-year study period. Instruments and Measurement. The literature indicates wide variability in defining diarrhea. 38,39 For this study, we chose a conservative definition: three or more watery or liquid stools on any 1 day, or two or more watery or liquid stools on 2 or more consecutive days. Percentageof days with diarrhea was defined as the number of days on which diarrhea occurred divided by the total number of days monitored. Percentage of days accounts for multiple occurrences of diarrhea in the same patient and eliminates the influence of monitoring duration across studies. 39 Stool frequency was defined as the total number of stools recorded daily for 7 days. Stool consistency was rated according to a 3-point scale: (1) watery (thin as water) or liquid (thicker than water, easily flows as a liquid), (2) gelatinous: like a jelly, or (3) normal, which included pasty (like a paste, thicker than jelly), semiformed (contains some formed stool), or formed (molded stool, without unformed portions). The definitions for stool consistency were posted at the patient's bedside. Subject Assurances. This study was approved by the Health Sciences Center and DVAMC Institutional Review Boards. Informed consent was obtained from each subject or legal designee before enrollment. Statistical Analysis. SPSS (SPSS, Inc.) for Windows (Microsoft), Version 6.0 was used for statistical analysis. Data were analyzed with t test and Chi square. Alpha was set at 0.05 or less. A power analysis was calculated to address the difference in diarrhea incidence. 4° A sample of 40 subjects per group was needed to achieve a power of 0.80, with a moderate effect size 0.30 and an alpha of 0.05. Power denotations for actual study data were calculated using a computer software program. 41 RESULTS Sample Characteristics. Over the 2-year study period, 75 subjects were enrolled in the study. Fifteen subjects were dropped from the study due to discontinued feeding (I 2), death (1), obstructed feeding tube (1), and patient request (1). DISCUSSION When a stringent criterion--three or more liq~ uid stools per day or two or more liquid stools per day × 2 days--was used, the incidence of diarrhea was comparable to studies with a similar definition. 39 The higher percentage of subjects with diarrhea in the ICU as compared with the general units also is consistent with previous findings. 2 Although more subjects in the No~PHM group developed diarrhea, the administration of PHM did not make a significant difference, either for incidence or percentage of days with diarrhea or average stool consistency, in this sample of acutely ill patients. HEART & L U N G VOL. 26, NO. 3 There were significant differences in the pattern of liquid and normal stools found between the PHM and No-PHM groups, however. When the two PHM groups were combined, subjects in the PHM groups had significantly fewer liquid stools and a higher number of normal stools than sub~ jects who did not receive PHM. Over the 7-day period, stools from subjects who received PHM were progressively less liquid or watery and more gelatinous, pasty, semiformed, or formed ("normal") in consistency. In contrast, stools from subjects who did not receive PHM were progressively more liquid or watery. These findings are consistent with those of earlier studies. Heather et al., 34 who studied a similar sample of enterally fed patients over 6 days, found no difference in frequency of stools, but noted firmer consistency for subjects who received 10.2 gin/day of psyllium compared to those who did not. Frank and Green 33 reported that the addition of psyllium, 7 gm/L of formula for patients with burn injury changed stool consistency from liquid to soft~ formed "silicone putty." With the exception of gelatinous stools, receipt of PHM by either continuous or bolus method r e s u l t e d in similar o u t c o m e s . The r e a s o n for dif~ f e r e n c e s in gelatinous stools m a y b e b e c a u s e of p r o p e r t i e s of PHM given continuously o v e r a peri- 235 od of time versus bolus dosage. Most gelatinous stools (26 of 37 total gelatinous stools) were reported by ICU nurses, and most were reported for subjects receiving PHM with continuous feeding. In the ICUs, nurses tended to add PHM to a large amount of formula (600 to 1000 ml) and infuse it over a longer period of time. Subjects receiving PHM with their continuous feedings received a higher percentage (91%) of their scheduled PHM doses than the bolus PHM group (81%). Although not statistically significant (p = 0.08), this difference may be clinically important. The dosage of psyllium, 7 gm twice-daily, was based on recommendations of the manufacturer. It is possible that greater differences in the incidence of diarrhea may have occurred if the dose of psyllium had been higher. In the current study, psyllium administration difficulties occurred, resulting in small-bore feeding tube occlusion, although occlusion rates were consistent with those previously reported. 42-44 Fewer occlusions occurred when only half of the powder (5 cc) was mixed at a time (with 50 ml water), and the feeding tube was irrigated with 20 to 30 ml water before and after administration, and 5 to 10 ml of water between each half of the dose administration. Because psyllium tends to fall out of suspension and create a "sludge" in the bottom of the feeding bag, the bag requires shaking every 30 minutes, especially at slow infusion rates. LIMITATIONS Based on our findings of 21% and 30% diarrhea in the PHM and No-PHM groups (conservative definition of diarrhea and small effect size), we calculate at least 366 subjects in each group would be needed to provide power of 0.80. Although the original study plan (based on an estimated medium effect) called for 120 subjects to be recruited, it was not feasible to continue the study after 2 years. T h u s the data are presented as pilot data. The pattern of stool outcomes that emerged are favorable for PHM administration, but additional study with a larger sample size is required to demonstrate efficacy for prevention of diarrhea. An additional limitation of this pilot study was the number of nurses involved in data collection. We recognized the difficulty inherent in collection of clinical data that is not controlled by the investigator and attempted to overcome this limitation through careful daily follow-up with the staff. Although a systematic bias was not apparent, it is possible that underreporting of stools and over- 236 reporting of formula volume occurred. To minimize reporting error, we recommend recruitment and training of a smaller cadre of staff members for data collection. IMPLICATIONS RESEARCH FOR FURTHER In our experience, acutely ill patients requiring enteral feeding often develop fecal incontinence. As a result of maceration from moisture and chemical irritation of stool, 45"47 fecal incontinence of liquid stool is a significant risk factor for skin breakdown. 4a'~° Because PHM alters the consistency of stool, there may be a protective effect on the surrounding skin. Further study of this possible effect is warranted. A major p r o b l e m d e t e r m i n i n g efficacy of dietary fiber for improvement of stool consistency is the wide variability among fiber sources (composition, physiochemical, and fermentative properties) and among subject responses to the fiber source (variations in microflora and colonic mucosal integrity). 13,5~53 We recommend comparing efficacy of several different fiber types (i.e., psyllium, pectin, soy) in a cross-over design. SUMMARY Subjects who received PHM with their continuous enteral feeding had a significantly higher number of gelatinous stools, and subjects in both PHM groups had significantly fewer liquid stools and a higher number of normal stools than did subjects who received No PHM. However, there was not a significant difference in incidence of diarrhea, percentage of days with diarrhea, or stool consistency scores between subjects who did and did not receive PHM. Further study with a larger sample and equal group size is necessary to demonstrate PHM efficacy for prevention of diarrhea. PHM a d m i n i s t r a t i o n requires close monitoring and attention to administration technique; 12% of small-bore feeding tubes required replacement due to obstruction with PHM. title: Use of standard enteral formula versus enteric formula with prebiotic content in nutrition therapy: A randomized controlled study among neuro-critical care patients
abstract: OBJECTIVE: To compare use of standard enteral formula versus enteric formula with prebiotic content in terms of nutrition therapy related outcomes among neurocritical care patients. METHODS: A total of 46 adult neurocritical care patients who received nutrition therapy with standard enteral formula (SEF group; n = 23) or enteral formula with prebiotic content (EFPC group; n = 23) during their hospitalization in intensive care unit (ICU) were included in this prospective randomized controlled study. Data on patient demographics (age, gender), diagnosis, co-morbid diseases, anthropometrics, length of stay (LOS) in hospital and ICU, Nutritional Risk Screening (NRS-2002) score, and Acute Physiology and Chronic Health (APACHE-II) score were recorded at enrollment. Data on daily nutritional intake [total energy (kcal/day), carbohydrate (g/day), protein (g/day), lipid (g/day), FOS (g/day), enteral volume (ml/day), fluid in enteral product (ml/day) and fluid intake (ml/day)], achievement of target dose [total fluid intake in enteral product (ml)/20 h], laboratory findings (blood biochemistry and complete blood count), complications and drug treatments were recorded on Day 1, Day 4, Day 7, Day 14 and Day 21 of nutrition therapy in SEF and EFPC groups. RESULTS: Use of EFPC compared to SEF was associated with significantly higher total energy, carbohydrate, protein, lipid, enteral volume and fluid intake (p values ranged from <0.05 to <0.001) on each day of nutrition therapy. Target dose was achieved by majority of patients (86.9%) and at day 4 of nutrition therapy in most of patients (71.7%) in the overall study population. Patients in the EFPC group had a non-significant tendency for higher rate (95.7% vs. 78.3%) and earlier (87.0% vs. 56.5% on day 4) achievement of target dose, lower rate (8.7% vs. 56.5%) and faster amelioration (none vs. 52.2% were diarrheic on day 7) of diarrhea and lesser need for insulin (56.5% vs. 13.0%, p = 0.002). Nutrition therapy was associated with significant decrease in prealbumin (Day 14 vs. Day 1, p < 0.05 for both), albumin (Day 14 vs. day 1, p < 0.01 for SEF, p < 0.05 for PEF), hemoglobin (Day 14 and Day 21 vs. Day 1and Day 14 vs. Day 4, p < 0.001 for each for SEF, Day 7, Day 14 and Day 21 vs. Day 1, p < 0.01 for each for PEF) and hematocrit (Day 14 and Day 21 vs. Day 1, p < 0.001 for each for both) levels in both SEF and EFPC groups. CONCLUSIONS: In conclusion, our findings revealed achievement of target nutritional intake in majority of neurocritical care patients via nutrition therapy, whereas EFPC was associated with a non-significant tendency for more frequent and earlier achievement of target dose along with significantly lower rate and faster amelioration of diarrhea as compared with SEF group. Prealbumin and albumin levels remained below the normal range, whereas C reactive protein (CRP) and white blood cell (WBC) were over the normal range throughout the nutrition period in both groups, while creatinine and urea levels were higher in EFPC than in SEF group. Hence, our findings seem to emphasize the importance of avoiding protein debt in provision of nutrition therapy and the likelihood of deterioration of nutritional status in elderly neurocritical care patients despite provision of early enteral nutrition support due to complex and deleterious inflammatory and metabolic changes during critical illness.

Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction 2. Materials and methods Critically ill patients have an increased risk of developing hospital acquired malnutrition due to complex and deleterious effects of illness on protein and energy metabolism resulting in metabolic dysregulation, hyper-catabolic state and depletion of energy stores [1e4]. Provision of adequate and timely nutritional support to critically ill patients is therefore considered crucial [2,3], given that protein/ energy depletion in the critically ill has been associated with poor patient outcome nosocomial bloodstream infections and increased mortality as well as an increased intensive care unit (ICU) length of stay and cost increment [2,3,5e8]. Malnutrition incidence in stroke patients ranges from 6.1% to 62.0% depending on the method and timing of nutritional assessment [9,10], while dysphagia was evident in approximately 50% of patients in the early period [11e13]. Nutritional status has been suggested to be closely related to long term clinical outcome among stroke patients with an increased risk of poststroke infection, recurrent stroke and mortality in case of malnutrition [10,14,15]. Early provision of enteral nutrition via the gastrointestinal tract is considered the ﬁrst choice of nutritional support and a marker of quality of care for critically ill patients [16e20], as reported to be associated with protection of intestinal mucosal integrity, reduction of infection rates, morbidity and mortality [2,21e24]. However, provision of adequate nutritional support to critically ill patients still remains a challenge due to impact of underlying diagnosis and concomitant treatments on nutritional status, as well as difﬁculties in determining optimal caloric intake for a favorable clinical outcome and likelihood of failure to reach target intake alongside discrepancies between prescribed and provided nutritional intake in the setting of critical illness [20,25e28]. Fortiﬁcation of enteral formulas with prebiotics serves the purpose of improving adequacy of nutrition therapy allowing speciﬁc alterations in the composition and/or function of the gastrointestinal microbiota in favor of host well-being [29e31]. Prebiotic fructo-oligosaccharides (FOS) selectively stimulate the proliferation of biﬁdobacteria and potentially lactobacilli and provide a substrate for fermentation and short chain fatty acid (SCFA) production [29,32e35]. Although the effect of FOS on the colonic microbiota have been extensively addressed in healthy populations consuming a normal diet, few clinical studies have investigated their use in enteral formulas and indicated potential beneﬁt of FOS content to enable a stabilized intestinal barrier homeostasis and reduced infection rates [30,31,34e36]. The present study was therefore designed to evaluate provision of nutrition therapy via standard enteral formula (SEF) versus enteric formula with prebiotic content (EFPC) among neurocritical care patients in terms of achievement of target nutritional intake, complications and changes in blood biochemistry and hematologic parameters. 2.1. Study population A total of 46 adult neurocritical care patients who received nutrition therapy with standard enteral formula (SEF group; n ¼ 23, mean ± SD age: 71.8 ± 20.0 years, 60.9% were males) or enteral formula with prebiotic content (EFPC group, n ¼ 23, mean ± SD age: 73.9 ± 15.3 years, 60.9% were females) during their hospitalization in Ankara Numune Training and Research Hospital ICU between April 2014 and June 2015 were included in this prospective randomized controlled study. Of 68 patients initially enrolled, 22 patients were lost to follow up due to death (n ¼ 12), transfer from ICU to another ward (n ¼ 4) or hospital (n ¼ 3) and switching to oral feeding (n ¼ 3), and ﬁnal study population was composed of 46 patients (23 patients in each group) (Fig. 1). Patients were randomized into SEF (Osmolite®, 1 kcal/1 ml) and EFPC (Jevity®, 1 kcal/ 1 ml) groups by the ICU nurse based on admission hour with consideration of admissions occurred at even hours in the SEF group and those at odd hours in the EFPC group. Patients aged 18e80 years, hospitalized at ICU with a neurological diagnosis and considered appropriate for provision of enteral nutrition based on presence of a functioning gastro intestinal (GI) tract and access via the gastric or jejunal route and/or the inability or unwillingness to meet oral nutrition were included in the study. ICU patients with non-neurological diagnoses, intubated patients, patients with multiple organ failure, immunosuppressive conditions (Acquired Immune Deﬁciency Syndrome, chronic corticoid usage, immunosuppressive therapy), lung damage, extremity fractures, thoracic and intraabdominal injuries, obesity (BMI>40 kg/m2), cachexia (BMI<17 kg/m2), malignancy, insulin dependent diabetes, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, dialysis, liver dysfunction, cirrhosis, bilirubinemia (>3 mg/dl) past history (last 6 months) of chemotherapy or radiotherapy, previous transplantation, pregnancy and total parenteral nutrition were excluded from the study. Written informed consent was obtained from each subject or his/her relative following a detailed explanation of the objectives and protocol of the study which was conducted in accordance with the ethical principles stated in the “Declaration of Helsinki” and approved by the Ankara Numune Training and Research Hospital Ethics Committee (Date of Approval: 18/09/2013; Reference number/Protocol No: 34/2013). 2.2. Data collection Data on patient demographics (age, gender), underlying neurological diagnosis, co-morbid diseases, anthropometrics [body weight (kg), height (cm), body mass index (BMI; kg/m2)], LOS in hospital and ICU, Nutritional Risk Screening (NRS-2002) score, enteral feeding route (oral, nasogastric, nasodeudonal, gastrostomy, jejunostomy) and Acute Physiology and Chronic Health (APACHE-II) score were recorded in each patient at study enrollment. Data on daily nutritional intake [total energy (kcal/day), carbohydrate (g/day), protein (g/day), lipid (g/day), FOS (g/day), Downloaded for Anonymous User (n/a) at Tufts University from ClinicalKey.com by Elsevier on May 06, 2020. For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2020. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved. 28 P. Tuncay et al. / Clinical Nutrition ESPEN 25 (2018) 26e36 Fig. 1. Flow chart of the study “Disposition of patients”. enteral volume (ml/day), ﬂuid in enteral product (ml/day) and ﬂuid intake (ml/day)], achievement of target dose [total ﬂuid intake in enteral product (ml)/20 h], laboratory ﬁndings (blood biochemistry and complete blood count) and enteral feeding related complications were recorded on Day 1, Day 4, Day 7, Day 14 and Day 21 of nutrition therapy in SEF and EFPC groups. Data on drug treatment (anticoagulants, diuretics, bronchodilators, cortisone, insulin and antibiotics) were also recorded in study groups. 2.3. Outcome measures Comparison of SEF or EFPC groups in terms of daily nutritional intake and achievement of target dose under 21-day nutrition therapy was the primary outcome of the study, while analysis of laboratory ﬁndings, complications and drug treatments between SEF and EFPC groups was the secondary outcome. 2.4. Assessment of nutritional status NRS 2002 scoring system was used to detect the presence or risk of undernutrition in the hospital setting [37]. All NRS-2002 scores (ranged 0e7) were recorded for all patients within 24 h after admission based on assessments of the nutritional status (BMI <18.5, 18.5e20.5, and >20.5 kg/m2), weight loss history (over 5% in 3 months, over 5% in 2 months or over 5% in 1 month) and reduced food intake as a proportion in the preceding week (0%e25%, 25%e 50%, 50%e75% and >75%)], disease severity and age. Anthropometric measurements were performed by the same nurse and dietician for each patient using digital electronic scales to ensure interrater reliability, and based on statements of patients or relatives for immobile patients. Patients with a NRS-2002 score of 3 were considered as nutritionally at risk and considered to be appropriate to be included in the nutritional support program [37]. All patients received nutrition therapy via 20-h continuous infusion (initial load of 10 ml/h increased 10 ml per 8 h to reach 20 h/day) with 4-h interval (from 24:00 p.m to 04:00 a.m) per day. 2.5. Energy, ﬂuid and macronutrient requirements and target dose Total energy requirement was calculated based on basal metabolic rate (for gender and age using Schoﬁeld formula) þ stress factor (20% for ICU hospitalization) þ activity factor (10% increase for immobility) þ thermal factor (10% increase for each unit over 37  C) þ respiratory support (15% decrease) þ thermic effect of food (10%). Protein and ﬂuid requirement were considered to be 1 g/kg/ day and 30e40 ml/kg/day, respectively. Target dose was considered to be the maximum dose to be achieved in accordance with energy and protein requirements calculated on a daily basis, and formulated as “Enteral target dose ¼ daily enteral intake (mL)/duration of nutrition (hour/day). 2.6. Blood analysis Blood biochemistry including blood glucose (BG), prealbumin, C-reactive protein (CRP), urea, creatinine, albumin, total protein, alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), gamma glutamine transferase (GGT), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), total bilirubin, direct bilirubin, total calcium, direct calcium, sodium and potassium levels were analyzed using Beckman Coulter AU5800 device with colorimetric method for albumin, total protein, total and direct bilirubin, with kinetic method for urea, ALT, AST, GGT, LDH and ALP, with hexokinase method for glucose and creatinine via and with enzymatic method for CRP. Complete blood count analysis for hemoglobin, hematocrit, platelet, white blood cell (WBC), red blood cell (RBC), mean corpuscular volume (MCV), mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC), RDW red cell distribution width (RDW), mean platelet volume (MPV), platelet distribution width (PDW) was performed with Beckman Coulter HMX device using impedance or photometric methods and associated calculations. Arterial blood gas analysis (pH, pO2, pCO2, HCO3, SO2) was also performed. 2.7. Statistical analysis At least 68 patients (34 patients in each group) were calculated to be included via sample size estimation based on a power of 80% at a type I error of 0.05, whereas due to failure to complete 21-day follow up by 22 patients due to death (n ¼ 12), transfer from ICU to another ward (n ¼ 4) or hospital (n ¼ 3) and switching to oral feeding (n ¼ 3), ﬁnal study population subjected to analysis was composed of 46 patients (23 patients in each group). Statistical analysis was made using the computer software Statistical Package for Social Sciences (IBM SPSS Statistics for Windows, version 22.0. released 2013, IBM Corp., Armonk, New York, USA). Fisher's exact test and Chi-square analysis were performed for categorical variables. Change over time is evaluated by dependent group t test or Wilcoxon test depending on distrusting pattern of continuous variables and by McNemar test for categorical Downloaded for Anonymous User (n/a) at Tufts University from ClinicalKey.com by Elsevier on May 06, 2020. For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2020. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved. P. Tuncay et al. / Clinical Nutrition ESPEN 25 (2018) 26e36 29 variables. Time-variations in laboratory parameters were evaluated via repeated measures t test, and post Hoc LSD for normally distributed variables, while Friedman test and Bonferroni corrected Wilcoxon test were used for not normally distributed variables. Correlation analysis was performed using Pearson analysis and Spearman's analysis for normally and not normally distributed variables, respectively. Data were expressed as “mean ± standard deviation (SD)” and “n (%)” values, where appropriate. p < 0.05 was considered statistically signiﬁcant. 3.3. Target dose achievement, complications and drug treatment 3. Results Table 1 Baseline characteristics in study groups. NRS-2002 scores were 3 in all patients in our cohort. Overall, target dose was achieved by majority of patients (86.9%) and on Day 4 (71.7%) of nutrition therapy in most of patients. Albeit not signiﬁcant, a tendency for higher rate (95.7% vs. 78.3%) and earlier (87.0% vs. 56.5% on Day 4) achievement of target dose was noted in EFPC as compared with SEF group. Target dose was achieved on Day Total (n ¼ 46) 3.1. Baseline characteristics in study groups Overall 50.0% of patients aged 80 years (47.8% of patients in the SEF and 52.2% of patients in EFPC groups) and 23.9% were in the 68e79 years age group (21.7% of patients in the SEF and 26.0% of patients in EFPC groups). Males composed 50.0% of total study population, 60.9% of patients in the SEF group and 39.1% of patients in the EFPC group (Table 1). Stroke was the leading neurological diagnosis in the study population (58.7%) and in SEF (60.9%) and EFPC (56.5%) groups, while hypertension was the most common co-morbid disease (26.1%) overall and in 26.9% and 20.9% of patients in SEF and EFPC groups, respectively (Table 1). No signiﬁcant difference was noted between SEF and EFPC groups in terms of gender, age, prevalence of diabetes, BMI, LOS in hospital or ICU (21e50 days in most of patients), route of enteral feeding (nasofeeding in majority of patients) and APACHE-II scores (10 in majority of patients) (Table 1). 3.2. Daily nutritional intake in study groups In the SEF group, the amount of total energy (from 297.4 ± 79.8 kcal/day to 1470.4 ± 600.7 kcal/day), carbohydrate (from 42.4 ± 11.4 g/day to 209.4 ± 85.5 g/day), protein (from 11.9 ± 3.2 g/day to 58.8 ± 24.0 g/day), lipid (from 10.1 ± 2.7 g/day to 49.9 ± 20.4 g/day), enteral volume (from 297.4 ± 79.8 ml/day to 1470.4 ± 600.7 ml/day), ﬂuid in enteral product (from 252.9 ± 67.9 ml/day to 1249.9 ± 510.6 ml/day) and ﬂuid (from 297.49 ± 79.8 ml/day to 728.3 ± 109.6 ml/day) intake was signiﬁcantly increased from baseline to Day 21 of nutrition therapy (p < 0.001 for each) (Table 2, Supplementary Table I). In the EFPC group, the amount of total energy (from 353.0 ± 33.4 kcal/day to 1845.2 ± 342.1 kcal/day), carbohydrate (from 57.4 ± 5.4 g/day to 300.0 ± 55.6 g/day), protein (from 14.1 ± 1.3 g/day to 73.8 ± 13.7 g/day), lipid (from 12.0 ± 1.1 g/day to 62.7 ± 11.6 g/day), FOS (from 2.5 ± 0.2 g/day to 12.9 ± 2.4 g/day), enteral volume (from 294.4 ± 27.8 ml/day to 1538.9 ± 285.4 ml/ day), ﬂuid in enteral product (from 353.0 ± 33.4 ml/day to 821.7 ± 25.3 ml/day) and ﬂuid (from 353.0 ± 33.4 ml/day to 1845.2 ± 342.1 ml/day) intake was signiﬁcantly increased from baseline to Day 21 of nutrition therapy (p < 0.001) (Table 2, Supplementary Table I). Comparison of nutritional intake in SEF vs. EFPC groups on Day 1 and Day 21 of nutrition therapy revealed signiﬁcantly higher amount of total energy (p ¼ 0.004 and p ¼ 0.014, respectively), carbohydrate (p < 0.001 for each), protein (p ¼ 0.005 and p ¼ 0.014, respectively), lipid (p ¼ 0.004 and p ¼ 0.014, respectively), enteral volume (p ¼ 0.004 and p ¼ 0.014, respectively) and ﬂuid intake (p ¼ 0.004 and p ¼ 0.001, respectively) in EFPC than in SEF group, whereas amount of ﬂuid intake in enteral product was signiﬁcantly higher on Day 1 (p ¼ 0.011) but lower on Day 21 (p ¼ 0.024) in EFPC than in SEF (Table 2, Supplementary Table I). Patients in the SEF group compared to those in the EFPC group had signiﬁcantly higher rate of enteral feeding related complications (56.5% vs. 13.0%, p ¼ 0.002), including diarrhea (56.5% vs. 8.7%). None of the patients were diarrheic after Day 4 in EFPC group, whereas 12 (52.2%), 11 (47.8%) and 9 (39.1%) patients in SEF group were still diarrheic on Day 7, Day 14 and Day 21 of clinical nutrition, respectively (Table 3). Patients in SEF and EFPC groups had similar rates for anticoagulant, diuretic, bronchodilator and cortisone usage, whereas insulin treatment was signiﬁcantly more common in SEF group (56.5% vs. 13.0%, p ¼ 0.002) (Table 3). In both SEF (from 52.2% to 95.7%, p ¼ 0.003) and EFPC (from 43.5 to 100.0%, p ¼ 0.002) groups a signiﬁcant increase was noted in antibiotic usage during study period when compared to the period prior to study enrollment, while no signiﬁcant difference was noted between pre-study and during study periods in terms of number of antibiotic regimens and duration of therapy (Table 3). 3.4. Laboratory analysis ﬁndings in study groups In the SEF group, nutrition therapy was associated with signiﬁcant decrease in creatinine (from 1.00 ± 0.44 mg/dl to 0.77 ± 0.37 mg/dl, p < 0.001), hemoglobin (from 11.90 ± 2.29 g/dl to 10.44 ± 1.13 g/dl, p < 0.001), hematocrit (from 52.23 ± 77.66 to 30.98 ± 4.06, p < 0.001), RBC (from 4.13 ± 0.80 10^6/mL to 3.94 ± 1.7010^6/mL, p < 0.001) and MPV (from 9.42 ± 1.00 fL to 8.99 ± 1.35 fL, p < 0.05) levels, whereas signiﬁcant increase in ALT (from 31.69 ± 26.94 U/L to 46.47 ± 44.85 U/L, p < 0.05) and platelet (from 206.52 ± 65.74 10^3/mL to 308.65 ± 153.98 10^3/mL, p < 0.01) levels from baseline to Day 21 (Table 4, Supplementary Table II). In the EFPC group, nutrition therapy was associated with signiﬁcant decrease in BG (from 138.73 ± 29.48 mg/dl to 111.69 ± 25.71 mg/dl, p < 0.01), CRP (from 106.39 ± 84.24 mg/dl to 61.17 ± 37.87 mg/dl, p < 0.01), hemoglobin (from 11.43 ± 2.13 g/dl to 9.93 ± 1.33 g/dl, p < 0.01), hematocrit (from 34.23 ± 6.66% to 29.92 ± 4.11% p < 0.01) and RBC (from 3.87 ± 0.72 10^6/mL to 3.38 ± 0.49 10^6/mL, p < 0.01) levels, whereas signiﬁcant increase in pH (from 7.43 ± 0.06 to 7.48 ± 0.04, p < 0.01) from baseline to Day 21 (Table 4, Supplementary Table II). Patients in the EFPC group compared to those in SEF group, had signiﬁcantly lower levels for CRP (61.17 ± 37.87 mg/dl vs. 106.60 ± 53.31 mg/dl, p ¼ 0.006) and HCO3 (14.49 ± 4.54 vs. 28.81 ± 5.19, p ¼ 0.004) and signiﬁcantly higher levels of creatinine (1.23 ± 0.84 mg/dl vs. 0.77 ± 0.37 mg/dl, p ¼ 0.023) and sodium (140.39 ± 8.64 mEq/L vs. 136.17 ± 4.76 mEq/L, p ¼ 0.048) on Day 21 of nutrition therapy, and signiﬁcantly higher levels of urea (85.21 ± 56.31 mg/dl vs. 53.43 ± 26.63 mg/dl, p ¼ 0.002) on Day 1 (Table 4, Supplementary Table II). 3.5. Correlations of FOS intake with certain parameters in EFPC group FOS intake was not correlated with prealbumin, CRP, WBC, APACHE-II score and LOS in hospital or ICU in the EFPC group (Table 5). 4. Discussion Our ﬁndings revealed achievement of target nutritional intake in majority of neurology patients who received either SEF or EFPC during their hospitalization in ICU, while a tendency for higher and earlier achievement of target intake was noted in patients who received EFPC compared to those treated with SEF. Use of EFPC was also associated with signiﬁcantly lower rate and faster amelioration of diarrhea, higher reduction in BG and lesser need for insulin therapy when compared to SEF-based nutrition therapy. EFPC as compared with SEF resulted in higher total energy, carbohydrate, protein, lipid, enteral volume and ﬂuid intake during the nutrition days. Signiﬁcant decrease in hemoglobin, RBC and hematocrit levels during nutrition therapy was evident for both EFPC and SEF groups, while EFPC was associated with higher levels creatinine and sodium and lower levels of CRP as compared with SEF. Nasofeeding was the most common route of enteral nutrition as applied via 20-h continuous infusion with 4-h intervals per day in our cohort of newly diagnosed neurocritical care patients. This is in agreement with use of nasogastric feeding in majority of patients with stroke for the enteral nutrition support within the ﬁrst week of event in clinical practice [11e13]. Also, continuous infusion with 4e6 intervals rather than an intermittent infusion is considered to be a more appropriate scheme for nutrition therapy in critically ill patients, particularly in terms of lower complication rates such as diarrhea [11e13]. The European Society for Clinical Nutrition and Metabolism (ESPEN) recommends implementation of nasofeeding in stroke patients as earliest as possible with consideration of percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) when dysphagia is likely to persist for more than 3-weeks [17,38]. Although enteral nutrition is the preferred choice of nutrition therapy in ICU patients, failure to reach a timely target intake (in 50e70% of patients, even after the ﬁrst week) as well as discrepancy between prescribed and delivered enteral nutrition (10e40% less intake than prescribed) have frequently been reported among critically ill patients [8,16,39e46]. Hence, if enteral nutrition cannot be commenced or fails to provide adequate feeding, initiation of supplemental PN is recommended as a strategy for preventing Downloaded for Anonymous User (n/a) at Tufts University from ClinicalKey.com by Elsevier on May 06, 2020. Achievement of target intake by 86.9% of newly diagnosed neurology ICU patients in our cohort and on Day 4 of nutrition therapy in 71.7% seems to emphasize adequacy of enteral nutrition provided in the early critical care period in neurology patients. Notably, initial diagnosis was considered amongst the risk factors predicting the discrepancy between prescribed and received enteral nutrition with lower risk of inadequate enteral nutrition intake reported in neurology patients as compared with cardiology and ICU patients [48]. Our ﬁndings seem to indicate PEF-based nutrition therapy to be more advantageous than use of SEF in terms of enabling target dose achievement in a higher percentage of patients and at an earlier period. Accordingly, while target dose was achieved by majority of patients on Day 4 (87.0%) of nutrition therapy and by all patients on Day 7 in the EFPC group, only 56.5% of patients in the SEF group achieved target dose on Day 4, while 13% had target intake on Day 21 of nutrition therapy. Enteral feeding has been associated with development of diarrhea in 8e41% of patients, while neurology patients were considered to have a higher risk of developing diarrhea than patients hospitalized with other diagnoses [49e52]. Notably, EFPC was associated with signiﬁcantly lower rate of diarrhea development (8.7% vs. 56.3%) as well as faster recovery of bowel function in diarrheic patients in our cohort of neurocritical care patients. Similarly, higher rate of diarrhea with SEF (30.3%) compared to EFPC (10.8%) was also reported in past study among elderly patients who received enteral nutrition [53]. In the acute setting, diarrhea is considered to be the most obvious gastrointestinal complication of enteral feeding [54e56] that contributes signiﬁcantly to inadequacy between EN prescription and intake due to losses in water, electrolytes and nutrients, regardless of the condition triggering its onset including enteral nutrition delivery system, gastrointestinal tolerance, or the use of diarrhea-inducing drugs such as antibiotics [56,57]. Accordingly, higher rate and longer course of diarrhea in the SEF group seems to be associated with lower amount of total energy, carbohydrate, protein, lipid, enteral volume and ﬂuid intake in this group during nutrition days, and thus lesser and later target dose achievement as compared to EFPC group. Production of SCFA by microbial fermentation of non-digestible carbohydrates and the growth of saccharolytic microbiota (such as biﬁdobacteria and lactobacilli) are the key mechanisms used by gastrointestinal microbiota to reverse abnormal water secretion in enteral nutrition and to prevent colonization of pathogenic microbiota, respectively [30,58e60]. FOS are naturally occurring indigestible carbohydrates (nondigestible polymers of fructose) fermented in the colon into SCFA [30,61], while use of 10 g FOS per day is considered sufﬁcient to stimulate growth of biﬁdobacteria in healthy subjects [34,62,63]. Enteral nutrition with FOS-enriched formula was also associated with increased growth of biﬁdobacteria [61] and total SCFA concentrations [64] in patients under long-term enteral nutrition. This seems notable given the lower fecal biﬁdobacteria reported in patients who develop diarrhea during enteral nutrition [65]. Concomitant antibiotic treatment was reported to be associated with higher incidence of diarrhea during enteral nutrition [30,66,67]. In our cohort, use of EFPC resulted in lower incidence and faster recovery of diarrhea, despite higher antibiotic usage with multiplicity of prescribed regimens as compared to SEF in this group. This seems to indicate potential therapeutic efﬁcacy of FOS in neurocritical care patients on antibiotic medication, despite the possibility suggested for SCFA-mediated or biﬁdogenic effect of FOS to be masked by antibiotics [30,56,68,69]. Nonetheless it should be noted that low concentrations of fecal biﬁdobacteria were identiﬁed in adult patients despite receiving FOS/ﬁbre-enriched enteral formulas for at least 12 days, along with no correlation of FOS intake with concentration of any fecal microbiota or use of antibiotics [30]. Also, using larger doses of FOS in the formulas delivered to ICU patients was considered not advisable in a past study among ICU patients receiving enteral nutrition, since additional (7 g/day) FOS intake was associated with no signiﬁcant change in SCFA and fecal output following 7e14 days of nutrition, despite signiﬁcant reduction observed in concentrations of Biﬁdobacterium [65]. In both SEF and EFPC groups, prealbumin levels were below the normal range (15e36 mg/dl) throughout the nutrition days in our cohort of neurocritical care patients. Although serum prealbumin has been widely used in assessing adequacy of nutrition support, based on its short half-life, change in prealbumin levels should be carefully interpreted in the hospitalized patient, especially in the critically ill population since it is a negative acute-phase protein synthesis of which decreases also with inﬂammation [70,71]. CRP is a marker for inﬂammation often used in conjunction with prealbumin to assess whether the changes in prealbumin indicate adequate nutrition support or changes in inﬂammation [70]. Notably, contradicting the role of prealbumin as a good marker for monitoring the adequacy of nutrition support, no correlation was reported between change in prealbumin levels and calorie or protein intake delivered via enteral nutrition in a past study, [71]. Alongside demonstration of a negative correlation between CRP and prealbumin levels, authors also indicated that change in CRP was the signiﬁcant predictor of change in prealbumin levels, suggesting that a change in inﬂammatory status, rather than nutrient intake, was responsible for the changes in prealbumin levels [71]. Likewise, inﬂammation rather than calorie or protein intake was shown to have a stronger effect on changes in prealbumin in another study, suggesting strong inﬂuence of inﬂammation of prealbumin and contradicting its use as a marker for in-depth nutrition assessment [72]. 33 In our cohort, while use of EFPC was associated with signiﬁcant decrease in CRP levels during the course of nutrition therapy as well as signiﬁcantly lower CRP levels as compared with SEF, serum CRP levels remained over the normal range (<3 mg/dl) in both SEF and EFPC groups throughout the nutrition therapy period. While increase in prealbumin levels is expected with the delivery of adequate calories and protein during the course of nutrition support [71,73], prealbumin levels remained below the normal range during enteral nutrition in our cohort despite the fact that target dose was achieved by 71.7% of our patients on Day 4 of nutrition therapy. Hence, given that CRP levels were above the normal ranges throughout the nutrition therapy period in both SEF and EFPC groups, decrease in prealbumin seems to be associated with increase in inﬂammatory process rather than inadequate calorie delivery in our cohort. This seems to support consideration of change in prealbumin to be a more reliable marker of changes in inﬂammatory rather than adequate nutrient delivery, particularly in critically ill patients [71]. Although FOS content in enteral formula was reported to be associated with signiﬁcant increase in prealbumin levels in acute stroke patients [74], FOS content was not found to be correlated with prealbumin levels as well as with hemoglobin and WBC count, APACHE-II scores and LOS in hospital or ICU in our cohort. SEF was reported to be associated with lower serum levels for serum albumin than EFPC in a past study in hospitalized patients receiving enteral nutrition [30]. Similarly, serum albumin levels were below the normal range (3.5e5.5 mg/dl) throughout the entire study in SEF group and albeit not signiﬁcant, a tendency for higher albumin levels was noted in EFPC than SEF group. Along with signiﬁcantly lower serum creatinine levels in SEF group than in EFPC group, this seems to emphasize the inappropriate amount of protein intake in SEF group in accordance with need for modiﬁcations in enteral nutrition dose until the amelioration of diarrhea. Nonetheless, given the signiﬁcantly higher levels for creatinine and urea in EFPC than in SEF group in favor of more severe protein catabolism, our ﬁndings emphasize the increase in protein requirements during critical illness, and the difﬁculty of preventing protein catabolism and the resultant negative nitrogen balance even with appropriate provision (1.2 g/kg/day to 2.0 g/kg/day) in ICU patients [16]. This seems notable given the correlation between negative energy balance and increased complications including sepsis and need for antibiotics in ICU patients [6,24]. Older age, lower caloric intake, and negative nitrogen balance were shown to be associated with increased risk of hospitalacquired infections in critically ill neurology patients under nutrition therapy [75]. Accordingly, alongside an increased antibiotic prescription rate during study period, hemoglobin, hematocrit and RBC levels were below the normal range and WBC counts were above the normal range throughout the nutrition days in both SEF and EFPC groups in our cohort, emphasizing the risk of anemia underlined by nutritional deﬁciency and infection in elderly neurocritical care patients despite provision of timely nutrition therapy. Aside from lower CRP and higher creatinine and urea levels in EFPC than in SEF group, no signiﬁcant difference was noted in other biochemical parameters and none of the hematologic parameters between EFPC and SEF groups during nutrition therapy period in our cohort. This seems consistent with similar hemoglobin, hematocrit, platelet, WBC, RBC, MCV, MCH and MCHC reported in 14day nutrition therapy implemented via SEF vs. EFPC in past study among acute stroke patients [76]. Tuncay et al. / Clinical Nutrition ESPEN 25 (2018) 26e36 a signiﬁcant decrease was noted in BG in EFPC group, alongside a lesser need for insulin therapy as compared with patients in SEF group. Signiﬁcant reduction in BG levels in EFPC group seems consistent with consideration of FOS with improved glycemic and metabolic control acting as an indigestible carbohydrate [77]. This also emphasize the association of recommended BG target of 140e180 mg/dl with lower mortality risk in critically ill ICU patients [78,79]. Deterioration of nutritional status in neurology ICU patients despite provision of early enteral nutrition support is considered likely given the complex and deleterious physiological changes during critical illness leading to variations in pro-inﬂammatory and anti-inﬂammatory components of the acute phase response and metabolic dysregulation including protein catabolism, negative nitrogen balance, and hyperglycemia [10,80]. Accordingly, while needs to be justiﬁed by more extensive research, current data suggest providing high protein intake and avoiding protein debt to be a priority, independently of caloric delivery in ICU care to improve prognosis [10,81,82]. Notably, in critically ill patients introduction of a new enteral feed with a higher protein to calorie ratio (Jevity Plus, Abbott) alongside completion of a range of enhancements in nutrition management was shown to be associated with likelihood of improved adequacy of energy (from 71% to 82%) and protein (from 65% to 75%) intake [83]. Our study was limited mainly by issues inherent in RCT methodology. First the method of selective enrollment, while minimizes the presence of confounding factors, leads to a trial population that differs from the general population. Also, potential lack of generalizability seems another important limitation due to relatively small sample size, which should be broader given the diversity of the critical patients. However since the study was planned to be completed within a 1-year period, and based on follow up of a 21day nutritional intervention in the ICU setting, high rate of lost to follow up patients within a speciﬁed time period become an inevitable outcome, mostly due to death or transfer to another unit. Second a monitoring bias is likely linked to the higher frequency of assessments and visits, compared to real life clinical practice. Third, due to strict design and time frame, limited possibility is evident to investigate the complex treatments common in real life or to fully detect side effects or complications and to provide reliable information on long-term treatment [84]. Fourth lack of data on ﬁbrinolytic treatment and stool culture analysis is another limitation which otherwise would extend the knowledge achieved in the current study regarding the tolerance to enteral nutrition and the impact of prebiotic enriched enteral nutrition on colon motility, respectively in neurocritical patients. In conclusion, our ﬁndings revealed achievement of target nutritional intake in majority of neurocritical care patients with SEF or EFPC based nutrition therapy, whereas EFPC was associated with a non-signiﬁcant tendency for more frequent and earlier achievement of target dose along with signiﬁcantly lower rate and faster amelioration of diarrhea despite concomitant antibiotic therapy and more marked BG lowering effect and lesser need for insulin as compared with SEF group. Prealbumin levels remained below the normal range despite achievement of target intake by majority of patients. This seems to be related to increased inﬂammatory process given that CRP and WBC levels remained over the normal range throughout the nutrition period in both groups. Nonetheless, not only prealbumin but also albumin, hemoglobin, RBC and hematocrit levels remained below normal range on nutrition days along with further decrease during the nutrition therapy in both EFPC and SEF groups, along with signiﬁcantly higher levels of urea and creatinine in EFPC group despite better target achievement. In this regard, our ﬁndings emphasize the importance of particularly avoiding protein debt and likelihood of deterioration of nutritional status in elderly neurocritical care patients despite provision of early enteral nutrition support due to complex and deleterious inﬂammatory and metabolic changes during critical illness leading to protein catabolism, negative nitrogen balance, metabolic dysregulation and susceptibility to infections. Our ﬁndings favor recommendation of EFPC in neurocritical patients, particularly for those with diarrhea and/or antibiotic therapy. Nonetheless, the potential therapeutic efﬁcacy of EFPC in neurocritical patients should be further addressed in larger scale studies based on a longer-term follow up of nutritional outcome as well as stool culture analysis of the impact of prebiotic enriched enteral nutrition on colon motility. title: Benefits of a synbiotic formula (Synbiotic 2000 Forte) in critically Ill trauma patients: early results of a randomized controlled trial
abstract: Background: Since probiotics are considered to exert beneficial health effects by enhancing the host's immune response, we investigated the benefits of a synbiotics treatment on the rate of infections, systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS), severe sepsis, and mortality in critically ill, mechanically ventilated, multiple trauma patients. Length of stay in the intensive care unit (ICU) and number of days under mechanical ventilation were also evaluated. Method: Sixty-five patients were randomized to receive once daily for 15 days a synbiotic formula (Synbiotic 2000Forte, Medipharm, Sweden) or maltodextrin as placebo. The synbiotic preparation consisted of a combination of four probiotics (10(11) CFU each): Pediococcus pentosaceus 5-33:3, Leuconostoc mesenteroides 32-77:1, L. paracasei ssp. paracasei 19; and L. plantarum 2,362; and inulin, oat bran, pectin, and resistant starch as prebiotics. Infections, septic complications, mortality, days under ventilatory support, and days of stay in ICU were recorded. Results: Synbiotic-treated patients exhibited a significantly reduced rate of infections (P = 0.01), SIRS, severe sepsis (P = 0.02), and mortality. Days of stay in the ICU (P = 0.01) and days under mechanical ventilation were also significantly reduced in relation to placebo (P = 0.001). Conclusion: The administration of this synbiotic formula in critically ill, mechanically ventilated, multiple trauma patients seems to exert beneficial effects in respect to infection and sepsis rates and to improve the patient's response, thus reducing the duration of ventilatory support and intensive care treatment.

full_text:  2006 by the Société Internationale de Chirurgie Published Online: 7 September 2006 World J Surg (2006) 30: 1848–1855 DOI: 10.1007/s00268-005-0653-1 Benefits of a Synbiotic Formula (Synbiotic 2000Forte) in Critically Ill Trauma Patients: Early Results of a Randomized Controlled Trial Katerina Kotzampassi, MD,1 Evagellos J. Giamarellos-Bourboulis,2 Antonios Voudouris,1 Pantelis Kazamias,3 Efthimios Eleftheriadis1 1 Department of Surgery, Faculty of Medicine, University of Thessaloniki, Thessaloniki, Greece Department of Internal Medicine, Faculty of Medicine, University of Athens, Athens, Greece 3 ICU of 424th Military Hospital, Thessaloniki, Greece 2 Abstract Background: Since probiotics are considered to exert beneficial health effects by enhancing the hosts immune response, we investigated the benefits of a synbiotics treatment on the rate of infections, systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS), severe sepsis, and mortality in critically ill, mechanically ventilated, multiple trauma patients. Length of stay in the intensive care unit (ICU) and number of days under mechanical ventilation were also evaluated. Method: Sixty-five patients were randomized to receive once daily for 15 days a synbiotic formula (Synbiotic 2000Forte, Medipharm, Sweden) or maltodextrin as placebo. The synbiotic preparation consisted of a combination of four probiotics (1011 CFU each): Pediococcus pentosaceus 5–33:3, Leuconostoc mesenteroides 32–77:1, L. paracasei ssp. paracasei 19; and L. plantarum 2,362; and inulin, oat bran, pectin, and resistant starch as prebiotics. Infections, septic complications, mortality, days under ventilatory support, and days of stay in ICU were recorded. Results: Synbiotic-treated patients exhibited a significantly reduced rate of infections (P = 0.01), SIRS, severe sepsis (P = 0.02), and mortality. Days of stay in the ICU (P = 0.01) and days under mechanical ventilation were also significantly reduced in relation to placebo (P = 0.001). Conclusion: The administration of this synbiotic formula in critically ill, mechanically ventilated, multiple trauma patients seems to exert beneficial effects in respect to infection and sepsis rates and to improve the patients response, thus reducing the duration of ventilatory support and intensive care treatment. C ritically ill trauma patients are at increased risk for the development of severe and even fatal infections despite advanced therapeutic measures and meticulous monitoring. This is the result of multiple factors, including the increased patients catabolism, Correspondence to: Katerina Kotzampassi, MD, Department of Surgery, Faculty of Medicine, University of Thessaloniki, Thessaloniki, Greece, e-mail: kakothe@yahoo.com decreased immune function, and gut-barrier malfunction. Additionally, the critical illness itself is coupled with prolonged use of mechanical ventilatory support as well as of other invasive procedures and devices; thus, the patient is subjected to an increased exposure to potentially pathogenic gram negative microorganisms, either from the hospital environment or, mainly, from his or her own microflora.1,2,3 In recent years, the importance of the intestinal microflora composition in physiological and pathophysiological Kotzampassi et al.: Synbiotics in Critically Ill processes in the human gastrointestinal tract has become ever more evident, and an ongoing number of trials dealing with the beneficial effects of probiotics—in particular, specific lactobacilli and bifidobacteria—given to manipulate the composition of gastrointestinal microflora have been published. The effects of probiotics are attributed to the enhancement of the immune response to pathogens, restoration to normal of increased intestinal permeability, balanced gut microecology, improved immunological gut barrier function, and downregulation of proinflammatory cytokines.4 They have been used extensively in many clinical situations, such as patients with acute pancreatitis, liver transplantation, antibiotic associated diarrhea, inflammatory bowel disease, and pouchitis.5,6,7 The experience, however, obtained from treatment in critically ill patients, and especially of those after multiple trauma—in whom the acute neurohormonal and inflammatory cascades and immediate immune responses are already over—is still limited. Thus, we proposed to investigate the benefits of treatment with a combination of pre- and probiotics regimens in critically ill, long-term mechanically ventilated patients after severe polytrauma. The end points of the study were infection rate, duration of stay under ventilatory support and in the ICU, and mortality. Proinflammatory indexes were supplementarily used for comparative evaluation of patients improvement. MATERIAL AND METHODS Patients All severe multiple trauma victims admitted over a 12month period to any 1 of the 5 participating surgical ICUs of the Thessaloniki Universitys tertiary-care AHEPA Hospitals and the affiliated 424th Military Hospital were eligible for the study. Patients were enrolled if they had two or more organ-system traumas, if it was predicted that they would have a long ICU stay and would need to be mechanically ventilated or under ventilatory support, if they were older than 18 years of age and with a life expectancy of more than 15 days after randomization. Pregnancy or lactation, immunosuppression, obesity (BMI > 40), or any contraindication to enteral administration of medication were additional causes for exclusion of patients from the study. A sample-size calculation based on data previously collected by our group demonstrated that approximately 102 patients would be required to demonstrate a 30% reduction in overall infections at the 5% significance level 1849 with a power of 80%. An intermediate analysis was performed after recruiting 60% of patients, the results of which are presented herein. The study was approved by the University of Thessaloniki Research Ethics Committee. Informed consent was obtained from all patients first-degree relatives on entry into the study. Randomization and Treatment Regimens This study was a double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. Patients were randomly assigned to receive synbiotic or placebo treatment according to a list blinded to study investigators and physicians in charge. The synbiotic preparation (Synbiotic 2000Forte, Medipharm, Sweden) consisted of a combination of 1011 CFU of each of four probiotics; Pediococcus pentoseceus 5–33:3, Leuconostoc mesenteroides 32–77:1, L. paracasei ssp 19, and L. plantarum 2362, as well as 2.5 g each of inulin, oat bran, pectin, and resistant starch. It was given in doses of 12 g (1 sachet) per day for a 15-day study period, diluted in 100 ml of tap water. The placebo preparation consisted of identical doses of powdered glucose polymer (maltodextrin, Caloreen, Nestle, UK). The two groups did not differ with respect to age, gender, underlying disease comorbidities, severity of trauma on ICU admission, Acute Physiology and Chronic Health Evaluation II (APACHE II) score, Glasgow Coma Scale, and route of nutrition (Table 1). All patients had at least one central venous catheter inserted and an arterial line. Medication was administered via endoscopic gastrostomy or through a nasogastric tube by ICU nursing staff. All received concomitant therapy with antibiotics, inotropes, and enteral or parenteral nutrition, as considered appropriate by the attending clinician. Proton pump inhibitors or histamine–H2 antagonists were used routinely. The study medication was terminated after a 15-day period; in the case of a patient discharge from ICU earlier, he or she was automatically excluded from the study. Study Variables Study variables included patients demographics and comorbidities,8 admission diagnosis, medical history, surgical procedures, medical treatment, devices, and microbiological, biochemical, and hematological data. The Glasgow Coma Scale and the APACHE II score on admission to the ICU;9 infections, sepsis, acute organ system failure, and outcome were also recorded.10,11 Follow-up continued until death or discharge from the ICU. 1850 Kotzampassi et al.: Synbiotics in Critically Ill Table 1.
