full_text: Int J Clin Exp Med 2019;12(9):11893-11897 www.ijcem.com /ISSN:1940-5901/IJCEM0099058 Review Article An overview of diarrhea in ICU patients receiving enteral nutrition Weiting Chen1*, Junbo Chen2*, Bin Lin3 Departments of 1Intensive Care Unit, 2Emergency Medical, 3Orthopaedics, Taizhou Integrated Chinese and Western Medicine Hospital, Wenling, Zhejiang Province, China. *Equal contributors and co-first authors. Received June 29, 2019; Accepted August 7, 2019; Epub September 15, 2019; Published September 30, 2019 Abstract: Enteral nutrition is accepted as the first choice for critically ill patients. Diarrhea, one of the most common complications, tends to prolong the time of reaching the target nutrient, or even interrupt the intake of nutrients. Consequently, it may not only reduce the therapeutic effect and prolong the length of stay in the hospital, but it also may induce electrolyte disturbance, internal environment changes, and may even be life-threatening. Therefore, doctors and nurses must pay attention to the occurrence of diarrhea. The present study briefly reviews the causes, prevention, and treatment of enteral nutrition-related diarrhea. Keywords: Enteral nutrition, diarrhea, antibiotics, probiotics, prebiotics Introduction Nutritional support therapy has received an increasing amount of attention in intensive care units (ICU). Enteral nutrition is increasingly and widely used in clinics in view of its safety, effectiveness, convenience, and conformity to physiological modes. Studies have shown that enteral nutrition protects the integrity of gastrointestinal mucosa, decreases the occurrence of toxins from the intestinal tract entering into the circulation, thus reducing the incidence of sepsis and multiple organ failure, and is associated with a reduced length of stay in the hospital and reduced mortality [1, 2]. However, critically ill patients often suffer from intolerance during the implementation of enteral nutrition, such as abdominal distension, diarrhea, reflux and constipation, especially diarrhea, which is the most common gastrointestinal symptom with an incidence of 14-36% [3-5]. Simultaneously, continuous diarrhea may further increase the workload of nurses and the cost of ICU hospitalization. Meanwhile, it damages perianal skin, reduces enteral nutrition intake, causes wound contamination, dehydration and the disturbance of water and electrolyte balance, and thus increases the mortality of patients [6-8]. Therefore, treating diarrhea should be considered a highly important task by medical staff in clinical practice. This article reviews the related risk factors, prevention, and treatment of enteral nutrition-related diarrhea in ICU patients during hospitalization. Definition Diarrhea refers to the abnormal increase in intestinal peristalsis and stool frequency. Annika et al. suggested that it should be divided into two pathophysiological mechanisms of permeable and secretory diarrhea [9]. However, the definition of diarrhea has not yet been unified. The World Health Organization recommends that it be defined as ≥3 defecations per day and sparse feces. For critically ill patients, some experts suggest that diarrhea should be defined as increased defecation frequency (≥3 times/d), increased defecation volume (> 200 g/d), pasty or watery stool (refer to Bristol Stool Scale type 5-7), while meeting the above conditions [9]. Enteral nutrition-related diarrhea Formulation of nutrient solution Nasointestinal/gastric tube feeding can affect the original physiological state of the intestinal Diarrhea in ICU patient receiving enteral nutrition cavity by changing the time of nutrient delivery, intestinal secretion/absorption capacity, and intraluminal microenvironment, interfering with intraluminal microflora metabolism, thus leading to diarrhea. In this regard, the formulation, supply and speed of enteral nutrition, the beginning and duration of enteral nutrition are all related factors associated with the occurrence of diarrhea. Different formulations of enteral nutrition can increase or decrease the incidence of diarrhea. As evidenced by prior research, the supplementation of fiber in the nutrient solution can reduce the gastric emptying rate, improve the barrier function of the intestinal cavity, enhance the regeneration of epithelial cells, increase the absorption of liquids and electrolytes in the colon, and then reduce the incidence of diarrhea [10]. However, hypertonic nutrient solution may result in the development of diarrhea and gastrointestinal intolerance through osmotic action [11]. FODMAPs are a type of food rich in oligosaccharides, disaccharides, monosaccharides, and polyols which are difficult to absorb. The rapid fermentation of this food in the intestinal tract causes an increase in the osmotic pressure in the intestinal cavity, which induces the increase of gas production and causes diarrhea, abdominal pain, abdominal distension and other related symptoms easily. Accordingly, a nutrient solution containing low FODMAPs can reduce the incidence of diarrhea [12]. In addition, the high nutritional value of nutrient solution makes it an excellent growth and reproduction medium for microorganisms. Hence, the contamination of the nutrient solution or of a nutrient delivery device (nasointestinal tube, etc.) can increase the risk of developing diarrhea. With respect to the above, nurses handling the daily infusion of enteral nutrition should pay attention to the fresh preparation of the nutrient solution and ensure low temperature preservation to prevent contamination. fast infusion speed will lead to faster gastrointestinal peristalsis and even intolerance in patients. For example, a study performed by Ronan showed that a target of over 60% nutrient solution supply increased the incidence of diarrhea [13]. Therefore, enteral nutrition should start with a low dosage and infusion speed, and gradually increase to the level that patients can adapt to, avoiding diarrhea. In this regard, in the process, the principle of gradual progress should be followed and the enteral nutrition infusion should be performed in a mode from low to high concentration, from small to large doses, and from slow to fast with a 24 h uniform infusion, so that patients have a gradual adaptation process. Furthermore, although there is no strong medical evidence to support the different roles of continuous and intermittent enteral nutrition in the reduction of diarrhea, a single-center trial has suggested that continuous enteral nutrition can reduce the incidence of diarrhea [14, 15]. Time of enteral nutrition Quantity of supply and the infusion rate of the nutrient solution For critically ill patients, enteral nutrition should be given 24-48 h after admission as long as intestinal function exists. Early enteral nutrition can not only provide energy to enhance the body’s resistance, but it can also protect the integrity of the intestinal mucosal structure and barrier function through the intestinal absorption of nutrients, reducing intestinal flora translocation. A prior retrospective study showed that patients with acute shock who received enteral nutrition within 24 h exhibited a lower risk of developing diarrhea than those who received enteral nutrition within 74-96 h [16]. There is a positive correlation between fasting time with the occurrence of malabsorptive diarrhea and osmotic diarrhea after enteral nutrition. In addition, in a previous study involving 130 acute shock patients who received enteral nutrition, the corresponding results suggested that when enteral nutrition lasted over 7 days, it was related to diarrhea. In other words, the longer the duration, the higher the incidence of diarrhea [17]. The presence of a period of fasting before enteral nutrition in critically ill patients can easily lead to intestinal dysfunction, and the patients are in a state of stress. Therefore, an overload of nutrient solution supply and a too It is speculated that diarrhea may also be related to the temperature of nutrient solution, fat content, calorie density, the location of nutrient tube and the source of protein. However, there is insufficient evidence to prove any such direct 11894 Int J Clin Exp Med 2019;12(9):11893-11897 Diarrhea in ICU patient receiving enteral nutrition relationship with diarrhea, which remains to be confirmed by further research [18]. Prevention of enteral nutrition-related diarrhea Diarrhea in ICU patients is a common complication of enteral nutrition. In order to prevent diarrhea, the following should be noted in addition to dealing with the causes of diarrhea: avoid unnecessary fasting; correct hypoproteinemia in time; avoid the long-term use of antibiotics and gastrointestinal prokinetic drugs as much as possible; pay attention to the low temperature preservation of nutrient solution, avoid contamination and heating during feeding, and control the infusion rate, etc. It can also be prevented by adding cellulose, probiotics, prebiotics and lactoferrin. Fiber-rich nutrient solution Cellulose can reduce diarrhea, as mentioned above. A meta-analysis of 51 studies found that fiber-rich enteral nutrients reduced the incidence of diarrhea [19]. In critically ill patients, mixed fibers can not only reduce the incidence of diarrhea in antibiotic-treated patients, but they can also increase the concentration of short-chain fatty acids (SCFA). Furthermore, cellulose can not only prevent the occurrence of diarrhea, but it can also have certain antiinflammatory effects. It can increase the growth and reproduction of anaerobic bacteria in the intestinal tract to avoid the over-proliferation of pathogenic bacteria and the subsequent occurrence of diarrhea. Therefore, ASPEN suggests that fiber-rich enteral nutrition should be considered for critically ill patients with diarrhea [15]. Probiotics and prebiotics Probiotics are a category of active microorganisms beneficial to the host, which mainly include the three categories of lactobacillus, bifidobacteria and gram-positive cocci. Probiotics can directly act on the intestinal microenvironment, which can produce digestive enzymes to realize better digestion and absorption of nutrients, but they also can inhibit the growth of harmful bacteria, adjust and correct the imbalance of various flora, promote the growth of intestinal mucosa, enhance intestinal immune function, and prevent diarrhea. However, there have be- 11895 en some controversies about the application of probiotics in recent decades [20, 21]. For instance, it has been reported that the use of probiotics may increase the mortality of patients with pancreatitis [22]. A randomized controlled study also found that lactobacillus exhibited no significant effect on improving the course and condition of diarrhea in critically ill patients [23]. Therefore, high-quality research is needed to further explore the value of probiotics in the prevention and treatment of diarrhea. Probiotic and prebiotic complexes are reported to be more effective in regulating intestinal flora than probiotics alone [24]. Prebiotics refer to food ingredients that are not easily digested. They can promote gastrointestinal health and effectively solve various intestinal problems by selectively stimulating the growth and activity of one or several beneficial bacteria, such as bifidobacteria and lactobacillus. Prebiotics widely used in clinic include isomaltooligosaccharides, fructooligosaccharides, and xylo-oligosaccharides, which can be decomposed and absorbed by beneficial bacteria in the intestine to promote the growth and reproduction of beneficial bacteria. Many studies have confirmed the beneficial effects of probiotics on the human body [25]. However, a multi-center study of probiotics and prebiotics conducted by the European Society of Pediatric Gastroenterology and Hepatology Nutrition showed that probiotics exerted no preventive role in the development of diarrhea [26]. An animal experiment found that probiotics, including fructose and lactulose, can even increase the possibility of intestinal bacterial translocation [27]. Therefore, the role of probiotics in the prevention and treatment of diarrhea remains to be determined. Lactoferrin Lactoferrin is a non-heme iron-binding glycoprotein present in milk and a bactericidal monomer glycoprotein released from neutrophil granules. It not only participates in the transport of iron, but it also has powerful biological functions such as broad-spectrum antimicrobial, antioxidant, anticancer, and immune regulation. Some clinical studies in children have shown that lactoferrin can reduce the duration of diarrhea. Moreover, a recent study also pr- Int J Clin Exp Med 2019;12(9):11893-11897 Diarrhea in ICU patient receiving enteral nutrition oved that lactoferrin can reduce the incidence of diarrhea in patients with long-term nasointestinal feeding [28]. Treatment of enteral nutrition-related diarrhea In the case of diarrhea, one should first determine the cause of the diarrhea, carry out an abdominal examination, observe whether there are signs such as abdominal distension and abdominal pain, and determine whether bowel sounds are hyperactive during auscultation, and collect stool samples right away and send them for examination to exclude infectious diarrhea. Meanwhile, it is necessary to pay close attention to the changes in a patient’s intake and output, hematuria and electrolytes, and to correct the imbalance of water and electrolytes at an early stage. The timely management of diarrhea is essential for critically ill patients. First, it is necessary to review the appropriateness of drug use and adjust the use of drugs, such as antibiotics, discontinue gastrointestinal prokinetic drugs and laxatives; reduce the dosage of the enteral nutrition solution and the infusion rate; replace the nutrient solution (low volume and high calorie nutrient solution or nutrient solution rich in cellulose and low FODMAPs), etc. It is not recommended to discontinue enteral nutrition immediately. Only patients with severe diarrhea should consider parenteral nutrition and then enteral nutrition after improvement [11]. Diarrhea is primarily treated by symptomatic treatment, such as the use of antidiarrheal agents such as loperamide or codeine, but it should be used after the exclusion of infectious diarrhea. It not only increases the burden of nursing and reduces the therapeutic effect, but also may increase the length of stay in the ICU and affect the prognosis. Therefore, great importance should be attached to diarrhea in clinical practice. There is a need to clarify the relevant causes of diarrhea immediately after it starts. title: Comparison of early enteral nutrition in severe acute pancreatitis with prebiotic fiber supplementation versus standard enteral solution: A prospective randomized double-blind study
abstract: Aim: To compare the beneficial effects of early enteral nutrition (EN) with prebiotic fiber supplementation in patients with severe acute pancreatitis (AP). Methods: Thirty consecutive patients with severe AP, who required stoppage of oral feeding for 48 h, were randomly assigned to nasojejunal EN with or without prebiotics. APACHE II score, Balthazar's CT score and CRP were assessed daily during the study period. Results: The median duration of hospital stay was shorter in the study group [10 ¬± 4 (8-14) d vs 15 ¬± 6 (7-26) d] (P < 0.05). The median value of days in intensive care unit was also similar in both groups [6 ¬± 2 (5-8) d vs 6 ¬± 2 (5-7) d]. The median duration of EN was 8 ¬± 4 (6-12) d vs 10 ¬± 4 (6-13) d in the study and control groups, respectively (P > 0.05). Deaths occurred in 6 patients (20%), 2 in the study group and 4 in the control group. The mean duration of APACHE II normalization (APACHE II score < 8) was shorter in the study group than in the control group (4 ¬± 2 d vs 6.5 ¬± 3 d, P < 0.05). The mean duration of CRP normalization was also shorter in the study group than in the control group (7 ¬± 2 d vs 10 ¬± 3 d, P < 0.05). Conclusion: Nasojejunal EN with prebiotic fiber supplementation in severe AP improves hospital stay, duration nutrition therapy, acute phase response and overall complications compared to standard EN therapy. ¬© 2007 The WJG Press. All rights reserved.

Key words: Severe acute pancreatitis; Prebiotics; Enteral nutrition; Treatment http://www.wjgnet.com/1007-9327/13/2733.asp Acute pancreatitis (AP) is an inflammatory disorder of variable severity including multiorgan failure (MOF), and high mortality in severe cases (15%-40%). In the first phase of the disease, the initial local inflammatory process in the pancreas leads to cytokine release and subsequent systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS)[1]. The standard therapeutic approach to the management of AP involves the reduction of pancreatic exocrine secretion by pancreatic rest achieved via stopping oral feeding. This concept has evolved since the last decade that gives nutrients beyond Treitz ligament, the post-pyloric feeding, and decreases pancreatic stimuli, thus improving the overall outcome. However, post-pyloric feeding could decrease pancreatic stimuli and improve the overall outcome[2]. Controlled trials comparing total parenteral nutrition (TPN) versus enteral (jejunal) nutrition (EN) in acute AP have revealed a significant benefit for the enteral nutrition groups in the length of hospital stay, morbidity, complications and in some studies, decreased mortality[3]. The logic behind the EN in acute AP is restoration of commensal bacterial flora, which dramatically alters early in the disease process[4,5]. The supplementation of synbiotics (combination nutritional supplements comprised of probiotics and prebiotics) to patients with severe AP has so far only been reported in one study[6]. Although there were fewer complications related to severe AP in symbioticsupplemented group, the study has received criticism for having several methodological shortcomings[7,8]. Therefore, pre-pro or symbiotic-supplemented enteral nutrition is a challenging area in the treatment of severe AP. The multicenter Dutch study formulated PROPATRIA in order to evaluate whether administration of probiotics in a large study group is able to reduce the infective complications in www.wjgnet.com 2734 ISSN 1007-9327 CN 14-1219/R World J Gastroenterol severe AP and this study is still in progress[9]. The aim of the present study was to determine the beneficial effects of prebiotic fiber-supplemented EN on the clinical course of severe AP in comparison with standard EN. MATERIALS AND METHODS Study design This study was a randomized prospective double-blind controlled clinical trial which compared the effect of a special prebiotic fiber-supplemented enteral diet with that of a control standard formula in patients with severe AP. Patients were treated in the Division of Gastroenterology, Faculty of Medicine, Gazi University. T he local institutional ethical board approved the study protocol and all patients gave their written informed consent before inclusion into the study. Randomization was done by computer-generated numbers and the sequence was implemented using numbered containers. Both patients and investigators were blind to the therapy. Patients Thirty consecutive patients with severe AP admitted to our department between September 2004 and May 2006, who required stoppage of oral feeding for 48 h, were included. Acute pancreatitis was defined as acute abdominal pain associated with elevated levels of serum amylase or serum lipase (> 5 times upper limit of normal) and ultrasound or computed tomographic evidence of acute pancreatitis[10]. Severe pancreatitis was defined as APACHE Ⅱ score > 8 or CT score > 7 or a C-reactive protein (CRP) level in excess of 150 mg/L[11-14]. Patients were excluded if (1) there was a delay between onset of symptoms and refeeding of over 30 d, (2) the patient was already on oral feeds at presentation, (3) the patient had an acute exacerbation of chronic pancreatitis, (4) if the patient needed surgery for complications of acute pancreatitis, (5) patients with paralytic ileus. APACHE Ⅱ score and CRP were assessed daily after admission in order to monitor inflammatory response during the study period. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen was performed 72 h after the onset of acute pancreatitis and the severity of pancreatitis according to Balthazar’s score was recorded[15]. Enteral nutrition A nasojejunal (NJ) feeding tube (size 8F Flocare ® polyurethane feeding tube, Nutricia Ltd) was inserted endoscopically under fluoroscopical guidance with its position checked by aspiration and pH measurement. Where aspiration was unsuccessful, a plain abdominal radiograph was performed. The patients started feeding after the position of the tube was rechecked with a plain abdominal radiograph 12 h after the procedure. If the tube was dislodged on the radiograph, the tube was repositioned immediately before starting feeding. Feeds were commenced at full strength and a rate of 30 mL/h increasing to 100 mL/h over 24-48 h. The caloric target was 2000 kcal/d, which was chosen over an individually calculated target in an attempt to simplify administration. The diets in the two groups were identical in caloric (125 www.wjgnet.com May 21, 2007 Volume 13 Number 19 Table 1 Demographic features and baseline biochemical, prognostic indices in study and control groups Study group (n = 15) Mean age (SD) 47.3 (16.8) Sex (Male:Female) 6:9 Body mass index mean (SD) 24.7 (7.8) Interval between onset of symptoms 2 (1-9) and admission (days) median (range) Etiology (n, %) Biliary 11 (73.3) Post-ERCP 2 (13.3) Hyperlipidemia 1 (6.6) Idiopathic 1 (6.6) At admission Serum amylase, mean (SD) IU/L 545 (232) Serum lipase, mean (SD) IU/L 554 (167) Serum CRP, mean (SD) mg/L 232 (97) Serum prealbumin, mean (SD) mg/L 17.6 (4.5) APACHE Ⅱ score, mean (SD) 9.4 (3.7) Balthazar CT score, mean (SD) 8.5 (4.6) Control group P (n = 15) 44.9 (11.2) 8:7 27.1 (9.5) 1 (1-6) NS NS NS NS 12 (80) 2 (13.3) 1 (6.6) 0 (0) NS NS NS NS 498 (277) 396 (187) 244 (104) 19.1 (6.7) 9.6 (3.8) 9.1 (5.2) NS NS NS NS NS NS ERCP: endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreaticography; CRP: C-reactive protein; CT: computerized tomography; SD: standard deviation; NS: not significant. kcal/100 mL), lipid (35%) and protein (20%) content. Study group received additional multifibers including 0.7 g/100 mL soluble fibers and 0.8 g/100 mL insoluble fibers, a total of 1.5 g/100 mL (24 g per day) multifiber supplement as a prebiotic. All patients received adjuvant peripheral parenteral nutrition with a standard solution containing 120 g/L glucose, 50 g/L aminoacid, 20% lipid (Aminomix 2®, Fresenius-Kabi, Hamburg-Germany). Statistical and power analysis Data were expressed as mean or percentage. Continuous data were compared using two-sample t test (parametric) or Mann-Whitney test (non-parametric), and comparison of categorical variables was made using chi-square test or Fischer’s exact test. P < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. Statistical analysis was done with SPSS 8.0 software (SPSS, Inc, Chicago, Ⅲ). A power calculation revealed that 30 patients were required to show a 25% difference in CRP concentrations with a 0.05 alpha value and 80% statistical power. RESULTS Thirty severe AP patients were divided into two groups and the study g roup received additional prebiotic supplementation. The demographic features of the patients are shown in Table 1. The most common etiology was gallstones in the study and control groups (73.3% vs 80%) , respectively. The median age, sex distribution, mean body mass index, distribution of etiological factors, mean interval between onset of symptoms and admission to hospital, mean value of baseline serum amylase, lipase, CRP, prealbumin and APACHE Ⅱ, Balthazar CT scores were similar between the two groups (Table 1). All patients started EN within 24 h of admission. The enteral feeding was well tolerated. The median duration of hospital stay Karakan T� et al . Seven patients in the study group and 6 patients in the control group were admitted to the Intensive Care Unit (46.6% vs 40%, P > 0.05). The median value of days in Intensive Care Unit was also similar in both groups [6 ± 2 (5-8) d vs 6 ± 2 (5-7) d]. All of these patients required assisted ventilatory therapy for respiratory failure. There were no complications associated with the tube insertion in both groups. In the control group, only one patient had removal of the NJ tube intentionally and the tube was re-inserted by endoscopy. In the study group, three patients had bloating and gas symptoms in the first 48 h of feeding. However, the symptoms subsided in the following days. The median duration of EN was 8 ± 4 (6-12) d vs 10 ± 4 (6-13) d in the study and control groups, respectively (P > 0.05). Deaths occurred in 6 patients (20%), 2 in the study group and 4 in the control group (Table 2). Early diagnostic endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) with endoscopic sphincterotomy (ES) was carried out in 10 patients in the study group and 11 patients in the control group. All of these patients had biliary pancreatitis. ERCP was not performed in only one patient with biliary pancreatitis for his severe cardiovascular collapse. All prognostic indices normalized earlier in the study group than in the control group (Table 3). The mean duration of CRP normalization was also shorter in the study group (7 ± 2 d vs 10 ± 3 d, P < 0.05). The mean duration of APACHE Ⅱ normalization (APACHE Ⅱ score < 8) was shorter in the study group than in the control group (4 ± 2 d vs 6.5 ± 3 d, P < 0.05). The mean duration of CT score normalization (CT score = 0 or 1) was also shorter in the study group than in the control group (12 ± 4 d vs 16 ± 3 d, P < 0.05). DISCUSSION A significant reduction in the commensal flora occurs early in the disease process, because of both disease and pharmaceutical treatment in severe acute pancreatitis. It has been observed in experimental pancreatitis that anaerobic P < 0.05 < 0.05 < 0.05 1 Normal values: APACHE Ⅱ < 8, CT score = 0-1, CRP negative. CRP: C-reactive protein; CT: computerized tomography. bacteria and lactobacilli are significantly reduced within 6-12 h after induction both in the distal small bowel and in the colon. These alterations lead to significant overgrowth with potentially pathogenic microorganisms, such as E coli, and dramatic increases in mucosal bar rier per meability (lumen to blood) and in endothelial permeability (blood to tissue), associated with increased pathogenic microbial colonization, translocation, microbial growth in mesenteric lymph nodes and finally pancreatic tissue[5,16,17]. Although translocation of pathogenic bacteria from gut lumen to lymph nodes is a well-documented entity, its presence and clinical significance in human beings are not clearly defined. A recent study indicated the importance of gut barrier function rather than the virulence of microorganisms, in the bacterial translocation process[18]. For this reason, we do not exactly know the underlying mechanisms of bacterial translocation-related distant infections (if it exists) in human beings. The early supplementation of synbiotics to severe AP patients has so far been reported only in one study[6], in which patients were supplemented daily with either 1 billion live or 1 billion heat-killed Lactobacillus plantarum 299 plus 10 g oat fibers during the first week. Infected pancreatic necrosis and abscesses were seen in one out of 22 patients (4.5%) supplied live Lactobacillus versus seven out of 23 (30%) of those supplied heat-killed Lactobacillus (P = 0.023). A much larger dose of Lactobacilli (2 × 40 billion per day) and four different fibers (20 g per day) could lead to a significant reduction (P < 0.05) in systemic inflammatory response syndrome and multiple organ failure, as eight out of 33 treated patients (24%) developed the syndrome compared with 14 out of 29 control patients (48%)[1]. Severe attacks of acute pancreatitis are strongly associated with the priming and subsequent over-activation of leukocytes, which contribute to the production of inflammatory mediators and the induction of distant organ failure[19]. The degree of oxidative stress and neutrophil activation are also of great importance for outcomes[20]. Severe AP patients have a documented deficit in antioxidants, flora, and the supply of fiber and nutrients, especially micronutrients which should be compensated for[1,21]. Patients in intensive care units usually have lost their entire Lactobacillus flora[22]. The colonic mucosa normally obtains most of its calories and antioxidants from the lumen and most of the absorbed nutritive substances are either produced by or released from microbial fermentation of plant fibers[23]. APACHE Ⅱ scores and ser um CRP nor malized earlier in the study group (4 ± 2 vs 6.5 ± 3 d and 7 ± 2 www.wjgnet.com 2736 ISSN 1007-9327 CN 14-1219/R World J Gastroenterol vs 10 ± 3 d, respectively, P < 0.05). These findings show that prebiotic fiber-supplemented EN improves acute phase response earlier than standard enteral solutions. The median duration of hospital stay was shorter in the study group than in the control group. This may be due to a consequence of early suppression of inflammatory response by prebiotic fiber supplementation. However, suppression of inflammation by probiotic and/or prebiotic fibers is still a speculative issue and further research is needed in order to clarify the mechanisms behind. Although the median duration of EN was similar between g roups, the rate of overall complications related to pancreatitis was higher in the control group. In contrast to probiotics, which beneficially affect the host by improving its intestinal microbial balance[24], the principle of prebiotics involves ingestion of a substance that selectively induces microbial growth and metabolic activity as opposed to direct administration of bacterial cells as dietary supplements. Thus, prebiotics encompass a wider range of products than probiotics. In addition, since probiotics produce a temporar y alteration in the composition of gut microbiota, changes are not maintained upon discontinuation of supplementation[25-29]. In the present study, since we did not use probiotics in EN, this is the first randomized study comparing the beneficial effects of prebiotics without probiotics on severe AP. One of the limitations of our study is the lack of bacterial count measurements in the stool of patients to see if there was a difference between the two groups. This might have added more information about the mechanisms behind the beneficial effects of prebiotics. In conclusion, nasojejunal EN with prebiotic fiber supplementation in severe AP improves hospital stay, duration of nutrition therapy, acute phase response and overall complications compared to standard EN therapy. The standard therapeutic approach to the management of AP involves the reduction of pancreatic exocrine secretion achieved via stopping oral feeding. This concept has evolved since the last decade that feeding beyond Treitz ligament decreases pancreatic stimuli. The addition of probiotics was shown to have beneficial effects. Prebiotics (non-absorbable fibers) enhances microbiota and has theoretical advantages over mucosal and systemic immunity. Research frontiers Recent studies indicate that lowered morbidity is related to post-pyloric feeding in severe acute pancreatitis. Few studies have mentioned the role of probiotics in this area. Prebiotics are not studied as an adjuvant therapy for acute pancreatitis. Related publications Prebiotic: Food substances that intend to promote the growth of certain bacteria in the intestines. Probiotic: Dietary supplements containing potentially beneficial bacteria or yeast, with lactic acid bacteria (LAB) as the most common microbes used. LAB have been used in the food industry for many years, because they are able to convert sugars (including lactose) and other carbohydrates into lactic acid. Synbiotic: Combinant nutritional supplements comprised of probiotics and prebiotics. title: Diarrhea in intensive care: Diagnosis and treatment
abstract: Diarrhea is a common complication in critically ill patients. The use of ready-to-use sterile formulas, disposable feeding lines, and flow regulated pumps should decrease the frequency of diarrhea due to enteral nutrition. Antimicrobial agents are an important cause of diarrhea, because they modify the digestive flora and may induce Clostridium difficile colitis. Occurrence of diarrhea is also correlated with several factors reflecting the severity of the underlying disease, such as shock and sepsis. Treatment of diarrhea includes rehydration agents that delay transit, restoration of a normal flora, treatment of a specific cause and of the underlying disease.

Bleichner, O. Thomas, J.P. Sollet Service de refmimation polyvalente, Centre hospitalier Victor Dupouy, Argenteuil, France (Accepted22 February 1993) Introduction Diarrhea is a common complication in critically ill patients. Although it is not well established whether or not it modifies the vital prognosis, its consequences may be severe: loss of water and electrolytes, failure to reach nutritional goals, risk of bedsores or systemic infection. Diarrhea also brings additional work for the nursing staff who change the patient frequently to avoid skin maceration and bedsores, and loss of comfort and dignity. In a fed patient, approximately 1500 ml of ileal liquid goes through the ileo-colic junction. Diarrhea Correspondence to: G. Bleichner,Servicede r6animation polyvalente, Centre hospitalier Victor Dupouy, 69 avenue du lieutenant colonel Proudhon, 95107 Argenteuilcedex, France. Tel: 34 23 24 51, Fax: 34 23 27 91. occurs if the volume of ileal fecal output exceeds the electrolytic reabsorption capacities of the colon, or if the reabsorption ability is impaired. The association of several mechanisms can create an electrolytic diarrhea by excess of intestinal secretion and/or lack of colic reabsorption. Those mechanisms include toxic injury, osmotic charge (due to a large osmolar supply), alteration of absorption capacity and acceleration of gastrointestinal transit. Several factors, often combined in the same patient, may cause diarrhea. The three main factors associated with diarrhea are (1) tube feeding, (2) alteration of intestinal flora and (3) severity of the underlying disease. A specific cause will be first suspected: diarrhea can be due to a disease of the intestine or the colon or to an Ogilvie syndrome. It can also reveal an intra-abdominal disease: peritoneal abscess, peritonitis, pancreatitis or fecal impaction. 34 Epidemiology Definition The true definition of diarrhea is based on the daily stool output. Wyman [1], in a study of the daily stool output of healthy subjects, has defined a priori diarrhea as more than 250 g of stool per day, which represents a daily stool output exceeding 3 standard deviations from the mean fecal output. Edes [2] defines diarrhea as more than 500 ml of watery stool on 2 consecutive days. In severely ill patients, because stool collecting is not easy, these definitions are difficult to use. The definitions usually used in intensive care vary widely according to the authors. These definitions take into account the frequency of defecation and the consistency of the stool: more than 1 [3], more than 2 [4,5,6] or more than 3 [7,8] liquid stools in a day. Another more subjective definition of diarrhea is an alteration of bowel habits associated with passage of loose or frequent stools sufficient to be noticed by the patient or by the nursing staff [9,10]. However, the subjective assessment of diarrhea using the frequency of defecation does not correlate with the weight of stool [11] and thus is not reliable when evaluating diarrhea. Another study has shown the inadequacy of patients claims and frequency of defecation to define diarrhea [12]. Hart [13] has proposed a semi-quantitative score according to the volume and the consistency of stool; this score allows a more accurate approach to diarrhea and is more easily used in intensive care patients. Prevalence rate The frequency of diarrhea reported in patients receiving tube feeding varies from 2.3 to 68 % [6, 7]. This is usually attributed to several factors: use of different definitions for diarrhea, lack in precision of definitions, differences in the patient environment (underlying disease, treatments, mode of nutrition). Diarrhea rate can be expressed as incidence or as percentage of days with diarrhea. A prospective study in enterally fed patients showed that the definition of diarrhea significantly influenced the reported incidence of diarrhea and the percentage of days with diarrhea [14]. In this study, duration of moni- toring was correlated with the incidence of diarrhea. When diarrhea was reported as the percentage of days with diarrhea, the influence of monitoring duration disappeared. In intensive care, two recent prospective studies reported diarrhea incidence of 12% to 30% of patients, respectively [15,16], and 14% to 15% of feeding days with diarrhea [15,17]. The frequency of diarrhea in intensive care seems to have decreased over the past years. Tube feeding related factors In intensive care, tube feeding induces an increased risk of diarrhea [18,19,20]. Diarrhea is more often associated with enteral than with parenteral feeding [6]. Frequency of diarrhea during enteral feeding can reach 70%, whereas other authors reported a 2.3% frequency [7]. When the patient is not fed, or during a fiber-free regimen, the weight of stool decreases and the stools become mainly composed of digestive cellular fragments and bacteria. Infusion rate Bolus feeds can cause diarrhea. Small volume boli of nutrients may be tolerated, but great volumes constantly produce diarrhea. Bowel movements are more frequent with bolus feeds of 50 to 150 ml/h than with a flow-controlled pump [3,21,22]. Constant infusion using gravity is better tolerated than bolus feeds and may help to avoid diarrhea. Infusion rates below 150 kcal/h are well tolerated [23]. Diarrhea can be avoided by using flow-controlled pumps in patients with intestinal or pancreatic disease, or after gastrectomy or ileal resection [24]. Cataldi-Betcher has reported a decrease of the frequency of diarrhea from 30% to 2.3%, due to the use of nutrition pumps [7]. For direct jejunal infusion, a flow-controlled pump is always necessary. Osmolality Hyperosmolality of the formula is no longer considered as a major cause of diarrhea. Even if abdom- 35 inal pain, flatulence and diarrhea can occur during hyperosmolar diet, as in the 'dumping syndrome', these observations were reported with artisanal hyperosmolar formulas administrated in bolus. This mode of nutrition is no longer used. The relationship between osmolality and diarrhea should be evaluated according to the site of instillation (gastric or jejunal) and to the underlying intestinal disease. Case [25] demonstrated that, whatever the feeding osmolality (250 to 700 mosmol/1), the osmolality measured in the duodenum reaches quite the same value (480 mosmol/1) a few minutes after gastric infusion. Then, osmolality declines from the duodenojejunal junction to the end of the jejunum. Equilibration of osmolality between plasma and intestinal juice can induce a hydroelectrolytic secretion, especially when sodium concentration of the formula is below 90 mosmol/1; this secretion will be increased according to the shortness of the equilibration segment. Regulation of gastric emptying is due to the volume and the caloric value of the bolus. Gastric emptying is regular when infusion rate is constant [26,27]. Experiments in dogs with post-operative ileus have demonstrated that a monomeric hyperosmolar diet does not produce more diarrhea or myoelectric intestinal activity than a polymeric iso-osmolar diet [28]. In clinical studies, tolerance of feeds is the same whatever the osmolality of the formula [9,23]. If the intestine is normal, pump or gravity regulated tube feeding using formulas with a large osmolality range and infusion rate varying from 50 to 150 kcal/h demonstrate the same frequency of diarrhea [9,16,23,27,29-33]. When using a naso-duodenal feeding tube, tolerance of varying osmolalities is good if the instillation is pump regulated [23]. A greater frequency of diarrhea has even been reported with isotonic diets [9]. However, an osmotic diarrhea can occur in particular situations: - In organic or functional diseases of the bowel, or alteration of gastric emptying, hyperosmolar formulas can favor diarrhea, especially when using bolus feeds or irregular infusion rates. In enterally fed postoperative patients, osmotic diarrhea due to carbohydrate malabsorption has been reported. In case of preexisting intestinal disease, increase of the infusion rate should be progressive. However, in moder- ately severe intestinal diseases, monomeric hyperosmolar diets with constant infusion rate induce more diarrhea than polymeric iso-osmolar regimens particularly in the beginning of the nutrition. This was not observed in more severe diseases, where a better choice is a diet of hydrolysed nutrients [34]. -Medications containing unabsorbable osmolar agents such as sorbitol are a cause of diarrhea in tube fed patients [2,35]. In the study of Edes, 80% of diarrheic patients had an osmotic diarrhea; the tube feeding formula was responsible in 21% and osmotic medications in 48% of diarrhea cases; the stool osmotic gap (= (stool osmolality) - 2 x (stool sodium concentration + stool potassium concentration)) was efficient in distinguishing osmotic from non-osmotic diarrhea in all cases. Lactose Lactose intolerance is a classical cause of osmotic diarrhea during enteral nutrition [36]. The lactose is not digested and creates an osmotic imbalance that leads to water movement toward the intestinal lumen and diarrhea. Lactose intolerance is found in 0 to 30% of European people and can be as high as 70 to 100% in other populations (Africa, Asia). Various diseases that alter the intestinal mucosa can induce a secondary lactase deficit. However, patients with secondary lactase deficit can tolerate a certain amount of lactose without diarrhea [37]. In these patients, bolus feeds of nutrients containing lactose increase stool volume above 1 l/day [38], whereas continuous gastric infusion of lactose (20 to 40 g/day) does not induce more diarrhea than a lactose free regimen [24,39]. Even with a constant rate, higher amounts of lactose (5.6 g/h) [40] induce diarrhea. One explanation is a partial lactase deficit. The action of lactose during enteral nutrition is proportional to infusion rate: tolerance is good if the rate is moderate and constant, but with high rates diarrhea appears. Tolerance of lactose can be good, even at high dosage, if it is administrated in combination with a lactase or in pre-hydrolysed form [40]. Manufacturers have practically suppressed lactose from most nutrition formulas. Thus, lactose is rarely responsible for diarrhea. 36 Lipids There are some cases where a diet with excessive amounts of lipids can cause diarrhea. Experimental data show that for the same caloric supply, a lipid rich diet (50% of lipid calories) causes more diarrhea (71% vs 13%) than poorer lipid diets (5, 15, 30% of lipid calories) [30]. In burned patients, diarrhea is less frequent in those with lipid poor regimens (< 20 g) (74%) than in other patients (44%) [31]. Frequency of diarrhea is 16% with a 13.5 g/l lipid diet versus 62% with an isotonic, isonitrogenous diet containing 33 g/1 of lipids. Some patients may be intolerant (abdominal pain and diarrhea) to medium chain triglycerides [41]. Several mechanisms are suspected in lipid-induced diarrhea: the decreased concentration of biliary acids below the critical micellar concentration may be responsible for steatorrhea. The toxic action ofhydroxylated fatty acids toward the intestinal mucosa can cause oversecretion and hydroelectrolytic diarrhea. Linoleic and arachidonic acids may stimulate the synthesis of prostaglandins that modify the motility and the hydroelectrolytic secretion of the intestinal tract. The action oflipids might also be due to a direct effect on cellular intestinal membranes [42]. In vitro, fatty acids in excess are responsible for alterations of electrolytic membranous exchanges. They can modify the electrolytic gradient of several active transport systems of the small intestine brush border membrane. Fatty acids in excess can induce a decrease of absorption of organic matter and electrolytes and thus favor osmotic diarrhea [43]. cosa [45]. Vitamin B 12 and folinic acid have atrophic action on the ileo-jejunum. Alterations of normal intestinal flora The relations between alterations of the intestinal flora and diarrhea are complex. Modifications of the intestinal flora correlate with the severity of the underlying intestinal disease. These alterations can be due to an outbreak of pathogenic bacteria, or to the predominance of a common bacterium to the detriment of the others. It creates an imbalance in the intestinal bacterial ecosystem, with loss of the 'barrier effect'. The main causes are exogenic bacterial contamination and alterations of the normal flora induced by drugs or stress (decrease of local or systemic antibacterial defenses, immunologic disturbance, intestinal motility disorders, malnutrition). The alterations of the digestive flora may create a syndrome comparable to the 'blind loop' syndrome, despite the absence of anatomic abnormality [46], with diarrhea due to lipid and saccharide malabsorption or to dysconjugation of biliary acids [47,48]. Conjugated biliary acids are normally absorbed in the ileum. In the case of intestinal bacterial overgrowth, these acids are hydroxyled by colic bacteria. These dysconjugated acids can alter the permeability of the mucosa, increase the activity of adenylcyclase and modify the composition of the mucosa. Bacterial overgrowth in the digestive tract also induces a risk of bacterial translocation from the gut to the portal circulation and can induce systemic infection. Microbial contamination of feeding solutions Insufficient supply of micronutrients Diarrhea can occur in zinc deficient patients. In burned patients, an insufficient supply of vitamin A (< 10 000 IU/day) statistically correlates with diarrhea [44]. The association of vitamin A deficiency and diarrhea has been suspected in deficient children, because diarrhea was corrected by vitamin A supplementation [44]. Vitamin A is necessary for the replacement of epithelia. In rats, vitamin A deficiency leads to atrophy of epithelia, loss of mucus and increase of bacterial adhesion on intestinal mu- Microbial contamination of nutrients was previously considered as the main factor responsible for diarrhea in intensive care units. This contamination has been observed in 20 to 90% of cases [8,49,50] and r e a c h e d 10 7 to 10 8 CFU/ml. Nowadays, the risk of bacterial contamination of feeding formulas is very low, due to the systematic use of ready-to-use industrial formulas and disposable feeding lines. Guttman has reported cases of severe diarrhea due to staphylococcal enterocolitis in the newborn [51]. Pottecher [52] has reported 70% of severe diar- 37 rhea in adults, associated with a high mortality rate; 66% of the stool cultures were positive for bacteria (Pseudomonas, Enterobacteriaceae, Enterococcus, Candida) also found in the contaminated (10 6 t o 10 9 bacteria/ml) nutrients. Roberts reported similar observations in elderly patients [46]. Septicemia due to Enterobacter cloacae [53,54], Serratia marcescens [55], Salmonella enteritidis [56], Klebsiella pneumoniae [54] and Pseudomonas aeruginosa [5], associated or not with diarrhea, has been attributed to nutrient contamination. Anderson [8] has shown that bacterial contamination of nutrients exceeding 105 CFU/ml statistically correlates with diarrhea although this association was individually inconstant. Enteroccocus spp. and yeast have recently been implicated in ICU diarrhea [57]. Other authors have not found this correlation [10,55,58]. The inconstant occurrence of diarrhea after administration of contaminated nutrients might be due to the occasional loss of the gastic acid barrier which normally eliminates bacteria brought by nutrition [59]. In ICU patients, the gastric barrier may be altered by secretory failure, by alcalinisation or by the use of antibiotics. The gastric barrier may also be inefficient if the contamination is large, or in the case of direct jejunal infusion of contaminated nutrients. The usual diet in normal patients contains a large amount of bacteria [60], but colonization and infection rarely occur. On the contrary, in immunocompromised patients, after an antimicrobial treatment modifying the digestive flora or when enteral feeding bypasses the stomach, a small bacterial inoculation is probably sufficient to induce digestive contamination. [8,10,50,55,62,63]. If a home-made preparation is absolutely necessary, the formula should be diluted with sterile water [49,61,64] and prepared with rigorous asepsia. Sterile feeding bags, even filled aseptically, remain sterile only for a few hours [10,61]. Nutrients preparation Administration length. Bacterial overgrowth is proportional to the length of administration, independently of other factors. At ambient temperature, using a closed system and strict asepsia, bacterial overgrowth is low for an administration length from 6 to 12 h [58,63,69], moderate for an administration length from 12 to 24 h [50,64] and becomes high after 24 h [49]. Industrial ready-to-use formulas are sterile and can be used safely. Thus, these formulas should be preferred to hospital-made preparations and should not be decanted [61]. Hospital-made formulas might be contaminated and induce diarrhea. After 24 h of nutrition, bacterial concentrations exceeding 103 CFU/ml were found in 15% of industrial formulas, 57% of diluated nutrients and 94% of formulas made from powder [55]. Basic products, nursing staff or work surface can be at the origin of the contamination Nutrients administration Hand hygiene, manipulation of bottle and lines. Contamination of nutrients is the same whether the lines are changed every 12 h or every 24 h [49,65], and is probably related to the frequency of manipulation, despite diverging results [50,65]. Manipulation of lines and bottle without hand washing is a cause of contamination of formulas [49]. Bacteria found in feeding solutions are frequently isolated from the hands of nurses [50,66]. The use of sterile gloves for manipulation ofenteral feeds might be the only way to avoid hand contamination [67]. Delivery sets should not be used for more than 24 h [68]. Temperature of preservation and administration. Ready-to-use sterile formulas can be stored and administered at ambient temperature without contamination [69]. Hospital made formulas should be stored at low temperature, because ambient temperature stocking can lead to an exponential growth of bacteria, which can reach 108 to 10~°CFU/ml in 24 or 48 h [55]. Cooling has a bacteriostatic action: at 4°C bacterial overgrowth is almost nil [50,70,71]. The use of cooled delivery systems allows a lower contamination than non-cooled systems [61]. However, cooling only slows bacterial proliferation [72]. Feeding lines material Materials used in naso- gastric tubes (PVC, polyurethane) may have an irregular internal surface, which can be responsible for adhesion and proliferation of bacteria. Silicone tubes 38 are smoother and should be preferred [73]. When bacterial colonization of feeding tubes occurs, it may not be detected by monitoring of nutrient containers [74]. Daily cleaning of the feeding tubes has been recommended to remove bacteria from the tube lumina. However, Anderton has demonstrated that none of the cleaning procedures tested was totally effective in suppressing tube contamination [75]. Retrograde contamination. When small caliber naso-gastric tubes are used, there is no backward contamination of nutrients for at least 24 h [49], except for a massive retrograde spread of contaminated nutrients from the stomach [50]. However, when using jejunal tube feeding, a backward contamination of the formula by the usual digestive bacteria can occur, especially with moving bacterial species [76]. Contamination standards At present there are no official recommendations about an acceptable level of bacterial contamination of hospital made formulas. Propositions found in the literature tend to be more rigorous through the years [8,52,55,62,65,69,77]. One can consider as unacceptable formulas containing more than 102 CFU/ml of one of the following bacteria: Escherichia coli, Salmonella spp., Shigella spp., Clostridium spp., Staphyloccocus aureus, Bacillus cereus, Klebsiella spp., Aeromonas, Vibrioparahaemolyticus, and other bacteria developing at temperatures lower than 20 ° C. However, administration of sterile formulas is no longer a problem. When diarrhea occurs in intensive care, particularly if it seems to be epidemic, a quantitative bacterial analysis of nutrients is necessary. Each time a technique that favors microbial contamination is used, a bacterial watch over formulas has to be performed regularly. When bacterial contamination occurs, inspection of each step of the feeding chain is necessary, from the production to the administration. Antacids and amisecretory drags Antacids and antisecretory drugs can be responsi- ble for diarrhea. Two mechanisms are involved: failure of the gastric acid barrier and magnesium toxicity. Gastric alcalinisation increases the gastric pH and favors bacterial overgrowth. When the pH rises above 4 after treatment with antacids or cimetidine, colonization of gastric and ileal juices is fast [59,78,79,80]. The frequency of diarrhea in intensive care patients is higher in those receiving cimetidine than in others (51% vs 25%) [6]. Two controlled studies of prevention of stress gastric hemorrhage demonstrated a higher rate of diarrhea in patients receiving antacids (11 and 15%) than in patients receiving cimetidine (0 and 4%) [81,82]; In these studies, antacids increase the pH more than cimetidine. Diarrhea is frequent during treatment of gastroduodenal ulcers with anti H2 receptors and omeprazole [83, 84], or during prevention of stress gastric ulcers with antacids. Bacterial overgrowth was proven to be responsible for prolonged diarrhea with malnutrition in a patient receiving cimetidine [85]. Direct toxicity of antacids may also be responsible for diarrhea. During gastric alcalinisation, frequency of diarrhea is proportional to the neutralization ability of the antacids used, particularly for high neutralization capacities (320 to 1064 mosm/l) [86,87]. It is due to magnesium hydroxide that has laxative properties whereas alumina hydroxide has constipating properties [88]. Magnesium-induced diarrhea can be accurately diagnosed by quantitative fecal analysis for soluble magnesium (Fine). In practice, the antacid supply should not be higher than 200 mmol of neutralizing capacity and should not contain a great amount of magnesium hydroxide. Sucralfate does not modify the gastric pH and should be preferred to antacids for the prevention of stress gastric hemorrhage [80]. Diarrhea due to antimicrobial agents Use of antimicrobial agents is the most frequently reported cause of diarrhea in ICU [15]. Antibacterial and pharmacologic properties of antimicrobial agents modify the digestive flora and particularly the anaerobic flora [90]. All antibiotics except aminoglycoside agents administrated parenterally have been implicated, especially lincosamides and betalactams (particularly cephalosporins). Modifications of the 39 intestinal flora can favor the emergence of pathogenic species or overgrowth of a strain out of the normal flora, and thus be responsible for diarrhea. Diarrhea rate during antibiotic treatment varies according to the authors, and also depends on associated factors: enteral feeding, severity of the underlying disease. Two prospective studies reported 22% and 41% diarrhea rate in patients receiving antibiotics, respectively [20,15]. Several authors have shown a correlation between diarrhea and antimicrobial treatment, especially if broad spectrum antibiotics and oral route are used [3,9,13,18,24,31,91]. However, a few authors have not found this correlation [6,55]. Several mechanisms can be responsible for diarrhea during antimicrobial treatments. Modifications of intestinal motility have been demonstrated in vitro [92]. A direct toxic action of the antibiotic agent can also be responsible: neomycin interferes with lipid absorption and can favor steatorrhea, clindamycin modifies water and glucose exchange in rats [93,94]. Alterations of intestinal flora and their consequences are the main causes of antibiotic-induced diarrhea. Diarrhea can be due to the action of Clostridium difficile (CD) toxins, or to alterations of the normal absorption and digestion mechanisms. The decrease of carbohydrate fermentation can produce an osmotic effect [95]. The decrease of colic bacterial concentration and the production of fatty acids may induce a decrease of hydroelectrolytic colic absorption. CD is the specific agent of pseudomembranous enterocolitis, and its pathogenic action is due to its toxins, toxin A (enterotoxin) and toxin B (cytotoxin). The emergence of a CD is not always associated with diarrhea or colitis. In fact, CD is found in 15 to 20% of diarrheic patients without colitis. Two to 3% of healthy adults and 30 to 70% of healthy children below 8 months are CD carriers [96] (Table 1). After treatment and clinical cure, CD and its toxin can be found in stool. In critically ill patients, two prospective studies have failed to demonstrate the responsibility of CD in the occurrence of diarrhea [6,31]. Hospital acquisition of CD infection was first reported in 1981 [97] and recently studied. CD has been isolated from the hands and in the stool of the nursing staff. McFarland [98] recently demonstrated that 21% of patients become colonized by CD during hospital stay; 63% of these patients are asymptomatic, and 27% suffer diarrhea without colitis. Contamination of symptomatic patient's rooms was 49% vs 29% for asymptomatic patients. Fifty-nine per cent of the nursing staff had CD isolated from their hands. It has been shown that hospital spread and persistence of CD is propagated by readmission of previously hospitalized and asymptomatic patients who harbor the germ [99]. Furthermore, treatment of asymptomatic carriers with metronidazole is not effective, whereas treatment with vancomycine is temporarily effective but is associated with a higher rate of CD carriers 2 months after treatment [I00]. Thus, CD infection can be considered as a nosocomial infection, propagated by asymptomatic carriers, and treatment of these carriers seems to be inefficient. Factors related to the severity of the underlying disease Frequency of diarrhea is correlated to the severity of stress, and this might have been under-estimated in previous studies. Shock and infection are often associated with diarrhea [18], and MOF (multiple organ failure) may be a predisposing factor for tube feeding associated diarrhea [66,101]. After a trauma, the severity of the digestive intolerance is correlated to the trauma score [102]. Several physiopathologic mechanisms are suspected: mesenteric ischemia, conse. quences of infection, hypoalbuminemia and malnutrition. vein. When intestinal necrosis is complete, evolution is often lethal. When the lesions are limited, spontaneous evolution can be favourable in 2 to 4 days. Most ischemic colitis (85%) is moderate and reversible. Mesenteric isehemia Severe infections Acute mesenteric isehemia is associated with diarrhea in 80% 0fcases. An ischemie colitis can occur in varied conditions of stress where circulatory disorders are not always of the first importance: shock, hypovolemia, dehydration, cardiac dysfunction, arrhythmia, complication of cardiac surgery, sepsis syndrome, myocardial infarction, use of epinephrine, digitalis, diuretics [104,105]. Perrotin reported that diarrhea was significantly correlated to shock in a group of 114 critically ill patients [18]. In patients resuscitated after a cardiac arrest, diarrhea due to intestinal ischemia is frequent (26%) and its prognosis is poor; it is often associated with bacteremia by digestive translocation of bacteria [106]. Shocks of different mechanisms are associated with a decrease of mesenteric blood flow and with arterial vasoconstriction responsible for necrotic lesions of the intestinal mucosa. The consequences for the intestine can vary from the absence of lesions to irreversible transmural necrosis. Mucosa is more sensitive to ischemia than musculous membrane. Lesions begin at the top of the villosity and produce alterations of the mucous permeability. These alterations occur when local oxygen consumption is highly decreased [107] and are not associated with angiographic arterial lesions. Diarrhea due to ischemic colitis is often bloody and associated with abdominal pain, flatulence, fever and leucocytosis [108,109,110]. Physical examination is often poor. Abdominal X-ray can show colic distension. Barium enema can show thumbprint defects, due to pseudopolypoid lesions and ulcerations, more often in the colon left angle (75%) but also affecting the right colon (15%) and the sigmoid (10%). Colonoscopy may show congestive, pseudopolypoid ulcerated lesions. When intestinal necrosis is complete, bacterial super-infection is frequent and realises a gangrenous enterocolitis. General symptoms are shock and bloody diarrhea, and local signs have little specificity. X-ray is suggestive if it shows air in the intestinal wall and in the portal Diarrhea is a common event during severe infections. Diarrhea is significantly associated with infection and positive blood culture, and antibiotherapy does not appear as a favoring factor when infection is taken into account [18]. Hart has found a correlation between diarrhea and the number of extra-digestive infectious sites. Moreover, during septic shock with capillary permeability alterations, pulmonary oedema and intravascular coagulation, diarrhea is a frequent event. Experimental administration of living bacteria at a concentration that little modifies the hemodynamic parameters (20% increase of cardiac index) leads to an important decrease of mesenteric blood flow associated with mesenteric arterial vasoconstriction [111]. Furthermore, administration of endotoxin induces necrotic lesions of mucosa [112]. Intraperitoneal administration ofzymosan and paraffin in rat induces a severe inflammation responsible for multiple organ failure with adult respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), and is associated with liquid diarrhea probably due to the inflammation itself [113]. During septic shock, the intestine is particularly sensitive to ischemia. Several mechanisms can alter capillary circulation and capillary permeability in the small bowel: intravascular disseminated coagulation, release of mediators (histamine, PAF, prostaglandins, kinins, TNF, polynuclear activation) [114]. These mechanisms can induce severe organic and functional alterations in the digestive tract. Hypoalbuminemia Severe hypoalbuminemia might be responsible for diarrhea. Brinson [115] has found this relation in 67% of critically ill patients with enteral feeding, albuminemia below 25 g/1 and acute malnutrition [103,115]. During early post-operative enteral feeding, Cobb has found a greater incidence of diarrhea in patients 41 with low plasma albumin (28.3+ 14 g/l) than in those with normal albumin (37 + I I g/l) [I 16]. Durr has found a correlation between albuminemia and enteral feeding tolerance [117]. Diarrhea was frequent in patients with hypoalbuminemia and low oncotic pressure, and disappeared during enteral feeding only if the oncotic pressure had been previously corrected by parenteral feeding [118]. Ford has shown that hypoalbuminemia below 30 g/1 in children was often associated with digestive complications and malnutrition, which were corrected after normalization of the albuminemia [119]. However, these results have not been confirmed by several authors who did not find this relationship between hypoalbuminemia and diarrhea [31,120,121]. The mechanism suspected is based on a specific action of hypoalbuminemia on the intestinal hydroelectrolytic secretion. Experimental studies with animals in which hypoalbuminemia was created by dilution or plasma exchange have shown that occurrence of diarrhea was associated with a decrease of hydroelectrolytic absorption and/or increase of filtration through intestinal capillaries, responsible for an intestinal over-secretion [122,123]. This rapidly returns to normal after correction of the hypoalbuminemia [124]. Hypoalbuminemia also increases the length of gastric emptying, decreases intestinal motility and induces gastric and intestinal oedema. This relation between hypoalbuminemia and diarrhea is demonstrative of the relation between diarrhea and the severity of the patient's underlying disease. However, relations between hypoalbuminemia and other diarrhea-related factors (infection, starvation, shock) should be further evaluated. Malnutrition Malnutrition of children in developing countries favors the occurrence of diarrhea, and diarrhea induces malnutrition. In critically ill patients, correct feeding is supposed to favor the preservation of mucosa [19]. In these patients, acute malnutrition or nutritional deficiency frequently exists before admission to an intensive care unit and might be responsible for malabsorption and diarrhea. Experimentally, interruption of enteral feeding can lead to intestinal mucosa atrophy. In animals, the presence of nutrients in the digestive tract has a trophic action on the intestinal mucosa. The lack of nutrients induces mucous atrophy, enzymatic and hormonal dysfunction and a decrease of cellular turnover [125]. The trophic action might be due to the mechanical contact of nutrients with villosity or to the specific action of certain nutrients (long-chain triglycerides, carbohydrates, amino acids) and to biliary and pancreatic secretions. There is also probably an indirect mechanism by the means of an increased mesenteric blood flow, autonomous CNS activity and secretion of intestinal hormones. In tube fed burned patients, resumption of enteral feeding after an interruption can induce diarrhea [31]. Early enteral feeding in critically ill patients is not always possible and can favor diarrhea in certain circumstances, particularly in multiple organ failure [126]. Thus, in patients who had prolonged periods of parenteral feeding or fasting, enteral nutrition should be introduced progressively. Treatment If an etiologic factor is found, a specific treatment can be proposed. Otherwise, the treatment includes symptomatic measures and compensation of the hydroelectrolytic losses. Several examinations are useful to direct the treatment. Acceleration of intestinal transit can be searched out with the cochineal test. Stool culture is necessary to diagnose diarrhea due to a pathogenic bacterium, especially CD. CD and its toxin should be looked for in stools. Two techniques are available for CD toxin research: immunologic assay, which has a poor specificity, and cytotoxicity assay that is more specific. Bacteriologic analysis of stool and quantitative differential analysis may show an imbalance of the intestinal flora but is not always interpretable. The 'H2 breath test' after glucose charge can help to identify bacterial overgrowth. Leukocyte counts of stools are useful to distinguish an invasive diarrhea where leukocytes are numerous from a toxic diarrhea. Without contra-indication (colectasy, gangrenous colitis), colonoscopy can identify an ischemic or a pseudo-membranous colitis. The persistence of diarrhea after interruption of tube feeding evokes a secretory mechanism. Measurement of the electrolytes in stools is helpful to ad- 42 just the correction of losses. The measure of the stool osmotic gap is necessary to diagnose an osmotic diarrhea. The clearance of alpha-1 antitrypsin can help to identify an exudative protein loss, and search for lipidic and nitrogenous excretion may help to quantify malabsorption. Etiologic treatment Modification of the feeding procedure is often sufficient to suppress diarrhea: use of a continuous pump, control of nutrient sterility if the feeding technique favors bacterial overgrowth. Other simple measures can be useful, such as suppression of lactose, reduction of lipid supply, reduction of nutrition flow and osmolarity [41,127]. A severe diarrhea can make enteral nutrition inefficient. Thus, enteral feeding should be decreased and combined with parenteral feeding. In some cases, only parenteral feeding allows nutritional goals to be reached. The prevention of antibiotic associated diarrhea by Saccharomyces boulardii has been studied in a prospective trial [20]. In a group of patients treated with antibiotics, 22% of those receiving a placebo had diarrhea versus 9.5% of those receiving Saccharomyces boulardii (P = 0.0038). In 33% of patients without diarrhea, CD was found at least once and CD toxin was found in 50% of these patients. The same results were found in patients with diarrhea. A previous study [128] has demonstrated the interest of Saccharomyces boulardii for the prevention of diarrhea in critically ill patients receiving enteral feeding with or without antibiotic treatment. When a bacterial or fungal species is emerging, a specific non-absorbable antibiotherapy can be discussed. Treatment of diarrhea due to CD includes interruption of the antibiotic treatment, metronidazole (1 g/day for 10 days) or vancomycin (500 to 1000 mg/day for 10 days). Guerding [129] has found no difference between metronidazole and vancomycin in the rate of cure and relapse in 554 patients. Success rates were similar with two dosages of vancomycin (125 mg vs 500 mg 4 times a day) [130]. The rate of relapse after treatment of CD colitis is 20% [131]. If CD colitis relapses, four kinds of treatments can be proposed: same treatment again, prolonged course of oral vancomycin, use of cholestyr- amine to fix toxins, restoration of a normal flora (Lactobacillus, [132], fecal enemas, non-toxic secretor strain of CD, Saccharomyces boulardii [20]). In the study of Surawicz [133], 13 patients were treated with metronidazole in 15% of cases, vancomycin in 15%, and both antibiotics in 69% and had relapses; relapses were treated with vancomycin and Saccharomyces boulardii, and 11 patients had no relapses during a mean observation time of 6 days. Bacteriotherapy using rectal administration has also been proposed to treat relapses [134]. Reappearance of Bacteroides spp. during these treatments, when these species are absent during CD diarrhea, may help to prevent future colonisation with CD. Treatment of ischemic colitis involves correction of circulatory and hydroelectrolytic disorders, and resection of necrosis. Symptomatic treatment Modifications of enteralfeeding Hydroelectrolytic losses are usually replaced with parenteral solutions. Oral rehydratation formulas have been developed but have not been evaluated in critically ill patients. Their action is based on the principle of co-absorption of sodium and glucose or other nutrients such as alanine by villosities. These formulas improve water and electrolyte absorption and reduce the severity of symptoms [135,136]. Sodium absorption in the jejunum is correct for Na concentrations above 90 mmol/l in the lumen. Thus, a sufficient sodium concentration in the formula can be useful to prevent and correct diarrhea [137], especially if an organic or functional intestinal disease alters the reabsorption ability of the colon. The interest of a glutamine supply has recently been emphasized. During stress, glutamine produced by muscular proteolysis is an important energetic substrate for the intestinal cell. Enteral or parenteral supply ofglutamine improves intestinal function and can help to prevent mucous atrophy due to parenteral feeding or toxic enterocolitis [138,139]. Alverdy has shown [140] that bacterial translocation from gut to mesenteric nodes increased in rats receiving standard formulas compared to rats receiving glutamine. Parenteral nutrients are usually free from 43 soya polysaccharides [13,144] and Ispaghula did not show a difference in the frequency of diarrhea. Actions and metabolism of fibers differ according to their nature. Cellulose and hemicellulose are insoluble and little digested by the colon. Pectins are soluble and totally digested by colic bacteria. Gums, bulking agents and alginates are soluble but little digested. Lignin is insoluble and not digested. The mechanisms of action of fibers are complex. Soluble fibers slow down gastric emptying and modify the absorption and the metabolism of glucose, cholesterol and biliary salts. In the colon, fibers modify the consistency of stool by two mechanisms. The first is the absorption of water (2 to 30 g of water per gram of fiber); this mechanism is of little importance in man because fibers are usually digested; however, during antibiotic treatments (ampicillin and metronidazole), the weight of stool increases, suggesting a role of fibers that are little digested and can therefore absorb water [145]. The second mechanism is the absorption of water and electrolytes by the colic mucosa, favored by short-chain fatty acids. These fatty acids are produced by the anaerobic degradation of undigested fibers due to bacterial enzymes. They represent an enegertic supply, which can be used by colic bacteria or can be absorbed and bring 540 kcal per day [146]. They have atrophic action on colic mucosa [147]. Fibers that can be fermented increase the weight of stools and are able to fix several materials (drugs, ions, toxins, metals) and to modify their metabolism [148]. The action of clay (kaolin, smectite), classically used in the treatment of diarrhea, has yet to be demonstrated. It is supposed that these substances favor the intestinal adsorption of bacteria, virus, toxins and antibiotics [149]. However, kaolin was not superior to a placebo for the prevention of diarrhea and hydroelectrolytic losses in children with gastroenteritis [150]. glutamine. However, the role and the possible application of glutamine supply in the treatment of diarrhea have not been evaluated. Opiates Codeine phosphate, 30-180 mg daily, and loperamide, 4-16 mg daily, are usually recommended for the symptomatic treatment of diarrhea in intensive care units [3,24]. In patients with chronic diarrhea, codeine phosphate 60 mg and loperamide 4 to 18 mg have similar antidiarrheic action [141,142]. Although these drugs both act on opiate intestinal receptors and delay intestinal transit, their pharmacologic properties are different [141]. Codeine phosphate is well absorbed by the intestine with a concentration peak a few minutes after ingestion, acts on both central and peripheral opiate receptors and has no antisecretory action. Loperamide only produces a low plasma concentration, and its digestive action does not depend on the plasma level, due to an entero-hepatic circulation. Loperamide has also a high antisecretory action by inhibition of prostaglandins and blockade of calcium channel [143]. Diphenoxylate is an opiate that is associated with atropine and has a limited interest due to its side effects. The symptomatic treatment of diarrhea in intensive care with these drugs is empiric. Slow down of the intestinal transit may favor bacterial overgrowth and colic distension. The short small intestine syndrome is the ideal indication for the use of these drugs. Fibers Increasing the viscosity of the formula is used to slow down the transit and to improve intestinal absorption. Fiber-free formulas are homogenous and fluid and can be administered easily through small naso-gastric tubes. However, indications for fiber- free diets are few. A few recent controlled studies have suggested that fibers may be interesting to prevent or to treat diarrhea [7]. Four studies have involved intensive care patients. One study has shown a reduction of diarrhea in patients receiving fibers. However, this result should be taken with caution, due to a higher rate of CD toxin in the group of patients with a fiber-free diet [15]. The other three studies with Other medications Other drugs are under investigation [151,152], and their role in the treatment of intensive care diarrhea is not known. Prostaglandins stimulate adenylcyclase and guanylcyclase and thus induce an intestinal chloride se- 44 cretion. Their metabolism is inhibited by bismuth and by inhibitors. - Chlorpromazine (1 to 4 mg/kg) is an antagonist of calmodulin and has an antisecretory action. It reduces the volume of diarrhea during cholera from 50 to 70% [153]. Nevertheless, its use is limited by its side effects. - Somatostatin is efficient in the treatment of certain hormonal diarrheas, and might be efficient in other kinds of diarrhea, due to its action on calmodulin and on intestinal circulation and motility. - Alpha-2 agonists, such as clonidine, have an antisecretory and spasmolytic activity. Therefore, they may be intersting for the treatment of diarrhea, but their use is limited by their side effects. New alpha-2 agonists have fewer side effects and may be of interest in the treatment of diarrhea. Conclusion Occurrence of diarrhea in intensive care is often accepted with resignation. Investigations are often limited and the treatment is mainly symptomatic. The search for the pathophysiologic mechanisms responsible for diarrhea (malabsorption, abnormalities of intestinal motility, bacterial overgrowth) is often insufficient. If the clinical context in which diarrhea occurs is well known, there is still a lack of epidemiological data about diarrhea in intensive care. In practice, with a stable patient, correction of nutritional supply, withdrawal of a drug, and symptomatic measures are often sufficient to stop diarrhea. Conversely, if the patient is unstable, diarrhea is a sign of severity related to infection, ischemia and multiple organ failure; in this case, enteral nutrition often maintains diarrhea and is insufficient to provide a correct nutritional supply.
