title: Oesophageal bezoar as a complication of enteral nutrition in critically ill patients. Two case studies
abstract: Enteral nutrition has a relatively low incidence of major complications. The most common complications are mechanical problems, bronchoaspiration and diarrhoea. A rare complication associated with the use of enteral nutrition is oesophageal bezoar. A bezoar is a body of undigested and partially digested matter in the gastrointestinal tract. The main risks factors are gastric motility dysfunction and the use of opiates or sucralfate. The aim of this paper was to present two cases of oesophageal obstruction resulting from the formation of bezoars due to enteral nutrition. Both patients experienced prolonged stays in the intensive care unit and were receiving enteral nutrition, and both cases involved an obstruction of the nasogastric tube and the regurgitation of solid chunks of enteral nutrition through the mouth and the nose. Impactions of solidified enteral nutrition in the distal parts of the oesophagus were confirmed with gastroscopies. Enzymatic complexes containing papain, cellulose, pancreatin, pepsin and diastase were used to successfully dissolve the bezoars in both cases.

Two case studies Fernanda Gil-Almagro, Francisco Javier Carmona-Monge ∗ Critical Care Unit C, Hospital Universitario Fundación Alcorcón, Budapest 1, 28922 Alcorcón, Madrid, Spain KEYWORDS Bezoars; Enteral nutrition; Critical care; Intubation; Gastrointestinal Summary Enteral nutrition has a relatively low incidence of major complications. The most common complications are mechanical problems, bronchoaspiration and diarrhoea. A rare complication associated with the use of enteral nutrition is oesophageal bezoar. A bezoar is a body of undigested and partially digested matter in the gastrointestinal tract. The main risks factors are gastric motility dysfunction and the use of opiates or sucralfate. The aim of this paper was to present two cases of oesophageal obstruction resulting from the formation of bezoars due to enteral nutrition. Both patients experienced prolonged stays in the intensive care unit and were receiving enteral nutrition, and both cases involved an obstruction of the nasogastric tube and the regurgitation of solid chunks of enteral nutrition through the mouth and the nose. Impactions of solidiﬁed enteral nutrition in the distal parts of the oesophagus were conﬁrmed with gastroscopies. Enzymatic complexes containing papain, cellulose, pancreatin, pepsin and diastase were used to successfully dissolve the bezoars in both cases. © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Implications for Clinical Practice • Oesophageal bezoar is a rare complication in critically ill care patients related to long periods in dorsal decubitus, intestinal hipomolity and orotracheal intubation. • It may be difﬁcult to identify its appearance early due to the absence of speciﬁc symptomatology. • Treatment with pancreatic enzymes was effective in the treatment of oesophagic bezoar in both of our cases ∗ Corresponding author. Tel.: +34 91 621 97 51. E-mail address: fjcarmona@fhalcorcon.es (F.J. Carmona-Monge). http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.iccn.2015.08.003 0964-3397/© 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 30 Introduction Enteral nutrition is the most physiological method for providing nutrition to critical patients with functional gastrointestinal tracts (Kreymann et al., 2006). Enteral nutrition is given via the insertion of a gastric or intestinal tube. Enteral nutrition has a relatively low incidence of major complications when compared to parenteral nutrition (Marik and Hooper, 2013). Among the complications that may appear, mechanical problems, bronchoaspiration of gastric content, regurgitation, diarrhoea and metabolic alterations have been observed (Huynh et al., 2013). A type of complication that appears with a very low incidence when enteral nutrition is administered at the gastric level is oesophageal bezoar. A bezoar is a body of undigested and partially digested matter in the gastrointestinal tract (i.e., the oesophagus, stomach or small intestine). Bezoars are classiﬁed according to their components, and the following types can be distinguished (Marcus et al., 2010; Robles et al., 1994): phytobezoars, trichobezoars and pharmacobezoars. There are different risk factors that may contribute to the appearance of oesophageal bezoars in critical care patients on enteral nutrition. Among the most frequently identiﬁed risk factors are the following: the presence of disorders of the anatomy and motility of the gastrointestinal tract (Kim et al., 2010; Shah et al., 1997); the appearance of gastroesophageal reﬂux, which is more frequent when a nasogastric catheter is used, and the patient has been in the dorsal decubitus position (Douzinas et al., 2006; Marcus et al., 2010); the use of dietetic formulas containing high concentrations of casein (Cremer and Gelfand, 1996; GarciaLuna et al., 1997; Marcuard and Perkins, 1988; Turner et al., 1991); and the use of common intensive care unit drugs that lead to hypomotility and delay the gastric emptying process (Kesarwani et al., 2010; Nguyen et al., 2008). Bezoars are formed following the reﬂux of the acidic contents of the stomach to the oesophagus, which causes the proteins in the enteral nutrition formula precipitate (Hofstetter and Allen, 1992; Turner et al., 1991). The majority of precipitates self-resolve, but occasionally precipitation continues until the oesophagus is obstructed. The aim of this study was to present two cases of oesophageal obstruction due to the formation of bezoars of enteral nutrition. Case presentation Case 1 A 66-year-old man with coma and hypotension had been in the intensive care unit for an extended period. Due to difﬁculty in the discontinuation of mechanical ventilation, a percutaneous tracheotomy was performed. Six days after his admission to the unit, the administration of enteral nutrition with a high protein formula (Isosource Protein® ) was initiated, but had to be suspended after 13 days due to diarrhoea. Parenteral nutrition was initiated (Structokabiven® ) and continued for 16 days. After the diarrhoea had resolved, enteral nutrition was gradually reintroduced. The patient then continued with high-protein enteral nutrition for 85 F. Gil-Almagro, F.J. Carmona-Monge additional days. After 120 days in the unit and after more than three months of the administration of enteral nutrition without complication, an obstruction occurred in the nasogastric tube. A 16FG Levin tube had been in situ for 104 days (as per our protocol, the positions of nasogastric tubes are checked daily, but replacement with a new tube is only performed when there is a problem with the existing tube or when a smaller feeding tube needs to be inserted). The tube was removed, but it was subsequently impossible to place a new catheter. Regurgitation of chunks of the enteral nutrition through the mouth and nostrils was observed. At that time, the patient was receiving no drugs through the nasogastric catheter but was receiving intravenous opiates. Given the impossibility of inserting the new tube, a gastroscopy was performed. Impaction of solidiﬁed enteral nutrition in the distal part of the oesophagus was observed. Attempts were made to remove the obstruction with an endoscope, but it was impossible to completely unblock the oesophageal lumen. Pineapple juice and water ﬂushes were used during the gastroscopy in an attempt to dissolve the impacted mass, but this procedure was unsuccessful (Figs. 1 and 2). Because it was impossible to unblock the oesophagus mechanically, pharmacological treatment with an enzymatic complex containing papain, cellulose, pancreatin, pepsin and diastase (Digestomen complex® ) was initiated. This enzymatic solution was administered through a nasogastric tube that was placed in the proximal part of the bezoar. After the complete dose was administered, an additional endoscopy was performed, and a clean oesophagus with no evidence of the bezoar was observed. A new nasogastric tube was placed without any further problems, and standard enteral nutrition was initiated with no complications. Case 2 A 67-year-old patient had been taken to the intensive care unit due to acute respiratory failure in the context of septic shock secondary to pyelonephritis. The patient remained in the unit for 170 days. Due to difﬁculty weaning the patient from mechanical ventilation, a percutaneous tracheotomy was necessary. The patient was given normal protein enteral nutrition (Isosource Standar® ) for 11 days without incidence. Due to an improvement in his clinical condition, the nasogastric tube was removed, and an oral diet was initiated. Worsening of his clinical condition necessitated the resumption of mechanical ventilation. A new nasogastric tube was inserted, and enteral nutrition was reinitiated (Diben Standar® ). In addition to the enteral nutrition, the patient was receiving only lactulose (10 g every 24 hours) through the nasogastric tube. All other pharmacological treatments were administered intravenously. Seven days after the initiation of the enteral nutrition, an obstruction in the patient’s nasogastric tube occurred (the tube was a 16FG Levin and had been inserted seven days previously). In the attempt to replace the tube, it was difﬁcult to insert the new tube through the proximal zone of the oesophagus. Regurgitation of chunks of enteral nutrition was observed. Until that time, the patient had not suffered nausea, vomiting or any form of pain. Bezoar as a complication of enteral nutrition Figures 1 and 2 31 Images acquired during the gastroscopies of the patients. Enteral nutrition impactions (bezoars) can be observed. A gastroscopy was then performed, and the presence of a bezoar occupying the entire longitudinal extent of the oesophageal lumen was detected. There was no enteral nutrition in the stomach. Deimpaction by fragmentation with an endoscope was impossible; therefore, the previously described enzymatic treatment was initiated a nasogastric tube that was placed in the proximal zone of the bezoar. After the treatment was completed, new endoscopy was performed. There were no traces of enteral nutrition in the oesophagus, and an oral diet was given to the patient until he was discharged to the ward. Discussion Impaction of enteral nutrition at the oesophageal level with consequent bezoar formation is uncommon, and few cases have been described in the literature (Marcus et al., 2010). Several factors seem to be involved in the pathogenesis of bezoars. In the case of critically ill patients, additional factors may be present, such as the use of opioids and sedatives that elicit hypomotility of the muscular ﬁbres, which delays gastric evacuation. Additionally, the administration of sucralfate is associated with the formation of speciﬁc insoluble substances from insoluble complexes with protein components in enteral nutrition (Carrougher and Barrilleaux 1991; Krupp et al. 1995). In such patients, the severity of the pathology, the long periods of time that they remain in dorsal decubitus position, and the need for nasogastric tube to be in place for long periods of time must also be considered (Ibanez et al., 1992). In both cases we used a 16FG nasogastric tube, although this may be not a very common practice, it makes evacuation of the stomach easier in cases where this may be necessary. However, the diameter of the tube has not been listed in the literature as a predisposing factor for oesophageal bezoar, and the size of the tube did not seem to have a role in the development of this condition in our patients. Regarding enteral nutrition, several authors have proven with in vitro studies that enteral formulas containing casein require less than one minute to solidify when acidiﬁed to pH < 5.0. Conversely, formulas containing other proteic components can be subjected to pH values as low as 1.0 without precipitation occurring (Turner et al., 1991). In the cases of our two patients, both had artiﬁcial airways due to difﬁculties in the discontinuation of mechanical ventilation. These patients had nasogastric tubes in place for long periods of time, were being given opioids (either at the time or in the days preceding the appearance of the complications) and were being fed with enteral nutrition formulas containing casein. Notably, neither of the two patients exhibited any symptoms that could have led us to suspect the formation of a bezoar. These bezoars were unexpectedly identiﬁed after obstructions of the nasogastric tubes necessitated replacement, but it became evident that the reinsertions of new tubes were impossible due to the bezoars. The ﬁnal diagnoses were made via digestive endoscopies, and in both cases, the impaction of the enteral nutrition occupied nearly the entire length of the oesophagus. The removal of a bezoar is typically a difﬁcult task, and when this task is performed via endoscopy, several sessions are usually needed for complete removal (Marcus et al., 2010). In the literature, different types of substances have been used to remove oesophageal bezoars, including effervescent liquids (Coca-Cola® ) (Irgau and Fulda, 1995; Yaqub et al., 2012), N-acetylcysteine and gastrograﬁn spray (Katsanos et al., 2010) and pancreatic enzymes (Gupta et al., 2001). In our patients, mechanical unblocking was not possible due to the large sizes of the bezoars. The dissolutions of the masses were achieved with an enzymatic complex that acted by breaking the glycosidic bonds of the cellulose and transforming it into glucose. This enzymatic treatment did not produce any adverse reactions in the patients. Among the clinical practice recommendations for critically ill patients, we would like to emphasise the importance of routinely ﬂushing the feeding tube and checking the gastric residual volumes to check for tube blockage and delayed gastric emptying. This procedure is particularly important in cases with slow infusion rates (42—21 mL/hour) because the risk of precipitation increases at lower rates, which in turn increases the risk of obstruction. It is also advisable to adopt 32 measures to reduce gastroesophageal reﬂux, such as elevating the head of the bed to 30◦ and inserting thin nasogastric tubes whenever possible. The use of prokinetics to improve intestinal motility may help reduce gastroesophageal ﬂux and accelerate the absorption of some drugs (e.g., metheclopramid and erythromycin). In contrast, the substitution of a nasojejunal tube for a nasogastric tube may be useful for patients who tend to have gastroesophageal reﬂux or slowed gastric emptying and for patients who will be fed with enteral nutrition for extended durations. The complications of the presence of an oesophageal bezoar may have serious consequences for the patient (e.g., bronchoaspiration and oesophagitis). Due to the low incidence and potential adverse effects of bezoars, it is important for nursing professionals to identify them as soon as possible to prevent the potential appearance of ulterior complications. title: Pectin-containing liquid enteral nutrition for critical care: A historical control and propensity score matched study
abstract: INTRODUCTION. Diarrhea and vomiting are common complications in critical care 1). Approaches have been undertaken to make liquid enteral nutrition LEN solid and thereby decrease them 2), however, there was no study that semi-solid LEN could reduce diarrhea. Herein, Pectin is dietary fiber that changes to a solid in the stomach 3). Pectin-containing liquid enteral nutrition PCLEN would mitigate vomiting and diarrhea. OBJECTIVES. This study compared PCLEN with traditional LEN TLEN for enteral feeding failure, diarrhea and vomiting rates in critical care. METHODS. We used TLEN as the primary LEN at the emergency and critical care center in Hitachi General Hospital. We adopted PCLEN as the primary LEN from 2014. During 2012-2016, 954 patients admitted to intensive care units and emergency wards were given PCLEN or TLEN. We conducted propensity score matching for 693 eligible patients for age, sex, and organ dysfunctions for six organs. RESULTS. We included 199 PCLEN patients and 199 TLEN patients. Severity was higher in the PCLEN group: APACHE2 15.3 ¬± 6.8 vs. 13.6 ¬± 5.4 (p=0.0055) and SOFA 6.2 ¬± 3.8 vs. 5.1¬±3.3 (p=0.0029), respectively. The enteral nutrition failure rate was significantly lower for PCLEN: 10.6 % than for TLEN: 17.6 % (p=0.043). The GRV at 24 hr was slightly lower for PCLEN: 7.9 ¬± 24.3 ml than for TLEN: 25.1 ¬± 63.0 ml (p=0.017). Although the vomiting events were not different, the diarrhea incidence rates were 28.1% vs. 38.2% (p=0.033), and the incidence rates of nosocomial pneumonia were 4.5% and 9.6% (p=0.048). For PCLEN, the enteral nutrition failure rates were not different for patients with gastric acid inhibitors and without them. CONCLUSIONS. PCLEN can be used effectively for critically ill patients irrespective of the use of gastric acid inhibitors. It can decrease the incidence of enteral nutrition failure and diarrhea in critical care.

full_text: Asia Pac J Clin Nutr 2019;28(1):57-63 57 Original Article Pectin-containing liquid enteral nutrition for critical care: a historical control and propensity score matched study Kensuke Nakamura MD, PhD1, Ryota Inokuchi MD, PhD2, Kazutaka Fukushima MD1, Hiromu Naraba MD1, Yuji Takahashi MD1, Tomohiro Sonoo MD1, Hideki Hashimoto MD1, Kent Doi MD, PhD2, Naoto Morimura MD, PhD2 1 2 Department of Emergency and Critical Care Medicine, Hitachi General Hospital, Ibaraki, Japan Department of Emergency and Critical Care Medicine, The University of Tokyo Hospital, Tokyo, Japan Background and Objectives: Pectin-containing liquid enteral nutrition (PCLEN) contains pectin, which becomes solid in the stomach and therefore mitigates vomiting and diarrhea. Its efficacy for use in critical care medicine was evaluated. Methods and Study Design: We used liquid enteral nutrition (LEN) (traditional LEN (TLEN)) as the primary LEN at the emergency and critical care center. We adopted PCLEN as the primary LEN from 2014. During 2012–2016, 954 patients admitted to intensive care units and emergency wards were given PCLEN or TLEN. We conducted propensity score matching for 693 eligible patients for age, sex, and organ dysfunctions for six organs. Results: We included 199 PCLEN patients and 199 TLEN patients. Severity was higher in the PCLEN group. The enteral nutrition failure rate was significantly lower for PCLEN than for TLEN. The diarrhea incidence rates were 28.1% vs 38.2% (p=0.033), and the incidence rates of nosocomial pneumonia were 4.5% and 9.6% (p=0.048). For PCLEN, the enteral nutrition failure rates were not different for patients with gastric acid inhibitors and without them. Conclusions: PCLEN can be used effectively for critically ill patients irrespective of the use of gastric acid inhibitors. It can decrease the incidence of enteral nutrition failure and diarrhea. Key Words: enteral nutrition, critical care, pectin, diarrhea, intensive care INTRODUCTION Liquid enteral nutrition (LEN) has been reported as important for critical care medicine. We recommend starting its administration as soon as possible in clinical practice.1 Early enteral nutrition is physiologically advantageous for immunity and the gut tract system.2 Nevertheless, various phenomena resist enteral feeding in critically ill patients: enteral nutrition failure often occurs.3 Diarrhea and vomiting are the main reasons for enteral nutrition failure.4 The recent widespread use of antibiotics reportedly exacerbates diarrhea events.5 Severely ill patients often cannot be relieved with enteral nutrition. Diarrhea and vomiting are common complications affecting chronic patients and those undergoing critical care. Therefore, approaches have been undertaken to make LEN solid and thereby decrease gastroesophageal reflux, vomiting, and diarrhea. Some LEN with a semi-solid consistency are sold. Although some studies have examined the efficacy of semi-solid LEN against gastroesophageal reflux,6-8 no large clinical study has been reported. Especially, whether diarrhea can be decreased or not has not been studied. Pectin is dietary fiber that changes to a solid by forming a bridged structure when exposed to calcium ion.9-11 Pectin-containing liquid enteral nutrition (PCLEN), which can be given in a liquid condition, is expected to become solid in gastric acid pH. In Japan, PCLEN has been marketed as HINE E-GEL® from 2014. When given as a diet, the solidification can occur without risk of tube occlusion and can be expected to inhibit diarrhea and vomiting. The use of PCLEN is increasing in chronic enteral feeding patients in Japan. Nevertheless, no report has described a study examining the efficacy of PCLEN. Especially, no reported study has examined the use of PCLEN in critical care medicine. The efficacy of pectin alone reportedly decreases diarrhea in a small randomized study in critically ill patients. However, no significant difference was found between the pectin group and the fiber-free group.12 Because solidification of PCLEN occurs because of the gastric acid pH, it is necessary to examine for PCLEN whether gastric acid inhibitors weaken solidification or not. HINE E-GEL® is expected to be solid under acidic conditions with pH less than 5. Proton pump inhibitor Corresponding Author: Dr Kensuke Nakamura, Department of Emergency and Critical Care Medicine, Hitachi General Hospital, 2-1-1, Jonan-cho, Hitachi, Ibaraki, 317-0077, Japan. Tel: +81-294-23-1111; Fax: +81-294-23-8317 Email: mamashockpapashock@yahoo.co.jp Manuscript received 08 August 2018. Initial review completed 22 August 2018. Revision accepted 30 August 2018. doi: 10.6133/apjcn.201903_28(1).0009 58 K Nakamura, R Inokuchi, K Fukushima, H Naraba, Y Takahashi, T Sonoo et al (PPI) or H2 blockers are often given in critical care medicine applications because of the risk of gastrointestinal bleeding.1 Although gastric pH differs according to a patient’s condition and the dose or frequency of the administered gastric acid inhibitor, gastric pH would temporarily be higher than 5 with gastric acid inhibitors.13-15 Solidification of PCLEN might therefore be difficult. For that reason, it is necessary to examine the efficacy of PCLEN in patients who are given gastric acid inhibitors. We used Mei Balance HP1.0®, which contains milk protein, as the primary LEN (traditional LEN (TLEN)) and adopted HINE E-GEL® from 2014 as the primary LEN for use in emergency and critical care centers. As described herein, we conducted a retrospective, historical control, and propensity score matched study to compare outcomes related to enteral nutrition between patients given TLEN and PCLEN for critical care treatment. We conducted subgroup analyses of patients with and without gastric acid inhibitors. METHODS We used Mei Balance HP1.0®, TLEN, as primary LEN at the Emergency and Critical Care Center of Hitachi General Hospital. We adopted HINE E-GEL®, PCLEN, for use as the primary LEN from May 2014. Mei Balance HP1.0® is LEN that contains milk protein 1.0 kcal/mL, osmotic pressure 420 mOsm/L and a protein, fat, carbohydrate balance of 20%, 22.5%, and 57.5%, respectively. It contains 1.2 g dietary fiber per 100 kcal. HINE E-GEL® is LEN that contains soy and collagen peptide protein 0.8 kcal/mL, osmotic pressure 360 mOsm/L and a protein, fat, and carbohydrate balance of 16%, 20%, and 64%, respectively. It contains 1.4 g dietary fiber per 100 kcal, which includes 0.9 g pectin. The initiation of enteral nutrition was performed in both groups as explained below. Gastric residual volume GRV was evaluated routinely in our intensive care units. Enteral feeding was started via a nasogastric tube when there was no ileus condition and recent GRV was less than 500 mL after the second day from admission. We started LEN at the flow rate of 10 - 20 mL/h, increased by 10 - 20 mL/h to reach the necessary calorie goals at every 8 hours when the GRV was less than 500 mL. When patients presented refractory diarrhea, needed the restriction of water or protein, or the higher fat balance was appropriate for respiratory failure or severe diabetes or respiratory failure, the other specific LEN were chosen. Selection of the other LEN did not differ during the study period. This study was approved by the Ethics Review Board of our hospital (2013-48). Patient selection An outline of patient selection is presented in Figure 1. Patients who were admitted to the Intensive Care Unit and the Emergency Ward at the Emergency and Critical Care Center of Hitachi General Hospital from October 2012 to August 2016 and to whom PCLEN or TLEN were given were included. Age <15 years old, patients who were given both PCLEN and TLEN in different periods, and patients who were given LEN except via nasogastric tube (for example via percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy PEG or intestine duodenal tube) were excluded. Propensity score matching was conducted for patients of TLEN and PCLEN groups by age, sex, and the presence of organ dysfunction for six organs at admission: 1) cardiovascular dysfunction that required initiation of noradrenaline and/or dopamine on day 0; 2) respiratory dysfunction that required postsurgical continuous mechanical ventilation; 3) renal dysfunction that necessitated initiation of intermittent acute hemodialysis or continuous renal replacement therapy on day 0, except for maintenance hemodialysis patients; 4) hepatic dysfunction as a comorbidity at admission for recorded “liver failure,” except for liver cirrhosis patients; 5) hematologic dysfunction that required platelet concentrate transfusion on day 0; and 6) neurological dysfunction of 100 on the Japan Coma Scale score, which is equivalent to scores of 6– 9 on the Glasgow Coma Scale, or greater severity. Finally, the matched TLEN and PCLEN patients were analyzed. This organ dysfunction score matching was obtained from the Japanese Diagnosis Procedure Combination inpatient database.16 Outcome measurements As the primary outcome, the enteral nutrition failure rate was analyzed. Enteral nutrition failure was defined as the discontinuation of enteral feeding or change to the other LEN by trouble related to enteral nutrition. 30-day survival, length of ICU stay and hospital stay, duration of enteral feeding, gastric residual volume GRV, incidence rate of vomiting, diarrhea, nosocomial pneumonia, the given LEN amounts, and calories per day on the seventh day were analyzed as secondary outcomes. GRV was analyzed at 2 hr and 24 hr after TLEN or PCLEN was started. Vomiting was recorded when observed during administration of TLEN or PCLEN. Diarrhea was defined as frequent stool more than three times per day and stool condition which met Bristol Stool Chart 5–7.17 Nosocomial pneumonia was diagnosed by infiltration in chest X ray and 2 positive in 1) >38°C fever, 2) white blood cell counts <4000 or >11,000/μl, or 3) purulent sputum. Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) was included. In subgroup analysis, the PCLEN group was divided to two groups with or without PPI or H2 blocker. Their respective outcomes were compared with that of TLEN. PPI/H2 blocker was counted positively when intravenous PPI or H2 blocker was given at the time of PCLEN start. Statistical analysis Propensity score matching was used to adjust for differences in age, sex, and severity of the condition at admission between TLEN and PCLEN. First, the propensity score was estimated. The log odds of the probability that a patient received PMX treatment was modeled for potential confounders: age, sex, and the six organ dysfunctions defined above. A one-to-one matched analysis using nearest-neighbor matching was performed based on the estimated propensity score of each patient. A match occurred when one patient in the PCLEN group had an estimated score within 0.25 SDs of another in the TLEN group. Differences were assessed by application of Student ttests, paired t-tests, chi-square tests, and one-way analysis Pectin-containing liquid enteral nutrition of variance between the TLEN and PCLEN group. Significant differences were then assessed using Tukey–Kramer and Steel–Dwass methods for multiple comparisons. The 30-day survival, length of ICU and hospital stay and duration of enteral nutrition were analyzed using log-rank tests. All statistical analyses were performed using statistical software (JMP 10; SAS Institute Inc.). Results are expressed as mean ± standard deviation values. p-values of <0.05 were inferred as significant. RESULTS During the study period, 954 patients were admitted to intensive care units or emergency wards and were given PCLEN or TLEN. From them, 261 patients were excluded according to exclusion criteria. Propensity score matching was performed for the remaining 693 patients for age, sex, and severity of condition at admission between TLEN and PCLEN. Finally, 199 PCLEN group patients and 199 TLEN group patients were included and analyzed. A patient selection outline is depicted in Figure 1. Basic characteristics are presented in Table 1. Although renal failure was higher in PCLEN group patients, other basic diseases were not found to be significantly different. Invasive treatments such as mechanical ventilation or hemodialysis were not different between groups. Vital signs and laboratory findings on admission were not significantly different. Severity was higher in the PCLEN group than in the TLEN group: Acute Physiology and Chronic Health Evaluation (APACHE) II 15.3±6.8 vs 13.6±5.4 (p=0.0055) and sequential organ failure assessment (SOFA) 6.2±3.8 vs 5.1±3.3 (p=0.0029), respectively. Outcomes for both groups are shown in Table 2. The enteral nutrition failure rate, at which enteral nutrition should be discontinued because of enteral feeding difficulty, was 10.6% for PCLEN and 17.6% for TLEN. PCLEN was associated with significantly better results (p=0.043). The enteral feeding durations were not different, as shown by the Kaplan–Meier curve (Figure 2A). 59 The 30-day survival rates and length of ICU and hospital stay were not significantly different. The GRV at 24 hr was slightly lower for PCLEN: 7.9±24.3 mL for PCLEN vs 25.1±63.0 mL for TLEN (p=0.017). Although vomiting events were not different, diarrhea events were lower for PCLEN, 28.1% for PCLEN vs 38.2% for TLEN (p=0.033). The nosocomial pneumonia rate including VAP was also lower for PCLEN: 4.5% for PCLEN vs 9.6% for TLEN (p=0.048). Enteral nutrition amounts and enteral calories per day on the seventh day were not significantly different. We conducted subgroup analysis of PCLEN in patients with and without PPI/H2 blocker (Table 3). In the PCLEN group, 122 patients were given PPI or H2 blocker; 77 patients were not. The incidence of enteral nutrition failure was not different for PCLEN between groups of patients with and without PPI/H2 blocker. Incidences for both groups were significantly lower than for TLEN. The groups’ survival rates were not significantly different. GRV at 24 hr was slightly lower for PCLEN with/without PPI/H2 blocker than for TLEN. Vomiting events and the nosocomial pneumonia rate were not different. Diarrhea was significantly lowest, 22.1% for PCLEN without PPI/H2 blocker. Meanwhile, incidence of diarrhea was not different between with and without PPI/H2 blocker for TLEN (Figure 2B). These results suggest that the diarrhea examined in this study was not associated as a side effect of PPI/H2 blocker, but that it might be related to pectin solidification. DISCUSSION This study analyzed the efficacy of PCLEN use in critical care medicine. The enteral nutrition success rate was significantly higher for PCLEN than for TLEN. Although vomiting events were not different, diarrhea, and nosocomial pneumonia events were significantly lower for PCLEN. The enteral nutrition success rates obtained with and without gastric acid inhibitors were not different. The diarrhea event rate was lowest for PCLEN without a gas- Figure 1. Diarrhea is an important problem occurring during critical care.18 The diarrhea incidence rate has been reported as 15–50% in critically ill patients, differing according to the definition of diarrhea.19-21 Critical care physicians often struggle with its management. Various factors cause diarrhea in critically ill patients, one of which is enteral nutrition.18 Although no cause-and-effect link has been clarified for any mechanism, long-term enteral nutrition, high-osmolality, low-fiber formula, bolus feeding, overly rapid increase to the target, and postpyloric enteral nutrition have been regarded as exacerbating the risk of Pectin-containing liquid enteral nutrition 61 Figure 2. Incidences for both groups were significantly lower than for TLEN. The groups’ survival rates were not significantly different. GRV at 24 hr was slightly lower for PCLEN with/without PPI/H2 blocker than for TLEN. Vomiting events and the nosocomial pneumonia rate were not different. Diarrhea was significantly lowest in PCLEN without PPI/H2 blocker. * p-values of <0.05 were inferred as significant. diarrhea.22,23 Diarrhea can be a reason for the discontinuation of enteral feeding in critical care. Physicians should often consider strategies to choose a variety of LEN that supports control or prevention of diarrhea. Results of the present study are important: PCLEN can decrease diarrhea and enteral nutrition failure, suggesting that PCLEN can be beneficial for critical care medicine. A number of studies have been conducted to examine whether solidification of LEN can inhibit difficulties related to enteral feeding, such as vomiting and diarrhea. Semi-solid LEN, which is already a semi-solid configuration before administration, reportedly inhibits gastroesophageal reflux.7 One report described that gastroesophageal reflux was decreased in clinical PEG patients.6 Another study of clinical PEG patients showed that nosocomial pneumonia was decreased.8 However, one report described that gastroesophageal reflux was not changed by semi-solid LEN.24 Therefore, the efficacy of semi-solid LEN has remained controversial. Moreover, although solidification of LEN theoretically inhibits diar- rhea, no study has examined semi-solid LEN for diarrhea. One case report described the possibility of semi-solid LEN to inhibit diarrhea.25 Pectin, which is contained in PCLEN used for the present study, is expected to be transformed to a solid by gastric acid pH in the stomach and to present the benefit of solidification. Because its solidification is stronger than that of semi-solid LEN, it can prevent LEN from inflowing rapidly into the duodenum and intestine.9-11 It is expected to inhibit diarrhea. Diarrhea was least frequent with PCLEN in this study when no gastric acid inhibitor was used, allowing pectin to contribute to diarrhea inhibition. PCLEN is liquid in the gastric tube, presenting little risk of tube occlusion. In recent critical care medical treatments, gastric acid inhibitors are often given to patients, possibly decreasing the solidification of PCLEN. However, PCLEN had a higher enteral nutrition success rate than TLEN in this study, irrespective of the presence of gastric acid inhibitors. These results were regarded as attributable to pectin 62 K Nakamura, R Inokuchi, K Fukushima, H Naraba, Y Takahashi, T Sonoo et al Figure 3. Conceptual diagram. Liquid enteral nutrition with pectin can be given safely through feeding tube. Pectin changes to a solid by forming a bridged structure with calcium ion under acid PH in the stomach. Effective solidification would be associated with decreased gastric residual volume, diarrhea and vomiting (?), and furthermore associated with decreased enteral nutrition failure and nosocomial pneumonia solidification under gastric acid inhibitors and by other characteristics of the PCLEN that was used, other than pectin. By the former reason, the degree to which gastric pH is shifted by gastric acid inhibitors depends strongly on the types and doses of PPI or H2 blockers that were given. When a large dose of PPI is given, pH is increased considerably.14,15 However, a typical dose of PPI/H2 blockers often does not achieve pH >5; alternatively, no condition of pH >5 exists.13 For the latter reason, differences contributing to results obtained between TLEN and PCLEN used for this study are the type of protein and slight difference of concentration, other than pectin: TLEN contained milk protein; PCLEN contained soy protein and collagen peptide. Although both milk and soy can cause diarrhea as one symptom of food allergy,26 the prevalence and new-onset rates of these food allergies are low in adults. More than 90% of Asian and North American people reportedly have lactose intolerance, which differs among races.27 Milk does not always cause diarrhea in people with lactose intolerance. However, a meal containing lactose can cause diarrhea under some conditions.28 Soy protein or collagen peptide might be better than milk protein for critical care in such countries. In this study, PCLEN was 0.8 kcal/mL, 360 mOsm/L; TLEN was 1.0 kcal/mL, 420 mOsm/L. These differences of concentration and osmotic pressure might affect the results. This study includes some limitations. Although propensity score matching and historical control were conducted, the study might include some biases because it was a retrospective study. One possible source of bias is the study period during which each LEN was given. As described above, some differences between PCLEN and TLEN existed other than pectin. It was difficult to analyze the effects of respective factors. Moreover, the definition of diarrhea is difficult, as discussed in recent reports.18 Certification of diarrhea was difficult in this study because of its retrospective nature. In subgroup analysis, patients of the gastric acid inhibitors subgroup might have presented more severity than those without inhibitors. Their basic characteristics might have been different. Conclusions Compared to TLEN, PCLEN was associated with lower incidence of diarrhea and nosocomial pneumonia. PCLEN was also associated with the enteral nutrition success irrespective of the patient use of gastric acid inhibitors. Diarrhea was inhibited most for patients with PCLEN but without gastric acid inhibitors. For critical care, PCLEN can be used effectively for enteral nutrition via a nasogastric tube. title: Pharmaconutrition for the obese, critically ill patient
abstract: Obesity is an epidemic that affects approximately 30% of the adult population in the United States. The prevalence of obesity in the critically ill seems to correlate with the rise in obesity in the general population. Delivery of standard enteral nutrition (EN) to patients in the intensive care unit (ICU) has been shown to decrease infectious complications. Obese ICU patients may be at increased risk for infections, ICU length of stay, and ventilation requirements compared to the nonobese. Pharmaconutrition has been shown to decrease many of these negative ICU outcomes. Because of obesity-associated increased ICU risk, provision of certain pharmaconutrients should be considered in obese patients requiring EN therapy. This review examines the evidence for specific nutrients such as green tea, curcumin, sulforaphane, poly-unsaturated fatty acids, L-arginine, L-citrulline, L-leucine, protein, probiotics, magnesium, medium-chain triglycerides, and zinc for the treatment of obesity. These nutrients could potentially be added to current EN formulas or provided as supplements.
source_review: Safety of using enteral nutrition formulations containing dietary fiber in hospitalized critical care patients

This review examines the evidence for specific nutrients such as green tea, curcumin, sulforaphane, poly-unsaturated fatty acids, L-arginine, L-citrulline, L-leucine, protein, probiotics, magnesium, medium-chain triglycerides, and zinc for the treatment of obesity. These nutrients could potentially be added to current EN formulas or provided as supplements. (JPEN J Parenter Enteral Nutr. 2011;35:60S-72S) Obesity is an epidemic that affects approximately 30% of the adult population in the United States. The prevalence of obesity in the critically ill seems to correlate with the rise in obesity in the general population. Delivery of standard enteral nutrition (EN) to patients in the intensive care unit (ICU) has been shown to decrease infectious complications. Obese ICU patients may be at increased risk for infections, ICU length of stay, and ventilation requirements compared to the nonobese. Pharmaconutrition has been shown to decrease many of these negative ICU outcomes. Because of obesity-associated increased ICU risk, provision of Keywords: obesity; probiotics; prebiotics; pharmaconutrition D function, and a preexisting low-grade inflammatory state.7-9 We propose the use of pharmaconutrition in obese ICU patients based on these observations.6,7,10-12 elivery of standard enteral nutrition (EN) in the intensive care unit (ICU) has been shown to improve patient outcomes (such as infectious complications) compared to parenteral nutrition (PN).1,2 Specialized formulas containing immune-modulating elements such as L-arginine, poly-unsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs), and RNA fragments have been used in ICU and trauma patients. These elements, described in the literature as immunonutrition or pharmaconutrition, have been given in pharmacologic doses to affect clinical outcomes. These specialized formulas have been shown to reduce nosocomial infection, duration of mechanical ventilation, ICU and hospital length of stay (LOS), and organ failure compared to standard EN formulas.3 Critically ill obese patients are at increased risk for these negative clinical outcomes and thus may stand to benefit from receipt of a pharmaconutrition formula.4-6 Obesity is associated with numerous physiological problems that complicate ICU care, including ventilation difficulties, hyperglycemia, immobility, thromboembolic disease, impaired hepatic Oxidative Stress and Obesity Oxidative stress is thought to play an imperative role in both the chronic complications associated with obesity (eg, diabetes mellitus) and the poor outcomes associated with the critically ill obese patient. For this reason, pharmaconutrition interventions should include both direct (eg, vitamin E) and indirect antioxidants (eg, nuclear factor-erythroid-2-related factor [Nrf2] inducers) that offer protection against oxidative and electrophile toxicities. The ability of docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) and eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) to suppress lipopolysaccharide (LPS)–induced inflammation has been described recently.13 Specific amino acids such as L-arginine and glutamine have been shown to decrease weight, improve hyperglycemia, and increase organ perfusion.14-18 Many of these agents have been shown to be of particular use in obesity-induced inflammation by improving cytokine dysfunction. Bowel-directed therapies such as probiotics, zinc, and medium-chain triglycerides have the potential to attenuate gut-derived endotoxemia and systemic inflammation. This report reviews the evidence of specific pharmaconutrients that potentially could be used to improve patient outcomes in obese critically ill patients, given as part of an enteral formulation or as an exogenous supplement. From the 1Department of Medicine; 2Division of Gastroenterology, Hepatology, and Nutrition, University of Louisville School of Medicine, Louisville, Kentucky; and 3Division of General Internal Medicine, Mayo Clinic, Rochester, Minnesota Received for publication February 28, 2011; accepted for publication March 29, 2011. Address correspondence to: Stephen A. McClave, MD, Division of Gastroenterology/Hepatology, University of Louisville School of Medicine, 550 South Jackson St, Louisville, KY 40202; e-mail: samcclave@louisville.edu. 60S Pharmaconutrition for the Obese, Critically Ill Patient / Hurt et al 61S Inducers of Antioxidant Response Elements Nrf2 is a transcription factor that plays a crucial role in regulation of phase II detoxification and antioxidant gene expression. Nrf2 is strongly expressed in numerous tissues, including adipose tissue.19 Kelch-like ECH-associated protein 1 (Keap1) acts as a negative regulator of Nrf2 by binding and repressing its ability to enter the nucleus. In the absence of Keap1, Nrf2 is translocated into the nucleus and forms a heterodimer with other transcription factors. This complex binds to the regulatory sequence, termed antioxidant response elements (ARE) or electrophile response elements (EpRE). These regulatory sequences are located in the promoter region of genes encoding various antioxidant and phase II detoxifying enzymes.20 Certain dietary agents can induce Nrf2 activity through various mechanisms. Agents such as curcumin, sulforaphane, epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), quercetin, ajoene, and liquiritigenin have actions that include (but are not limited to) increasing the activity of Nrf2. Curcumin Curcumin, derived from the spice tumeric (Curcuma longa), is a diferuloylmethane. More than 300 reports regarding the benefits of curcumin on obesity and obesity-related complications have been published. By activating Nrf2-signaling pathways, curcumin downregulates many of the known proinflammatory cytokine/chemokine culprits in obesity-related illness (tumor necrosis factor [TNF]–α, interleukin [IL]–6, resistin, leptin, and monocyte chemotactic protein-1).21,22 Curcumin is known to activate peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor (PPAR)–γ and upregulate adiponectin.23,24 In vitro studies have shown that curcumin suppresses nuclear transcription factor–κB (NF-κB) activity.25 Curcumin suppresses adipocyte differentiation and inhibits fatty acid synthase.21,22 Finally, recent evidence of synergistic anti-inflammatory effects of low doses of curcumin in combination with polyunsaturated fatty acids has been described.26 Systemically, the effects of curcumin include improved lipid metabolism, enhanced insulin sensitivity, and a reduction in abdominal adiposity. Human data investigating 6–10 mg/d of curcumin have confirmed a number of these benefits.22 There are no reports of curcumin-induced toxicity to date. However, no studies have investigated curcumin in critical illness. Sulforaphane Sulforaphane (SF) is a natural isothiocyanate that is present in cruciferous vegetables, such as broccoli and cabbage. Sulforaphane is one of the most potent naturally occurring inducers of cytoprotective enzymes.27 Recent studies illustrated the ability of SF to attenuate LPS- induced increases of IL-1, IL-6, and TNF-α expression in microglia. Furthermore, SF was shown to attenuate LPSinduced activation of NF-κB and activator protein–1 (AP-1).28 Other studies in peritoneal macrophages demonstrated SF potently inhibited LPS-stimulated production of TNFα, IL-1, cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2), and inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS). Nrf2 pathways were shown to be crucial in the inhibition of LPS-induced inflammation.29 Several other naturally occurring pleotropic agents are well described in the literature. Ajoene (derived from garlic), quercetin (widely distributed in the plant kingdom), and liquiritigenin (from licorice) are all inducers of cytoprotective enzymes via the Keap1/Nrf2/ARE system.27,30-32 Green Tea Catechins Green tea or Camellia sinensis is a rich source of flavonols. Catechins are the predominant form of flavanols. The most abundant and studied catechin is EGCG. One cup of green tea is equivalent to approximately 50 mg EGCG or approximately 75 mg/kg EGCG in mice.33,34 Green tea catechins are pleotropic agents with several molecular targets and biologic activities. Depending on the dose and environment, green tea extract (GTE) has been demonstrated to be both anti- and pro-oxidant, and its catechins in particular have several antiobesity effects as well. Many of the catechins downregulate expression of PPAR-γ and the insulin-sensitive glucose transporter GLUT4 in adipocytes.35 GTE has been demonstrated to decrease expression of fatty acid synthase, acetyl coenzyme carboxylase–1, and glycerol-3-phosphatase in adipose tissue.36 The end result of all these effects is decreased adipocyte differentiation and proliferation.36 EGCG has been shown to decrease cholesterol absorption from the gastrointestinal tract by interfering with the micellar solubilization of cholesterol.37 Green tea catechins have been shown to inhibit the sodium/glucose cotransporters in brush-border vesicles as well.38 Green tea catechins appear to increase energy expenditure, increase glucose uptake by skeletal muscle, and decrease glucose uptake in adipose tissue. The mechanisms responsible for these effects include downregulation of lipogenic enzymes, as well as upregulation of enzymes involved in β-oxidation in both adipose tissue and the liver.36,39 EGCG increases anti-inflammatory adipokines such as adiponectin.40 Obesity is also associated with a decreased number and reduced function of regulatory T cells and, as a result, lower anti-inflammatory IL-10 levels. EGCG enhances transcription factors to increase release of IL-10 and downregulate NF-κB.41 Other studies have also shown EGCG supplementation may be beneficial to patients with abnormally excessive T cell function, such as those with inflammatory disorders.42 62S Journal of Parenteral and Enteral Nutrition / Vol. 35, Suppl. 1, September 2011 Systemically, it appears that green tea catechins can acutely improve endothelial function, reduce fat mass, improve insulin sensitivity, increase energy expenditure, and improve lipid profiles.37,43,44 Although both animal studies and early human studies reveal promising results, large randomized controlled studies are needed to further validate the use of EGCG as a treatment for obesity and its related inflammatory complications. There are no available human data on green tea catechins in patients with critical illness. Although the evidence of benefit from green tea catechins is ever growing, toxicities are also becoming more apparent. EGCG is a known hepatotoxic agent at suprapharmacologic doses.45 Depending on both the dose and tissue (eg, pancreatic β cells), EGCG can act as a prooxidant. Most important, more than 30 cases of hepatotoxicity suspected to be secondary to GTE have been described since 1999.46 Given these risks, GTE inclusion into a formula for critically ill obese patients would not be recommended at this time. Polyunsaturated Fatty Acids PUFAs have been an essential part of specialized EN and pharmaconutrition for more than 20 years. A recent meta-analysis in critically ill, burn, and trauma patients showed reduced infections, hospital LOS, and mortality in patients who received a formula containing PUFAs compared to those receiving standard EN.47 Diets rich in PUFAs have been associated with lower risk of cardiovascular disease. High circulating triglyceride levels are effectively reduced using prescription-grade fish oil rich in PUFAs.48,49 Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) may be improved with diets high in PUFAs but low in saturated fatty acids. Hepatic PUFA levels are decreased in obese individuals with NAFLD compared to normal-weight individuals with no apparent liver disease.50 A recent study used rats fed a choline-deficient high-fat diet (CDHF) to induce NAFLD.51 Fish oil added to the CDHF reduced the ratio of hepatic ω6/ω3 PUFA levels, and attenuated hepatic fibrosis compared to control animals not receiving fish oil.51 In a prospective nonrandomized trial, study participants who received 12 months of treatment with PUFA supplementation at 1 g/d showed improved liver enzymes, a decreased ratio of hepatic ω6/ω3 PUFA levels, improved echotexture on ultrasound (suggesting less fibrosis), and better hepatic perfusion on Doppler studies. None of these changes were seen in those patients who refused therapy.52 Mechanisms proposed to explain these observations included a decreased proinflammatory response, improved adipokine profiles, and enhanced organ perfusion following treatment with PUFAs.14,18,53 Response to dietary fats may differ when comparing patients with NAFLD to those with alcoholic fatty liver disease (AFLD). Saturated fatty acids may actually be protective in AFLD.54 One of the first studies to show this protective effect used 3 groups of rats fed a fish oil/ethanol (ETOH) diet.55 After 6 weeks, 1 group of animals was switched to 2 more weeks with fish oil/dextrose, whereas a second group was switched to fish oil/palm oil. A third group of control animals continued to receive fish oil/ ETOH through the last 2 weeks of the study. All rats in the study after 6 weeks of the fish oil/ETOH diet developed fatty infiltration and necrosis. The first group switched to fish oil/dextrose showed no significant histological improvement at 8 weeks, whereas the group switched to fish oil/palm oil exhibited near normalization of hepatic histology.55 This study suggested that diets enriched in saturated fatty acids, not PUFAs, improved AFLD. PUFAs appear to increase lipid peroxidation in animal models of AFLD, an effect not seen with saturated fats.56 The mechanism that may explain this discrepancy may involve the fact that dietary saturated fats increase liver membrane resistance to oxidative stress in AFLD.57,58 Although saturated fatty acids appear to promote apoptosis and liver injury in patients with NAFLD, they appear to have the opposite effect in AFLD.59-62 For patients with NAFLD, PUFAs can reduce TNF-α through reduction of NF-κB and other proinflammatory mediators such as COX-2 and adiponectin.54 PUFAs appear to be beneficial in patients with NAFLD, whereas saturated fats appear to result in deleterious effects. For the obese, critically ill patient, in the absence of ETOH abuse, PUFAs should be included as part of the nutrition therapy. However, if ETOH abuse is a concern, a formula using medium-chain triglycerides as a fat source should probably be used instead. L-Arginine In addition to PUFAs, L-arginine is a current component of many pharmaconutrition enteral formulas. L-arginine is a semi-essential amino acid that modulates vascular tone through nitric oxide synthase (NOS) pathways.63,64 The metabolic by-product of protein processing, asymmetric dimethylarginine (ADMA), is a competitive NOS antagonist. Elevated levels of ADMA are found in numerous conditions that increase cardiovascular risk, including obesity.65,66 Elevated levels of ADMA have been shown to be an independent predictor of cardiovascular morbidity.67,68 Because of higher levels of ADMA in relation to L-arginine, obesity could be considered an arginine-deficient condition.69 Despite the association between abnormal L-arginine/ ADMA ratios and morbidity, few studies have evaluated the effect of L-arginine supplementation in reducing associated risk from cardiovascular disease. Three human studies have demonstrated the effects of supplemental L-arginine on endothelial function.70-72 Pharmaconutrition for the Obese, Critically Ill Patient / Hurt et al 63S In one study, hypertensive patients were randomized to receive 2 g tid of L-arginine, 4 g tid of L-arginine, or placebo for 4 weeks. The group receiving the 4-g dose of L-arginine had significantly lower systolic and diastolic blood pressure compared to the other 2 groups.72 In a second study, 90 participants were randomized to receive either L-arginine or placebo daily for 6 months. At the end of treatment, the large artery elasticity index (LAEI) was shown to be higher and systemic vascular resistance was significantly lower in the L-arginine group compared to baseline and to placebo controls. Furthermore, there was a significant decrease in systolic blood pressure and aldosterone levels.70 A third study evaluated nondiabetic normal-weight post–coronary bypass patients randomized to L-arginine (6.4 g/d) or placebo for 6 months. Compared with placebo, L-arginine decreased ADMA levels significantly.71 L-arginine has been shown to reverse the effects of lipotoxicity in animal models with diabetes mellitus.73,74 In these studies, animals fed L-arginine had reduced total body weight and decreased abdominal adipose tissue. Lower levels of serum glucose, triglycerides, long-chain fatty acids, ADMA, and leptin were also seen in animals placed on L-arginine, compared to controls fed L-alanine. Furthermore, L-arginine increased expression of key genes in white adipose tissue responsible for fatty acid and glucose oxidation.73,74 In the only clinical study evaluating this issue, obese patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus were placed on an intensive exercise training program and a hypocaloric (1000 kcal/d) diet for 21 days.75 At entry into the study, patients were randomized to receive 8.3 g/d of L-arginine vs placebo. Controls placed on placebo showed improvement in metabolic parameters and weight measurements. Study participants placed on L-arginine in addition showed significantly decreased fat mass and waist circumference, with preservation of lean body fat-free mass. Mean daily glucose and fructosamine levels were lowered, adiponectin levels were significantly increased, and leptin-to-adiponectin ratios were reduced in those patients on L-arginine therapy compared to controls on placebo.75 In contrast to the disparate effects of dietary fats seen between NAFLD and AFLD, L-arginine seems to have a more uniform beneficial effect on both disorders. In an animal model of AFLD and steatosis, rats fed L-arginine had improved steatosis, lower endoxin levels, and decreased release of proinflammatory cytokines after 8 weeks compared to control animals on placebo.58 Obese patients with nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) may have some degree of hepatic hypoperfusion at baseline, a defect that may be ameliorated by L-arginine supplementation.9 L-arginine has been shown experimentally to improve hepatic blood flow.76 In an animal model using a 2% cholesterol diet to induce NASH, rats fed L-arginine showed better perfusion of the hepatic microcirculation, greater portal blood flow, and improved hepatic artery flow compared to controls on placebo. This effect was confirmed in a subsequent study of AFLD, where rats were fed an ethanol diet to induce steatosis. Again, L-arginine improved microcirculation and blood flow compared to controls receiving placebo.76,77 One additional potential benefit of L-arginine is the ability to improve skeletal muscle utilization of branchchained amino acids (BCAAs). In a study using an animal model for obesity, rats fed L-arginine for 12 weeks had decreased levels of BCAAs compared to control animals receiving L-alanine. Study animals on L-arginine also showed an improved metabolic profile, suggesting enhanced utilization of BCAA by skeletal muscle.78 L-Citrulline L-arginine treatment is hindered by extensive splanchnic elimination by intestinal arginase, limiting its bioavailability. L-citrulline is a colorless, water-soluble amino acid found naturally in high concentrations in fruit (eg, watermelon). L-citrulline is a metabolic by-product of ornithine metabolism in the intestine and is subsequently converted to L-arginine in the kidney.79 What little hepatic metabolism of citrulline that occurs is limited to periportal hepatocytes.79 Human studies have shown that orally administered L-citrulline raises serum levels of L-arginine in a dose-dependent manner. In fact, ingestion of L-citrulline will achieve significantly higher peak plasma levels of arginine than the same dose of L-arginine 4 hours after administration.80,81 The increased splanchnic elimination of arginine may explain the increased complaints of gastrointestinal side effects observed following a high dose L-arginine and the lack of these side effects seen after ingestion of L-citrulline.79 Few human studies have evaluated the effects of supplemental oral citrulline on endothelial function.82-84 In 1 study, patients with right heart failure and a preserved ejection fraction were randomized to receive L-arginine (8 g/d) or citrulline malate (3 g/d) to observe effects on blood pressure and right ventricular function.82 Both arginine and citrulline significantly decreased pulmonary artery pressure, decreased systolic and diastolic blood pressure, and improved right ventricular ejection fraction at 2 months compared to baseline. There were no significant differences between the 2 groups.82 A subsequent study of similar design evaluated patients with stable right heart failure and endothelial function before and after 2 months of dietary supplementation with L-arginine and citrulline malate. Results showed equally improved endothelial function in both groups by the end of the study.83 A third study evaluated watermelon supplementation 64S Journal of Parenteral and Enteral Nutrition / Vol. 35, Suppl. 1, September 2011 on blood pressure in prehypertensive adults. Watermelon supplementation improved aortic hemodynamics significantly compared to placebo.84 L-citrulline may play a beneficial role in BCAA metabolism. In a study of male cyclists before and after a 137-km race, L-citrulline supplementation at a dose of 6 g taken 2 hours prerace increased L-arginine and L-citrulline levels and decreased BCAA levels following exercise, suggesting enhanced utilization and increased incorporation of the amino acid into skeletal muscle.85 L-Leucine Sarcopenic obesity is a counterintuitive process where despite overly abundant fat stores, obese subjects suffer loss of lean body mass. Optimal weight loss management should succeed in reducing the mass of adipose tissue while preserving lean muscle.86,87 L-leucine is one of the 3 BCAAs, along with isoleucine and valine. L-leucine is metabolized not in the liver but directly in the skeletal muscle. L-leucine can cross the blood-brain barrier.88,89 Of the 3 BCAAs, only L-leucine has activity with the metabolic pathway mammalian target rapamycin (mTOR), which is involved in protein synthesis.89 As a result, L-leucine is involved in the activation of skeletal muscle protein synthesis,90 acting along with insulin on the mTOR pathway in skeletal muscle.91 To optimize muscle protein synthesis, it has been estimated that humans need 25–30 g of protein, containing at least 2.5 g of L-leucine, with each meal.86,92 There is some evidence that L-leucine may affect regulation of food intake via the hypothalamus, involving signaling pathways such as mTOR.89 Neurons in the arcuate nucleus (ARC) of the hypothalamus express mTOR, which may respond to leptin stimulation.93 During exposure to high-fat meals, insulin and leptin receptors are downregulated in the ARC, which may be partially responsible for insulin resistance.94,95 During fasting, there is decreased activity of mTOR in the ARC. Following meals, activity increases.95 This suggests that mTOR may have a role in nutrient sensing and regulation of food intake. L-Leucine is a potent and selective stimulator of mTOR and thus may play a role in stimulating satiety.89 When large protein meals are consumed with significant amounts of L-leucine, the potential benefits to the obese patient include enhanced protein synthesis and greater satiety (reducing further caloric consumption).89 The addition of leucine to a formula designed for the obese, critically ill patient may reduce the loss of lean body mass, thereby providing protection against sarcopenia. It appears to be most effective when given as 2.5–3.0 g po bid or tid. For this reason, a powdered supplement given in addition to the obesity formula would be ideal. Protein There has been a large amount of clinical interest in protein supplementation with obese patients in both the ambulatory and ICU settings. High-protein diets appear to be associated with greater satiety than normal diets.96 These observed effects on satiety are largely due to effects on glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1) and ghrelin, as well as the high leucine content of protein.86,97,98 Limited evidence supports a high-protein hypocaloric enteral feeding strategy in the obese, critically ill patient.99 Two major dietary sources of protein include whey and soy. Soy protein has been shown to decrease lipotoxicity in numerous animal studies.96 In an animal model, rats fed a soy diet had less hepatic lipid deposition than controls fed a casein-based protein diet.100 Animals fed the soy protein had decreased leptin levels, smaller adipocytes, lower body fat content, decreased brown adipose tissue, and overall decreased weight gain compared to animals fed casein.100 One component of soy believed to confer many of the benefits observed in obesity is isoflavones.7 Animals fed soy with high-dose isoflavones had decreased total adiposity and hepatic fat compared to animals fed low-dose soy or casein.99 Despite these benefits, high doses of isoflavones have an estrogenic effect, which may limit their use in certain patient populations (such as patients with breast cancer).7 Soy protein has been shown to be beneficial in a few small clinical studies.101,102 One prospective trial randomized overweight and obese individuals to a soy-based hypocaloric diet vs a similar placebo (non-soy-based) diet for 12 weeks.101 The soy-based group experienced significantly greater weight loss, lower total serum cholesterol levels, and greater reduction in serum low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels compared to the placebo group.100 A study randomized obese and overweight individuals to 1 of 3 treatment groups for 6 months: lifestyle education, soy protein diet, or soy protein diet with a physical activity program.102 Those patients in both soy protein groups had significantly greater weight reduction and decreases in body fat mass than those patients randomized to lifestyle education alone.102 Although limited, there is some evidence that whey protein has some potential benefits over those observed with soy protein.98 A prospective trial evaluated the role of various protein diets on satiety and biomarkers such as ghrelin and GLP-1, randomizing patients to a single casein, whey, or soyenhanced breakfast.96 The whey-based meal had significantly higher levels of L-leucine and other BCAAs than the soy- or casein-based meals. The whey-based meal better increased satiety as measured by a visual analog appetite scale. One of the potential mechanisms to explain the enhanced benefits observed with the whey protein is the increased levels of L-leucine (compared to soy protein), which may enhance the mTOR pathway inducing satiety. Pharmaconutrition for the Obese, Critically Ill Patient / Hurt et al 65S Bowel-Directed Therapy Intestinal barrier function with increased gut permeability is an emerging concept in obesity and the metabolic syndrome.103 Evidence suggests that intestinal-derived toxins such as LPS or endotoxin may enter the systemic circulation and contribute to the development of obesityrelated complications. This occurs, in part, through stimulating the release of proinflammatory cytokines by resident macrophages.103 The term metabolic endotoxemia has been coined to describe this condition, which has clearly been demonstrated to occur in both humans and animal models of obesity.104,105 Marginal deficiencies of certain minerals may be contributing factors, as deficiencies of magnesium and zinc may be associated with increased intestinal permeability.106,107 A key concept underpinning metabolic endotoxemia is the altered composition of the intestinal microflora, which has been observed in both clinical studies of obese humans and in basic studies using animal models of obesity.108-110 Limited data suggest that prebiotics (fiber) and probiotics may attenuate metabolic endotoxemia to improve metabolism.103,108 Medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs) appear to maintain enterocyte structure and may further blunt the effects of endotoxemia by attenuating macrophage activation.111 Therefore, bowel-directed therapies aimed at maintaining intestinal barrier function have theoretical benefit in obesity and the metabolic syndrome, particularly in acutely ill patients. Potential therapies include, but are not limited to, prebiotics, probiotics, zinc, magnesium, and MCTs. Prebiotics and Probiotics Symbiotic bacterial microorganisms (the microbiota) play a critical role in the digestion of intraluminal compounds, homeostasis of the mucosal immune system, and the maintenance of intestinal barrier function. The microbiota even influence metabolism. Once thought to be relatively static and dictated by colonization with maternal microbes at birth, the bowel flora are now considered to be a dynamic “virtual organ” influenced by diet and potentially by disease states, including obesity, the metabolic syndrome, and liver disease. In fact, there are approximately 10-fold more prokaryotic than eukaryotic cells in the human body, with the bowel microflora making the greatest contribution. Probiotics are defined as live microorganisms that, when administered in adequate amounts, confer a health benefit to the host apart from their simple caloric value. Likewise, prebiotics, such as the fermentable soluble fiber inulin, modulate the composition of the bowel flora to confer health benefits. Many newer commercial preparations contain both prebiotics and probiotics and are called synbiotics. Experimental data from the past decade describe a previously unsuspected role of the microflora in obesity. Germ-free mice (born without a microbiota) are leaner than control mice with normal flora despite increased food consumption and a decreased metabolic rate.112 Subsequent microbial colonization of these mice was associated with a 60% increase in body fat, increasing insulin resistance, and NAFLD within 2 weeks.112 Furthermore, bowel flora transplantation with the intestinal flora from obese mice was associated with greater weight gain than microbial transplantation from lean mice. Subsequent data documented an abnormal ratio of the major bacterial phyla (increased firmicutes and decreased bacteroidetes) in stool from both obese humans and rodents. This imbalance resulted in increased digestive efficiency (energy extraction). The ratio normalized with weight loss.109,110 Other groups have focused on the potential protective role of bifidobacteria. A pediatric observational study found that infants with higher fecal bifidobacteria counts were seemingly protected from becoming overweight later at age 7.113 High-fat feeding reduces fecal bifidobacteria in a mouse model of obesity and the metabolic syndrome. Importantly, prebiotic (inulin-type fructans) supplementation selectively reversed this effect, causing increased bifidobacteria counts, which attenuated metabolic endotoxemia, obesity, insulin resistance, and systemic inflammation (proinflammatory cytokines) in this model.108 In addition to attenuating endotoxemia, bifidobacteria also appear to favorably modulate gut-derived hormones involved in energy homeostasis, such as GLP-1.103 Lactobacillus supplementation has been shown to be associated with increased weight loss and greater vitamin B12 levels following bariatric surgery.114 Clinical studies in patients with liver disease demonstrated that alcoholic hepatitis was associated with reduced fecal counts of bifidobacteria and lactobacilli.115 Probiotic supplementation with these micro-organisms was in turn associated with improved liver enzymes. Although more human data are clearly needed, experimental data support the potential role of prebiotics and probiotics in treating obesity and the metabolic syndrome. However, the utilization of probiotics in critically ill patients remains a controversial topic, as some studies have reported adverse outcomes (such as bacteremia and death).116,117 Bacteremia has not been reported to occur with either provision of bifidobacteria or prebiotic supplementation. Limited data suggest that these interventions could improve tolerance to tube feeding. In a crossover study of healthy volunteers, fiber-containing EN was associated with increased bacterial counts of bifidobacteria compared to EN without fiber.118 Low bifidobacteria counts have been demonstrated to occur in some cases of diarrhea in patients receiving tube feedings,119 suggesting a therapeutic role for this probiotic in this setting of intolerance. 66S Journal of Parenteral and Enteral Nutrition / Vol. 35, Suppl. 1, September 2011 The dose of inulin or fructooligosacharides required to increase bifidobacteria has been estimated to be 5–20 g per day.103 Safety concerns regarding the use of soluble fiber in critically ill patients include intestinal gas production derived from fermentation (which could be problematic in patients with ileus). However, compared to other sources of fiber or sugar alcohols (which also increase bifidobacteria), inulin was associated with relatively less gas production.120 Fructooligosacharides are currently included in many EN products and should also be considered for inclusion in a specialized obesity formula for critically ill patients. Magnesium and Zinc Magnesium and zinc play a potential role as pharmaconutrients for obesity and the metabolic syndrome.7 These minerals are important for the maintenance of intestinal barrier function. In an animal model, a low-magnesium diet decreased gut bifidobacteria at 4 days and was associated with loss of intestinal tight junctions and increased release of proinflammatory cytokines.106 These data provide an intriguing link between this mineral, the microbiota, and metabolic endotoxemia. Intestinal oxidative stress caused by ethanol consumption has been shown to displace zinc from zinc-finger proteins, which in turn has been associated with decreased tight junctions, increased intestinal permeability, and endotoxemia.107 ETOHinduced liver injury can be attenuated by zinc supplementation.121 Other causes of intestinal oxidative stress could be anticipated to result in similar effects, but more data are needed in critically ill obese subjects. At the present time, 50 mg per day of elemental zinc should be considered for these obese patients. Nausea may accompany zinc supplementation, and copper deficiency may result from chronic supplementation at higher doses of zinc. The importance of magnesium in critical illness was recently demonstrated in a study showing that patients with diabetes mellitus admitted to the ICU who became hypomagnesemic had a 2-fold increased mortality after adjustment for illness severity.122 Magnesium should be administered at least at the Reference Dietary Intake (RDI) level. Magnesium oxide may be given at a dose of 400 mg daily. However, magnesium requirements may vary considerably in the ICU, in part because of variation in the severity of preexisting deficiency, diarrhea, diuretic use, and renal function. Magnesium supplementation may be associated with diarrhea, but this may be counterbalanced by the antidiarrheal effects of zinc. Medium-Chain Triglycerides MCTs are already incorporated in many EN products. In contrast to long-chain fatty acids (LCFAs), MCTs do not require incorporation into chylomicrons but rather are absorbed intact and enter the portal circulation directly where they may be rapidly oxidized by the liver. Although saturated LCFAs appear to contribute to lipotoxicity, experimental data suggest a possible protective role of MCT for obesity-related illness. Of 2 groups of mice fed isocalorically, the study group fed a higher percentage of calories as MCT gained 20% less weight, compared to controls with a higher percentage of LCFA.123 Rats pretreated with high-dose MCT by gavage (5 g/kg daily for 1 week) vs corn oil were protected against intravenously administered LPS in a sepsis model.111 Although all of the corn oil–treated rats died, MCT prevented death in a dose-dependent fashion. MCT improved the structure of the intestinal epithelium, attenuated production of proinflammatory cytokines, and reduced the incidence of liver injury. These data suggest that pretreatment with MCT may provide a protective effect in sepsis. Unfortunately, pretreatment is not an option for most patients with gram-negative sepsis. An important caveat was uncovered in a subsequent study. In an animal model of NASH, increased hepatoxicity was seen from a combination of MCT + LCFA compared to LCFA alone.124 However, total replacement of LCFA with MCT was hepatoprotective. Furthermore, although the combination of LCFA + MCT may be toxic, the combination of MCT + fish oil should be safer and thus warrants further study. In summary, emerging data suggest a possible role for MCT in the management of obesity, sepsis, and NASH. Conclusions Pharmaconutrition therapy for obesity is a complex subject with limited human data. This is especially true with regard to the critically ill, obese patient. Nonetheless, these physiologic concepts are exciting and offer potential therapeutic strategies in the future. A powdered obesity “cocktail” given in addition to a standard obesity formula may be the most practical method of delivering and studying these agents. Agreement on what to include in such a supplement will continue to be debated. However, developing strategies to address not only obesity itself but also the chronic underlying inflammatory state that is associated with obesity and its comorbidities is paramount to the management of these patients in the ICU.
