full_text: Best Practice & Research Clinical Gastroenterology Vol. 17, No. 5, pp. 833 –848, 2003 doi:10.1016/S1521-6918(03)00073-8, www.elsevier.com/locate/jnlabr/ybega 10 Use of some pre-, pro- and synbiotics in critically ill patients Stig Bengmark* MD, PhD Honorary Visiting Professor Departments of Hepatology and Surgery, London Medical School, University College, Liver Institute, 69-75 Chenies Mews, London WC1E 6HX, UK Maintenance of the gut environment is a key factor in determining outcome in the care of critically ill and postoperative patients. It is especially important to maintain both gastrointestinal secretions, full of anti-infectious and anti-inflammatory compounds, and the gut flora. Prebiotics, usually polysaccharides, exhibit strong bio-activity and the ingestion of prebiotics has been shown to reduce the rate of infection and restore health in sick and postoperative patients. Probiotics may have at least five functions, all of great importance to the sick patients: the reduction or elimination of potentially pathogenic micro-organisms of various kinds; the reduction or elimination of various toxins, mutagens, carcinogens, etc.; modulation of the innate and adaptive immune defence mechanisms; the promotion of apoptosis; and the release of numerous nutrient, antioxidant, growth, coagulation and other factors necessary for recovery. A combination of preand probiotics is referred to as ‘synbiotics’. Our experience of synbiotic treatment in critically ill patients is limited, but cutting-edge results from studies of severe acute pancreatitis, chronic hepatitis and liver transplantation offer great hope for the future. This is especially important as pharmaceutical treatment, including the use of antibiotics, has largely failed, and the medical world is in much need of new treatment paradigms. Key words: lactic acid bacteria; plant fibres; fruits; vegetables; Lactobacillus; pectin; resistant starch; beta-glucans; inulin; prebiotics; probiotics; synbiotics; antibiotics; intensive care; postoperative care; surgical operations; liver transplantation; acute pancreatitis; chronic liver disease; morbidity; infections; sepsis; potentially pathogenic micro-organisms; rejection; noninfectious complications; immune system; cytokines; immunoglobulin. How and what to feed very sick patients has been central to the discipline medicine since its inception. In the early days, there was no alternative: both nourishment and treatment were based on oral supply, and herbal remedies were important tools in the treatment of even the sickest patients. With the invention of suitable tubes, it became possible to infuse nutritional solutions rectally into patients who for one reason or another were unable to eat enough, a treatment modality that was frequently used at the end of the 19th and beginning of the 20th century. Intravenous feeding is a new approach in this area and has only begun to dominate perioperative nutrition within * Tel./Fax: 44-20-7511-6842. E-mail address: s.bengmark@ucl.ac.uk (S. Bengmark). 1521-6918/03/$ - see front matter Q 2003 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 834 S. Bengmark the past decades.1 During the 20th century, enormous efforts were made to develop techniques to feed the sickest patients only via the circulatory system, billions of commercial dollars being spent in such research. During most of the 20th century, there was simply no interest in developing feeding techniques based on the utilization of the normal digestive tract and normal human food. There are several reasons why efforts to develop a perfect parenteral feeding system failed: the importance of the immune system was disregarded, the necessity of maintaining an intact mucosal lining in the gastrointestinal tract was not fully understood, and the importance for health of the nutrients and antioxidants released by microbial enzymes in the lower gastrointestinal tract were not realized. In addition, the importance of maintaining the human gastrointestinal tract’s daily production of about 10 l of gastrointestinal secretions—‘the mother’s milk of the adult’—was overlooked; this provides a vast number and range of various compounds necessary for immune and infection control locally in the gut (see below).2,3 It is only over the past 20 years that the medical and business worlds have begun to focus on the importance of the gastrointestinal tract and its flora for health. Enteral feeding formulas are developed and produced on the concepts used to produce parenteral formulas, for example as a mixture of chemicals. Pure substances such as amino acid sugars are absorbed in the small intestine. If intended for the large intestine, where they are often needed, they must be supplied as a substrate from which they can be released in the large intestine by microbial fermentation. There is usually no place in clinical nutrition for enteral feeding solutions that do not contain ‘colonic food’ for example ‘food for the flora’, such as plant fibres. Furthermore, many of the chemical ingredients in today’s feeding formulas may, like antibiotics and other pharmaceutical agents have detrimental effects on the mucosa, immune system and flora. It must be realized that the sickest patients, even more than healthy individuals, need to be supplied with a great variety of nutrients and antioxidants at the level of the mucosa of the lower gastrointestinal tract. THE HOMEOSTASIS HYPOTHESIS AND GUT CONNECTION It has been suggested that health and well-being are based on homeostasis or an equilibrium between the numerous systems in the body that control the range of bodily functions.4 The modern diet is vastly different from that of our Palaeolithic forefathers: they ate on an annual basis some 500 different plants, whereas we eat fewer than 50; they ate their food raw and often fermented, whereas we conserve, dry and cook our food, processes known to destroy many sensitive nutrients and antioxidants. It seems that our ancestors ate 5 –10 times as much fibre as we do, at least 10-fold more antioxidants and, 50 times more omega-3 fatty acids. Most importantly, our ancestors ate billions of times more bacteria and fungi. The soil was their main food store, and the food was extensively ‘contaminated’ with microbial ingredients. This may be why we are now seeing an increase in various atopic diseases, infections and so called Western diseases.5 The flora (and hypothetically the probiotics supplied) has five important functions of special importance to the very sick: the reduction or elimination of potentially pathogenic micro-organisms † the reduction or elimination of various toxins, mutagens, carcinogens, etc.; † modulation of the innate and adaptive immune defence mechanisms; † the promotion of apoptosis; Pre-, pro- and synbiotics critically ill patients 835 † the release of numerous nutrient, antioxidant, growth, coagulation and other factors that are absorbed and utilized in the body. A deranged gut environment (of the flora or mucosal lining) has, over the past 30 years, increasingly been recognized as a source of allergic and autoimmune disease, as well as of acute and chronic infection. This is particularly true in patients undergoing advanced medical and surgical treatment, and in patients suffering from various acute diseases. Numerous studies have referred to microbial translocation as the cause of increased morbidity in these groups of patients, but this concept alone does not seems fully to explain why some but not other patients develop infectious complications. At least two other factors are equally important: (i) the condition of the immune system, especially before the illness; and (ii) the increased virulence of the potentially pathogenic micro-organisms observed in these groups of patients. It is an old belief that bacteria cannot cause harm as long as they are confined to the lumen of the gastrointestinal or respiratory tract, but this has been strongly disputed by Alverdy et al in a recent review.6 Indeed, it has been observed that systemic injection (intravenously, intraperitoneally or into the mesenteric lymph nodes) of a similar quantity and species of translocated bacteria into healthy or stressed animals resulted in little systemic inflammation, no organ failure but complete recovery.6 It had already been observed some 25 years ago that fewer Pseudomonas aeruginosa organisms were needed to induce lethality when injected intracaecally rather than intravenously.7 Similarly, it was observed that the instillation of the same organism intratracheally resulted in 100% mortality, whereas the same strain in equal doses caused no death when injected intravenously.8 It is also increasingly believed that blood-borne infections have little impact on outcome, the occurrence of organ failure and mortality. Instead, it is suggested that the dramatically increased virulence of potentially pathogenic micro-organisms within the lumen of the intestine or the respiratory tract plays a much more important role in terms of outcome. The dramatic change in physical environment at mucosal surfaces in the critically ill, induced by disease and a lack of nutrients pH, redox state, osmolarity and counterregulatory hormones seem significantly to alter the virulence of potentially pathogenic micro-organisms. Of special interest is the fact that increased luminal release by the host of norepinephrine during catabolic stress is a strong inducer of increased virulence of the luminal bacteria.9 Many have suggested that potentially pathogenic micro-organisms, normally indolent colonizers, change their phenotype under stress and become life-threatening pathogens. Alverdy has proposed that these bacteria adhere to the host cell walls for nutritional purposes, citing as an example Escherichia coli, which induces contactdependent activation of the signal transduction pathways within the mucosal cells, resulting in a disruption of epithelial tight junction permeability, cytokine release, cell apoptosis and activation of the neutrophils. MALNUTRITION AND REDUCED RESISTANCE Two studies, one European10 and one American11, performed over the past 10 years, have suggested that as many as 50% of intensive therapy unit (ITU) patients show signs of malnutrition and impaired immunity. Patients with malnutrition and impaired immunity are the victims of a much higher complication rate after surgery 836 S. Bengmark Table 1. Factors other than malnutrition of importance for increased morbidity and poor outcome in severe surgical and medical conditions. † Reduced patient resistance because of severe disease, multiple illnesses, extremes of age and immunosuppression † Invasive medical devices such as endotracheal tubes for mechanical ventilation, intravascular catheters and urinary tract catheters † Antibiotics and other drugs that reduce resistance to infection † Crowding in the intensive care unit, which increases the risk of cross-infection and trauma, which, as in the case of liver transplantation, leads to a significant increase in the number of days spent on ITU (normal nutrition status 3 ^ 2 versus 44 ^ 36 days in severe malnutrition), days on ventilation (2 ^ 2 versus 41 ^ 37 days), hospital stay (31 ^ 24 versus 82 ^ 40), rate of tracheostomy (0% versus 67%) and also mortality (0% versus 28%).12 In addition to malnutrition, other factors (Table 1) significantly contribute to the high rate of infection observed in ITU. These infections are most common at four locations: the respiratory tract (approximately 30 –65%), urinary tract (in the region of 25%), bloodstream (approximately 15%) and surgical sites (some 8%). They are often seen following trauma and after surgical and advanced medical treatments such as liver and stem cell transplantation (50 – 85% of patients), extensive liver and pancreas operations (approximately 33%), extensive oesophageal, gastric and colorectal operations (around 20%) and coronary bypasses (approximately 10%). ANTIBIOTICS AS A TREATMENT TOOL Numerous antibiotics and combinations of antibiotics have been tried for preventing and treating infections in severe conditions such as acute pancreatitis, burns, trauma, and advanced surgical and medical treatments such as liver and stem cell transplantation, as well as in general ITU populations—most often with little or no influence on outcome, morbidity and mortality. Selective digestive tract decontamination, a treatment that aims to prevent infection by prophylactically eradicating the potential pathogens from oropharynx, stomach and bowel while preserving the normal anaerobic flora) has been extensively tried since its introduction about 20 years ago.13 It usually consists of the administration for at least 3 –4 weeks of a combination of three or more non-absorbable antibiotics, such as colistin, nystatin, gentamycin, tobramycin, amphotericin B or polymyxin, plus topical treatment of the oral cavity with a paste containing the same antibiotics, the treatment being repeated four times a day. More than 30 controlled studies have been performed and have been summarised in two recent meta-analyses.14,15 Both meta-analyses confirmed a small but significant reduction in risk of pneumonia and a small survival benefit in certain subpopulations of critically ill patients. However, two recent controlled studies, one American16 and one European17, found no benefit of selective digestive tract decontamination in patients with liver transplantation, reporting a 30-day infection rate for the treatment group versus controls of 32.4% versus 27.9% for the American study and 86% versus 84.5% for the European. Pre-, pro- and synbiotics critically ill patients 837 EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION AS A TREATMENT TOOL It is clear that an enteral supply of nutrients and antioxidants a prerequisite for controlling infections and other complications in ITU patients. As the aim of such treatment is to modulate the stress response, and as the therapeutic window is narrow, it is essential that this treatment is started as early as possible.2,18 Almost a century ago, Andresen19, based on his own clinical experience, recommended that feeding should be instituted at the operation table and 200– 250 ml nutritional solution supplied, to be continued day and night thereafter. He reported that such nutrition was safe, counteracted postoperative intestinal paralysis and contributed to patients’ recovery. Early enteral nutrition (within 12 hours) helps to reduce postoperative and posttraumatic infection, maintains tissue antioxidant level, improves protein synthesis and wound healing, and preserves intestinal mucosal integrity (see Ref. [20] for a recent meta-analysis). Of special importance is Kompan et al’s finding that enteral nutrition for trauma patients that starts within 6 hours of admission to the ITU, compared with after 24 hours, is associated not only with normal intestinal permeability, but also a significant reduction in the incidence of organ failure.21 There was a time when the supply of a large number of calories (hyperalimentation) to critically ill patients was fashionable, but this is no longer true. Instead, we have become increasingly aware that overfeeding macronutrients to critically ill individuals—as to healthy ones—is highly dangerous. Excessive nutrition, provided parenterally or enterally, seems often to lead to serious and sometimes fatal, metabolic consequences. Hyperalimentation is today rarely indicated in perioperative nutrition, at least not during the first 2 weeks after surgery. Even the goal of maintaining calorie and nitrogen balance in the average surgical patients has lost its importance. Instead, the role of enteral nutrition as a tool to modulate the immune system is increasingly emphasized. As many as 80% of the total immunoglobulin-producing cells of the body are localized in lamina propria of the gut22, and large quantities of especially IgA are released daily to the gut lumen. The synthesis of IgA is highly dependent on T-cells, several cytokines produced by activated lymphocytes influencing different steps in the IgA differentiation pathway.23 Changes in nutrition, physical activity, sleep, mood, age, gender, circadian rhythm, body temperature, the consumption of drugs and illness are known to influence lymphocyte function, the production of immunoglobulins and resistance to disease. Care in ITU settings is most often associated with dramatic changes in not only the level of nutrition and physical activity, but also all the other bodily functions; this, in combination with an increased consumption of drugs, leads to a much reduced immune response on the part of the patient. It is only recently that immunological control has been realised to be much more important than nitrogen balance and calorie supply. Two recent studies have clearly demonstrated much more pronounced effects on immunological than nutritional parameters. Shirabe et al24 compared parenteral hyperalimentation with enteral nutrition in liver resection patients and found no difference in the levels of nutritional parameters such as retinal-binding protein, transferrin, pre-albumin and 3-methylhistidine.24 In sharp contrast, significant differences between enteral and parenteral nutrition were observed in immunological parameters, including lymphocyte number, response to phytohaemagglutinin and natural killer cell activity. Most importantly, the incidence of infectious complications was 8% in the enteral nutrition group compared with 31% in the parenteral hyperalimentation group. Windsor et al performed a similar study in acute pancreatitis and reached similar results.25 Here, disease severity scores (APACHE II; 6 versus 8; P , 0.0001), C-reactive protein (84 versus 156 mg/l; 838 S. Bengmark P , 0.005), IgM anticore endotoxin antibody and total antioxidant index were significantly better in the enteral nutrition group. In addition, the systemic inflammatory response, sepsis rate, incidence of organ failure and length of stay in ITU were all significantly better in the enterally fed group. Clearly, the practice of early before (, 6 hours) enteral nutrition for acutely ill patients has benefited management. As summarised in the recent review by Marik and Zaloga20, it generally seems to have the number of infections, albeit not eliminate them. PREBIOTICS Early enteral feeding is, however, not enough: the nutrition should also contain fibre (e.g. prebiotics). Prebiotic usage is a way of maintaining mucosal growth, mucosal function and water and electrolyte balance, providing the host with energy and nutrients and increasing resistance against invading pathogens. The human digestive tract is from birth already highly dependent on a supply of prebiotics for its growth and optimal function. Breast milk is extremely rich in non-digestible oligosaccharides: apart from elephant milk, no other mammalian milk contains as much non-digestible oligosaccharide as breast milk.26 The complex fucosylated oligosaccharides in human milk, with structural similarities to immunomodulatory cell surface glycoconjugates, are supposed to protect breast-fed infants against inflammation and infection.26 These nondigestible oligosaccharides are likely also to function as prebiotics and stimulate the growth of the non-pathogenic gut microflora in breast-fed infants. The administration of glucans significantly reduces mortality rate from hospital infection in trauma patients.27 Fresh fruits and vegetables, often cannot for various reasons, be given to the sickest patients, but there is a whole series of fibres that can be used. One of these is pectin, known for its bio-activity. It is a mucosal protectant, an antioxidant, a vehicle for the transport of lactic acid bacilli (LAB) through the gastrointestinal tract and a superior substrate for bacterial fermentation.3 The unripe banana (green sweet banana as well as plantain) is rich in both pectin and resistant starch. Green banana (250 g/l, which is equivalent to two fruits) or 2 g pectin per kilogram of food was recently tried as a supplement to a rice diet in children in Bangladesh suffering from persistent diarrhoea.28 The amount and frequency of stools, the duration of the diarrhoea, the number of episodes of vomiting and the use of oral rehydration, or the amount of intravenous fluid given, were all significantly reduced in the two treatment groups.28 Recovery on the third day was seen in 59% in the green banana group and 55% of the pectin group compared with only 15% of the rice-only group. It is this author’s strong conviction that pectin (up to 10 g/day) should be routinely used in all ITUs to protect against peptic ulcers, to prevent oxidation and to stimulate and feed the microbial flora; other plant fibres could also be used. SYNBIOTICS From a genetic point of view, there is wide variation among the LAB bacteria. Indeed, the genetic difference between one LAB and another can be greater than that between a fish and a human being. Most of the LABs consumed have a low ability to ferment strong fibre such as inulin or phlein, have poor antioxidant systems, do not Pre-, pro- and synbiotics critically ill patients 839 adhere to human mucus and, most importantly do not or only poorly survive the acidity of the stomach and bile acid content of the small intestine. Strong bio-activity cannot in my opinion, be expected from LABs such as yoghurt bacteria, known for their ability to grow in fibre-free environments and chosen mainly for their palatability. Instead, LABs living and growing on plants, often under harsh conditions, can be expected to exhibit much stronger health-promoting abilities. These LABs must, for their nourishment and survival, have the ability to ferment rather resistant fibre. This is probably why a much greater clinical success in severely sick patients can be expected from LABs collected from growing plants, silage, sourdough, sauerkraut/and other ‘ethnic type’ foods. By far the greatest interest has focused on LABs from grains such as oat and rye. In future, attempts will certainly be made systematically to study the various bio-activities of all plants known for their health-promoting potential. When the microbiology of growing rye was recently investigated, more than 180 LAB species were found, some of which demonstrated unique biological properties (see below). There are about 195 000 plants on earth, but modern man has chosen to obtain about 90% of his food from only 17, and over 50% of his calorie and protein need from eight, cereal grains. The reduced variation typical of modern Western foods, as well as methods for conserving and preparing foods, creates problems as it significantly reduces the variation in availability of nutrients and antioxidants to the human body. The nutrition provided to the very sick is even further restricted. A minimum requirement is, in my view, that every bottle of nutrition solution should contain fibre. SINGLE LACTIC ACID BACTERIUM/SINGLE FIBRE AS TREATMENT TOOL Lactobacillus plantarum is commonly found in rural Asians and Africans consuming large quantities of fresh plant-based foods, which are rich in L. plantarum.29 There are strong indications that the Western lifestyle and diet militates against colonization with L. plantarum. This LAB, always present in the stools of those who have a rural lifestyle, was identified in two-thirds of North American Seventh Day Adventists, who are mainly vegetarians, only but in 1 in 4 North Americans eating a Western diet.30 A recent study has shown that the three dominant clusters isolated from mucosal biopsies of healthy Swedish volunteers are L. plantarum (24% of individuals), L. rhamnosus (12%) and L. casei, subsp. pseudoplantarum (10%).31 Lactobacillus plantarum was identified in onethird of healthy Swedish infants at 3 –8 weeks of age.32 A detailed analysis of the health-promoting potentials of different probiotic bacteria in combination with different prebiotic fibres led a Lund University-based group of scientists to develop an enteral nutrition formula obtained by fermenting oatmeal with L. plantarum strain 299, chosen after studying several hundred LAB. To identify the most suitable LAB for the purpose, fermented oat containing a mixture of 19 selected human-specific Lactobacillus strains (with a density 5 £ 108 colony-forming units, of each strain) was consumed by healthy volunteers, mucosal biopsies being taken from jejunum and rectum 1 day before, and 1 and 11 days after the end of administration. After a supply-free interval of 11 days, only 5 out of 19 strains, most frequently the 299 strain, could be identified in the stools.33 Strain 299 has been shown to have the capacity to ferment oat and to survive the acidity of the stomach and the bile acid content of the small intestine.33 Experience from experimental and some early clinical observations is summarised elsewhere.34 The L. plantarum 299/oat-based formula was recently tried in 840 S. Bengmark three groups of critically ill patients: patients with severe pancreatitis, those who had had recent abdominal surgery and those who had undergone with liver transplantation. Severe acute pancreatitis Pancreatic necrosis and infection are strong determinants of organ failure and poor outcome in severe acute pancreatitis.35 The mortality rate after pancreatitis is 5 –10 times higher if the area of necrosis becomes infected36,37, and infected pancreatic necrosis occurs in approximately 25% of cases after 1 week and approximately 75% after 3 weeks.2 Furthermore, all regimens including antibiotics38 – 40 and various proinflammatory cytokine inhibitors38 have failed to make any significant impact on outcome. In recent study in Györ, Hungary, patients with severe acute pancreatitis were randomized on arrival in hospital to receive 1 week of treatment with a twice-daily application, via a nasojejunal tube, of either a freeze-dried preparation containing 109 live L. plantarum 299 together with a substrate of 10 g oat fibre, or a similar preparation, in which the Lactobacillus had been heat-inactivated.41 The study was designed to be interrupted when the infection rate on repeat statistical analysis would showed a significant difference between the two group, which occurred when 45 patients had completed the study. At that time, 22 patients had received treatment with live and 23 with heat-killed L. plantarum 299. Infected pancreatic necrosis and abscesses occurred in 1 out of 22 (4.5%) of the treatment group compared with 7 out of 23 (30%) of the heat-killed group (P ¼ 0.023). The only patient in the lactobacillus group who developed an infection had signs of urinary infection on the 15th day after admission, that is, at a time when he had not received treatment for 8 days. It is likely that the supply to critically ill patients will in future be continued for longer than 1 week. Furthermore, the length of stay was much shorter in the live LAB group (13.7 days versus 21.4 days), but did not, because of the small size of the patient group; reach full statistical significance. Abdominal surgery patients The same freeze-dried preparation containing live L. plantarum 299 together with oat fibre was recently used in a controlled study of extensive abdominal surgical operations and compared both with the same preparation containing heat-inactivated Lactobacillus and with standard enteral nutrition.42 The patients underwent either liver resection (n ¼ 29), pancreatic resection (n ¼ 26), gastric resection (n ¼ 22), colonic resection (n ¼ 9) or intestinal bypass (n ¼ 4); they were equally distributed between the three treatment groups. Each group consisted of 30 patients. The 30-day sepsis rate was 10% (3 out of 30 patients) in the two groups receiving either live or heat-inactivated LAB, and 30% (9 out of 30 patients) in the group receiving standard enteral nutrition (P ¼ 0.01), the biggest difference being observed in the number of cases of pneumonia (enteral nutrition, 6 patients; live LAB and fibre, 2 patients; heat-killed LAB and fibre, 1 patient). The effect seemed to be most pronounced after gastric and pancreatic resection, for which the sepsis rate with enteral nutrition only was 8 out of 16 patients (50%) compared with 3 out of 17 (17%) in the heat-inactivated group and 1 in 15 (7%) in the live LAB group. The patients in the group treated with live LAB received significantly fewer antibiotics (P ¼ 0.04): the mean length of antibiotic treatment with live LABs was 4 ^ 3.7 days, with heat-killed LAB 7 ^ 5.2 days and with enteral nutrition 8 ^ 6.5 days. Non-infectious complications occurred in 3% of enteral only nutrition 17% of Pre-, pro- and synbiotics critically ill patients 841 heat-inactivated LAB and 13% of live LAB patients. No significant changes were observed in the levels of haemoglobin, leukocytes, C-reactive protein, blood urea nitrogen, bilirubin, albumin, total lymphocyte count, CD45 RA, CD45 RO, CD4, CD8 or NK cells, or in the CD4:CD8 ratio. There was no difference in the mean length of hospital stay. Another recently published study compared the effect of a probiotic fruit drink (PRO VIVA) containing the related but not identical Lactobacillus plantarum, 299V, which originates from sourdough, in contrast to 299, which was originally recovered from human intestine.43 The content of LAB and oat fibre is much less in this drink than that used in the studies above involving 299: in the fruit drink, as little as 5% of LABfermented oat is mixed with 95% of fruit juice, leading to a final content of L. plantarum 299V of just 5 £ 107. The drink was, however, supplied over longer periods than in the other studies, including a minimum of 1 week before the operation. Sixty-four patients were supplied with PRO VIVA, 65 patients receiving no additional treatment. Almost all patients, in both groups received a single dose of intravenous cefuroxamine and metronidazole on induction. No significant differences were observed between the groups in terms of bacterial translocation (12% versus 12%; P ¼ 0.82), gastric colonization with enteric organisms (11% versus 17%, P ¼ 0.42) or septic morbidity (13% versus 15%; P ¼ 074). These two trials deserve some comment. The first involved patients undergoing much larger operations than those in the second study. The risk of developing septic complications is known to be much higher in such patients as those involved in the first study. This is further underlined by the fact that the control patients (patients not supplied with LABs) in the first study developed septic complications in 30% (of cases pancreas and stomach operation 50%) compared with 15% in the second study, who had mainly colorectal operations. This seems to indicate that the most pronounced effects can be expected in the patients with the highest risk of septic morbidity. The low dose of pro- and prebiotics supplied in the second study could also explain the difference in outcome. The supply of LABs in a density of 107 or lower is generally regarded as being too small for significant probiotic effects to be expected. In the first study, a concentrated powder of LAB and fibre was supplied, whereas the second used a formula diluted approximately 20-fold. Furthermore, different strains of L. plantarum were used in the two studies. Liver transplantation Rayes and her group in Berlin performed a prospective randomized study44 involving 95 liver transplant patients and compared the incidence of infections and other complications among three different groups, all supplied with early enteral nutrition: 1. selective digestive tract decontamination (SDD) four times daily for 6 weeks (n ¼ 32); 2. Lactobacillus plantarum 299 (LLP) in a dose of 109 plus 15 g fermentable fibre (n ¼ 31) for 12 days postoperatively; 3. identical treatment to group 2, but with heat-killed L. plantarum 299 (HLP) (n ¼ 32). All the patients received enteral nutrition from the second postoperative day onwards, group 1 without, and groups 2 and 3 with, nutritional fibre. There were no deaths. There were 23 SDD, 4 LLP and 17 HLP, postoperative infections. Fifteen out of 32 patients (48%) in the SDD group, 11 out of 32 (34%) in the HLP group and 4 842 S. Bengmark out of 31 (13%) in the LLP group showed signs of infection (P ¼ 0.017). The most prevalent infections were cholangitis, which occurred in 10, 8 and 2 patients respectively, and pneumonia, seen in 6, 4 and 1 patient. The micro-organisms most often isolated were Enterococci, seen in 8, 8 and 1 patients, and Staphylococci, identified in 6, 3 and 1 patients. No Escherichia coli or Klebsiella infections occurred in the LLP group. Non-infectious complications occurred in 15 SDD, 19 HLP and 16 LLP patients, and early rejection in 10, 15 and 10 patients, respectively. The number of patients requiring haemodialysis was 8 SDD, 4 HLP and 2 LLP, and the number of reoperations 6, 2 and 4, respectively. The CD4:CD8 ratio was higher in the LLP group than the other two groups (P ¼ 0.06), and the ITU stay, hospital stay and length on antibiotic therapy were shorter in the LLP group; none of these results, however, reached statistical significance. MULTI-LACTIC ACID BACTERIUM/MULTI-FIBRE COMPOSITION AS A TREATMENT TOOL Some evidence suggests that more powerful synbiotic effects can be obtained by supplementing several LABs and providing several different fibres as a substrate for fermentation. Exceptionally bio-active LABs such as L. plantarum are collected from ethnic-type foods such as sauerkraut and sourdough or from silage. One might expect that LABs growing on plants are selected by nature because of their ability to ferment fibres from that particular plant for its own nourishment. A microbiology team based University of Lund extensively screened growing, ecologically cultivated rye for new and interesting LABs to be used for clinical purposes and found no fewer than 180 different strains.45 In addition, they screened the human gut, from which they isolated 355 various strains.46 The ability of these over 500 strains to bind to porcine mucin, express cell surface hydrophobicity and bind collagen, fibronectin and other extracellular matrix proteins was studied. Based on this, eight strains were selected for further studies (three L. plantarum strains, two L. paracasei subsp. paracasei, Leuconostoc mesenteroides and Pediococcus pentosaceus). These eight strains were all able to survive exposure to 20% bile for 1 hour and pH 2.5 for 2 hours, and could utilize inulin or amylopectin as a sole carbon source during in vitro culture. Three of the strains produced b-galactosidase, which is proposed to alleviate the symptoms of lactose intolerance. Furthermore, these strains produced antimicrobial substance(s) with activity against the homologous strain, Gram-positive bacteria, two Lactobacillus strains also showing some activity against the gastric pathogen Helicobacter pylori. The exposure of Lactobacillus strains to pH 5 for 1 hour induced the de novo production of several proteins, five of which crossreacted with stress proteins. This is important as such proteins are suggested to protect other surface proteins and adhesins during transport through the gastrointestinal tract. Four out of the eight studied LAB strains transcribed nuclear factor-kB to the nucleus of macrophage U 937. This induced the induction of proinflammatory interleukin [IL] 1b and IL-8) and anti-inflammatory (IL-10) cytokines by L. plantarum and, to a much lesser extent, by Leuconsotoc mesenteroides. Lactobacillus plantarum and Pediococcus pentosaceus produced antioxidants, equivalent to 100 mg vitamin C.45 The production of antioxidants by colonic bacteria can be expected to provide a beneficial effect by scavenging free radicals in the gastrointestinal tract, a function which can be important as the availability of antioxidants in the gut decreases rostrally. Pre-, pro- and synbiotics critically ill patients 843 From the eight strains extensively studied, four were chosen to be used in a synbiotic composition. This mixture, given the name Synbiotic 2000, consists of 1010 of each the following four lactic acid bacteria (probiotics) Pediococcus pentoseceus 533:3, Leuconostoc mesenteroides 32-77:1, L. paracasei subsp. paracasei 19 and L. plantarum 2362 and 2.5 g of each of four fermantable fibres (prebiotics): betaglucan, inulin, pectin and resistant starch. All the LABs except one (L. paracasei) were of rye origin. Synbiotic 2000 in progressive liver disease The activation of macrophages by gut-derived endotoxin has been assumed to be responsible for the raised levels of tumour necrosis factor-a (TNF-a), which is probably a key factor behind the progressive liver damage usually seen in patients with liver cirrhosis. The expression of Toll-like receptors 4 and 2 on peripheral blood monocytes is known to be critically involved in TNF-a production in response to endotoxin and Gram-2 positive microbial stimulation. If the supply of synbiotics is capable of downregulating the expression of Toll-like receptors and reducing the production of TNF-a they might constitute a cheap tool with no side-effects for the long-term treatment of patients with liver disease. The recent finding that in vitro TNF-a production by peripheral blood mononuclear cells in response to stimulation by endotoxin or Staphylococcus aureus enterotoxin B is reduced by a median of 46% (range 8 –67%) in comparison to the pre-supplementation level in 8 out of 11 cirrhotic patients supplied with Synbiotic 2000 is thus of considerable interest.47 The administration of Synbiotic 2000 to patients with chronic liver disease was well tolerated in this study, and no adverse events or general changes in clinical state was observed. The effect of Synbiotic 2000 was further investigated in a double-blind controlled study involving 55 patients with chronic liver disease. The patients were divided into three groups, receiving either Synbiotic 2000 (n ¼ 20), only the fibre from Synbiotic 2000 (n ¼ 20), or a placebo (of non-fermentable fibre) (n ¼ 15) (Qing-Liu, personal communication, 2003). A 1-month supply gave rise to a significant increase in the LAB flora in the LAB-supplied group but not the other two groups. A faecal pH was significantly reduced between 6.5 and 7 in the placebo group to 5.0 –5.5 in the two treatment groups. Significant decreases in E. coli, Staphylococcus and Fusobacterium (P , 0.001, P , 0.01 and P , 0.05, respectively), but not Pseudomonas and Enterococcus, were observed in both the treatment groups but not the placebo group. A significant decrease in serum ammonia level was observed in the Synbiotic 2000-treated group (from 60.5 ^ 2.9 to 38.6 ^ 3.9 mmol/l) and the fibre-only group (from 63.6 ^ 3.9 to 41.5 ^ 5.2), but not in the placebo-treated group (from 60.5 ^ 2.9 to 58.6 ^ 3.9). The level of endotoxin fell in the two treatment groups but not in the placebo group. The levels of alanine transferase decreased from 252 ^ 182 to 84 ^ 65 (P , 0.01) U/l) in the group supplied with Synbiotic 2000 and to 110 ^ 86 (P , 0.05) U/l in the fibre-only group, but no fall was seen in the placebo group. Furthermore, improvements in psychometric test results and in the degree of encephalopathy were observed in both treatment groups. Synbiotic 2000 in liver transplantation A study of liver transplant patients was recently concluded at Berlin University, Germany.48 Thirty-three patients in a randomized, double-blind study were supplied 844 S. Bengmark with Synbiotic 2000 and then compared with 33 patients supplied with only the four types of fibre used in the Synbiotic 2000. Treatment started the day before surgery and was continued until the 14th day after surgery. Only one patient in the Synbiotic 2000-treated group showed any signs of infection, in contrast to 17 out of 33 (51%) in the fibre-only group. Synbiotic 2000 in other serious conditions Ten patients suffering from severe chronic distal colitis were administered twice-daily enemas of Synbiotic 2000 over a period of 2 weeks, 9 of the 10 patients concluding the study. Significant reductions in diarrhoea scores (before: 2.5 ^ 0.38) were observed on day 7 (1.13 ^ 0.13; P , 0.05) and day 14 (1.13 ^ 0.23; P , 0.05) as well as 1 week after conclusion of the treatment, on day 21 (0.75 ^ 0.25; P , 0.01).49 The frequency of visible blood in the stools was also significantly reduced, from 2 ^ 0.27 at the start of the study to 1 ^ 0.38 (P , 0.05) and 1.12 ^ 0.35 (P , 0.05) on days 14 and 21, respectively. In addition, the frequency of nocturnal diarrhoea and urgency were significantly reduced, and stool consistency improved on days 7, 14 and 21. Two patients reported excessive bloating and wind, but no other significant adverse events or side-effects were observed. Future aspects Not only the gastrointestinal tract, but all body surfaces are normally coated by a protective flora, essential for prevention of infection and inflammation. Second to the GI tract with its 1– 2 kg of flora is the skin, in the adult human calculated to be inhabited by approximately 200 g of bacteria. Other important sites are the mouth and pharynx, the respiratory tract and the vagina, each supposed to be inhabited by approximately 20 g of flora. Too much washing and cleaning will impair this defence layer and open the door for opportunistic infections. Animals have the instinct to lick their wounds and herby provide both protective flora and growth factors, secreted by the salivary glands. To apply topically lactic acid bacteria on the skin and around all penetrations of the skin by foreign materials such as tubes, drains, tracheostomies etc. is receiving increasing interest. Such a treatment could also be of potential interest for treatment of burns. It has been observed that infants treated with probiotics suffer much less diaper dermatitis.51 Two recent reports suggest that consumption of LAB-containing drinks prevents formation of biofilm and removes both yeast and bacteria from silicon rubber voice prostheses.52,53 The flora is invariably reduced at all these sites in sick and hospitalized patients due to special hygienic requirements and large supply of antibiotics and other drugs. An overflow of probiotic bacteria from the GI tract to all the other sites seems to occur normally, a function, which most likely is severely reduced in the sick. It is not unlikely that in the future a dietary supply of pre- and probiotics be complemented by spraying or applying gels of LAB on sensitive body surfaces, especially around the skin penetrations, but also by using LAB-containing aerosols to promote flora of the respiratory tract, where such a protection layer is much needed. There are fundamental differences between various lactic acid bacteria. The expected clinical effects from use of various LAB are most varying and conclusions can never be made from one strain to another. Generally milk-fermenting LAB are bioactively less powerful than fibre-fermented LAB such as Lb plantarum, Lb paracasei, and Lb casei. The majority of Pre-, pro- and synbiotics critically ill patients 845 the clinical studies have this far been performed with Lb rhamnosus GG, but often with rather modest clinical effects—see further. The clinical efficiency of LAB such as Lb Casei Shirota and Lb plantarum appears this far to be the most pronounced, but stronger clinical efficacy can most likely be achieved through use of mixtures of several probiotic LABs in combination with several bioactive prebiotic fibres. SUMMARY Most studies have so far involved patients with liver and pancreas disease such as severe acute pancreatitis or chronic liver disease, or in connection with extensive upper abdominal operations and liver transplantation. Bacterial and fungal infections occur in liver transplantation during the first postoperative month in up to 85% of cases despite extensive antibiotic treatment and selective digestive tract decontamination. Two key controlled studies from Berlin University have reported a reduction in these complications using synbiotics (the postoperative supply of one Lactobacillus organism and one type of fibre giving a reduction to 13%, and four lactobacilli and four types of fibre a reduction to 3%). Similarly, a reduction from 30 to 4.5% was observed in a study in patients with severe acute pancreatitis. The only patient in the LAB- and fibre-treated group, who developed an infections did so 8 days after the conclusion of treatment. Studies on cirrhotic patients have shown a decrease in the degree of encephalopathy and improved liver function; positive effects on the composition of the flora, including a significant decrease in the amount of endotoxin and serum ammonias, as well as in E. coli, Staphylococcus and Fusobacterium but not Pseudomonas and Enterococcus, have been observed both with a combined supply of pre- and probiotics, and with prebiotics alone. It appears from transplantation studies that infection with Pseudomonas and Enterococcus can also largely be prevented. Although the mechanisms underlying the clinical effects are not fully understood, much of the evidence supports the hypothesis that the effects are induced via several important functions of both pro- and prebiotics. Practice points † priority should be given to preserving salivation and gastrointestinal secretion in the critically ill, as these are important functions of the innate immune system † consequently, H2-blockers, proton inhibitors and antisecretory drugs should if possible be avoided † attempts should always be made to provide a maximum amount of prebiotic fibre † one should aim to provide several prebiotic fibres in order to optimize the availability of substrate for release through the fermentation of important nutrients, antioxidants, coagulation factors, etc. at the lower gastrointestinal tract mucosa † therapeutic probiotic bacteria have almost no side-effects and can be safely used in immunocompromised patients † critically ill patients, especially those receiving antibiotics, undoubtedly suffer from reduced commensal flora and could benefit from a daily supply of probiotic bacteria 846 S. Bengmark † attempts should be made to use only pre- and probiotics with a documented strong bioactivity † attempts should be made to start the supply of probitics, immediately after if not before operation, as well as immediately with acute medical illness or trauma † as many sensitive nutrients, for example glutamine and glutathione, are destroyed by preservation, especially by heating and drying procedures, the additional supply of fresh fruit and vegetables is recommended Research agenda † information on the content of various prebiotic fibres is scarce, a documentation of bioactivity being available for only a handful of fibres. Attempts must be made to explore the bioactivities of the large number of fibres available in nature † efforts should be made to identify prebiotics, that can serve as a substrate for the production of specific nutrients such as arginine, glutamine and polyphenols † attempts should be made to find new and strong bioactive fibres from plants not yet fully explored † the bioactivity of probiotic bacteria has been investigated in only a few LABs, a large number still needing to be studied † as LABs growing on plants have the greatest ability to ferment fibres, the search for bioactive probiotics should be focused on plants known to contain special health-promoting substances † the synbiotic effects of a mixture of probiotic bacteria, also in combination with various fibres, should be researched † studies are greatly needed on the large group of critically ill patients not yet investigated, such as those receiving stem cell transplantation or those with burns † synbiotics have great potential for use instead of antibiotics and lavage in bowel preparation, which should be explored † synbiotics have the potential to reduce pro-coagulatory factors, such as fibrinogen and plasminogen activator-1, and increase fibrinolysis. Their ability to prevent adhesion and thrombosis should thus be investigated † as a larger supply of pre- and probiotics seems to enforce the innate immune system, and as such a supply can be provided for an unlimited time without side-effects, the administration of synbiotics should be tried systematically in various patients with endemic chronic diseases, such as those suffering from arteriosclerosis, cancer, diabetes, chronic lung, liver and intestinal diseases, HIV, cystic fibrosis and other dehabilitating diseases and those on renal dialysis REFERENCES 1.
